personal manual · cybersecurity
Cherry Root
— A Breach Diary —
A personal reference manual for understanding attacks, defenses, and how the internet really works.
NameKaylla Dearo
StartedApril 23
GoalCybersecurity Expert
12 blocks · 146 terms · attack & defense
First Quiz Block Mastered 50 Studied 10 Favorites
cherry root › guided exercises
Guided Exercises
Hands-on, step-by-step exercises with hints and worked solutions. Try each step yourself first — reveal the hint or solution only if you get stuck.
Block 0 · PC Basics
Read what a computer is really saying — binary, file permissions, and paths.
1 · Decode binary2 · Read permissions3 · Build a path
Block 1 · The Internet
Take a URL apart, decode HTTP status codes, and spot what's insecure on the web.
1 · Read a URL2 · Status codes3 · Spot the insecure
Block 2 · Networks
Know your ports, tell TCP from UDP, and recognize the devices and what really protects a network.
1 · Ports & protocols2 · TCP, UDP & OSI3 · Devices & defense
Block 3 · Linux
Live in the terminal — core commands, permissions and sudo, pipes, processes and logs.
1 · Basic commands2 · Permissions & sudo3 · Pipes, processes, logs
Block 4 · Programming Basics
Read Python output, store and compare values, and pull data from lists and dicts — the scripting skills a hacker needs.
1 · Predict the output2 · Variables & logic3 · Lists, dicts & files
Block 5 · Security Fundamentals
The core ideas: CIA triad, threats vs vulnerabilities vs risk, encryption vs hashing, and authn vs authz.
1 · CIA & core concepts2 · Encryption vs hashing3 · AuthN, AuthZ & defense
Block 6 · Web Attacks
Name the attack from a clue, pick the right fix, and reason about why each one works.
1 · Identify the attack2 · The right fix3 · Spot & reason
Block 7 · Pentest Tools
Match the job to the tool — nmap, Burp, Wireshark, Hashcat, Hydra, curl and friends.
1 · Match the tool2 · nmap & ports3 · Cracking & web tools
Block 8 · Pentest Methodology
The kill chain in order — recon to report — and the reasoning behind each phase.
1 · Order the phases2 · Name the phase3 · Reason it through
Block 9 · Social Engineering
Name the human attack from a clue, dodge the common traps, and recognise the techniques.
1 · Spot the attack2 · Don't get fooled3 · Recognise the technique
Block 10 · Malware
Tell the malware families apart, bust the common myths, and name the rest.
1 · Identify the malware2 · Bust the myth3 · Name the rest
Block 11 · Defense
Match each blue-team control to its job, think like a defender, and know how to respond.
1 · Match the control2 · Defensive thinking3 · Respond & prevent
That's every block — you've reached the end of the guided exercises. Nice work!
cherry root › exercises › Block 0
Exercise 1 · Decode Binary
Difficulty: beginner · work it out, type the number
🎯 Goal: Convert binary numbers to decimal by hand. Place the digits under 8 4 2 1, then add the places that have a 1.
You'll use:B·06 Binary & HexB·02 CPU
What is binary 1011 in decimal?
Show hint
Under 8 4 2 1: the 1s are over 8, 2 and 1. Add those.
8 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 11.
What is binary 100 in decimal?
Only the 4s place has a 1: 4 + 0 + 0 = 4.
What is binary 1111 in decimal?
All four places are on: 8 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 15 — the largest a 4-bit number holds.
🍒 What you learned: binary is base-2 — each place is a power of two. Add the place values where a 1 appears. This is exactly how a CPU counts.
Next:Exercise 2 · Read permissionsAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 0
Exercise 2 · Read File Permissions
Difficulty: beginner · read the string, then answer
🎯 Goal: Read the permission string -rwxr-xr-- (owner / group / others, each r-w-x).
You'll use:B·09 PermissionsB·07 File System
In -rwxr-xr--, what can others (the last group) do?
The last three characters are r-- — read, no write, no execute. So others can only read.
How many of the three groups (owner, group, others) are allowed to write to this file? Type a number.
Show hint
Look for the w in each group: owner rwx, group r-x, others r--.
Only the owner's rwx contains a w. Group and others cannot write — so just 1.
What does chmod 777 file do?
777 means rwx for owner, group and others — full access for everyone, which is almost always too permissive.
🍒 What you learned: a permission string is owner / group / others, each with r-w-x. Reading it tells you instantly whether a file is over-exposed.
Next:Exercise 3 · Build a pathAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 0
Exercise 3 · Build a Path
Difficulty: beginner · read the tree, type the path
🎯 Goal: Use the folder tree to build an absolute path, and understand why ../ can be risky.
You'll use:B·08 PathB·07 File System
Type the absolute path to notes.txt:
/
  home/
    cherry/
      notes.txt
Show hint
Start at the root / and list every folder down to the file, separated by /.
From the root down: /home/cherry/notes.txt.
In the path /home/cherry/notes.txt, what is the file name?
The part after the last / is the file: notes.txt. Everything before it is folders.
Why is ../../etc/passwd dangerous when it comes from user input?
Each ../ moves up one folder. Unchecked, it lets an attacker reach files outside the intended directory — a path-traversal attack. See A·05.
🍒 What you learned: absolute paths start at / and are unambiguous; the file name is the part after the last /. Relative paths and ../ depend on where you are — exactly what path-traversal attacks abuse.
Next:All exercisesBack to Exercise 1
cherry root › exercises › Block 1
Exercise 1 · Read a URL
Difficulty: beginner · take the URL apart
🎯 Goal: Break this URL into its parts. Reading a URL is the first skill of web recon.
https://shop.example.com/cart?id=42#top
You'll use:W·05 URLW·04 HTTP / HTTPS
What is the scheme (protocol)? Type it without the ://.
The scheme is the part before ://: https.
What is the host (the domain)?
Show hint
It is everything between :// and the first /.
The host is shop.example.com — it sits between the scheme and the path.
Which part is the query string?
The query string starts at ?: ?id=42. It is visible and logged — never put secrets there.
Is the fragment #top sent to the server?
Everything after # stays client-side — the server never receives the fragment.
🍒 What you learned: a URL is scheme + host + path + query + fragment. The query is visible and logged; the fragment never leaves the browser.
Next:Exercise 2 · Status codesAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 1
Exercise 2 · Decode Status Codes
Difficulty: beginner · read what the server is saying
🎯 Goal: HTTP status codes tell you how a request went. Learn to read them at a glance.
You'll use:W·07 Status CodesW·06 Request & Response
What is the status code for a normal, successful page? Type the number.
200 OK means the request succeeded.
A 404 means:
404 is "not found" — a 4xx code, meaning a client-side problem with the request.
Which status-code class (the first digit) means a server error? Type the digit.
Show hint
2xx = success, 3xx = redirect, 4xx = client error, 5xx = ...
5xx codes (like 500) mean the server failed to handle a valid request.
Between 401 and 403, which means "you are not authenticated"?
401 = not authenticated (log in first). 403 = authenticated but not allowed.
🍒 What you learned: 2xx success, 3xx redirect, 4xx client error, 5xx server error. And a 200 only means the HTTP request worked — not that the app did the right thing.
Next:Exercise 3 · Spot the insecureAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 1
Exercise 3 · Spot the Insecure Thing
Difficulty: beginner · think like an attacker
🎯 Goal: Catch the common web-security traps that fool beginners.
You'll use:W·04 HTTP / HTTPSW·05 URLW·09 Cookies
A phishing site has a padlock (HTTPS). Is it safe to trust?
A certificate is free and easy to get. The padlock proves encryption, not honesty — phishing sites use HTTPS too.
Where does this link actually take you? Type the real domain.
https://good.com@evil.com/login
Show hint
Anything before an @ in the host is treated as a username, not the destination.
The part before @ is just a username. The real host is evil.com — a classic phishing trick.
Which cookie flags help protect a session token?
HttpOnly blocks JS access (stops XSS theft), Secure requires HTTPS, and SameSite limits cross-site sending (stops CSRF).
Plain HTTP sends data in ______ — anyone on the network path can read it. (one word)
HTTP is plaintext — unencrypted, so it can be sniffed or modified in transit. HTTPS fixes this.
🍒 What you learned: HTTPS means encrypted, not trustworthy; the real host is right before the first / (watch the @ trick); and session cookies need HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite.
Next:All exercisesBack to Exercise 1
cherry root › exercises › Block 2
Exercise 1 · Ports & Protocols
Difficulty: beginner · know the common ones
🎯 Goal: Memorize the ports you'll meet constantly in recon, and match protocols to behavior.
You'll use:N·10 Ports & ProtocolsN·04 TCP
Which port does HTTPS use by default? Type the number.
HTTPS listens on port 443 (plain HTTP is 80).
Which port does SSH use by default?
Show hint
It's a low, two-digit number — one of the first you learn for remote access.
SSH listens on port 22 by default (though admins often move it).
Which port does DNS commonly use?
DNS uses port 53 — worth remembering, since DNS is a common exfiltration channel.
Which transport protocol is reliable and ordered?
TCP guarantees delivery and order via its handshake and acknowledgements. UDP does not.
🍒 What you learned: 80 = HTTP, 443 = HTTPS, 22 = SSH, 53 = DNS. TCP is reliable and ordered; the others trade that for speed or serve different jobs.
Next:Exercise 2 · TCP, UDP & OSIAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 2
Exercise 2 · TCP, UDP & OSI
Difficulty: beginner · how data moves
🎯 Goal: Tell TCP and UDP apart, and place the OSI model.
You'll use:N·04 TCPN·05 UDPN·03 OSI Model
Which protocol is connectionless and fast (used by DNS, VoIP, games)?
UDP skips the handshake for speed, which is why real-time services use it.
How many steps are in the TCP handshake? Type a number.
Show hint
It's known as the "___-way handshake": SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK.
It's the 3-way handshake: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK.
How many layers does the OSI model have?
The OSI model has 7 layers, from Physical up to Application.
A SYN scan (a stealthy port scan) abuses which mechanism?
A SYN scan sends only the first packet of the handshake to learn if a port is open, without completing the connection.
🍒 What you learned: TCP is reliable (3-way handshake); UDP is fast and connectionless. The OSI model has 7 layers, and scanners like nmap exploit how the handshake works.
Next:Exercise 3 · Devices & defenseAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 2
Exercise 3 · Devices & Defense
Difficulty: beginner · what does what
🎯 Goal: Match network devices to their job, and bust two common security myths.
You'll use:N·07 RouterN·09 FirewallN·15 NAT
What connects different networks together?
A router moves traffic between networks; a switch connects devices within one network.
"A firewall blocks all attacks." True or false?
A network firewall filters by rules. An attack like SQL injection over port 443 looks like allowed traffic and sails through.
Is NAT a security control?
NAT exists to share one public IP among many devices. It happens to hide them, but it is not access control — port forwarding can expose them.
Is a private IP like 192.168.0.5 reachable directly from the internet? Type yes or no.
No — private ranges (192.168.x, 10.x) are internal only and not routable on the public internet.
🍒 What you learned: routers join networks, switches join devices; a firewall filters but doesn't stop app-layer attacks; and NAT shares addresses — it is not a security control.
Next:All exercisesBack to Exercise 1
cherry root › exercises › Block 3
Exercise 1 · Basic Commands
Difficulty: beginner · name the command
🎯 Goal: Match each task to the Linux command that does it. You'll type these every day.
You'll use:L·05 Basic CommandsL·09 Grep
Which command lists files including hidden ones? (command + flag)
Show hint
ls lists files; a flag reveals the dotfiles.
ls -a (or ls -la) shows hidden dotfiles like .ssh and .env.
Which command prints your current directory path? (3 letters)
pwd = "print working directory".
Which command searches text for a pattern? (4 letters)
grep searches files or output for matching lines — perfect for sifting logs.
What does rm do?
There is no recycle bin in the shell — rm deletes for good. rm -rf is especially unforgiving.
🍒 What you learned: ls -a reveals hidden files, pwd shows where you are, grep searches text, and rm deletes permanently.
Next:Exercise 2 · Permissions & sudoAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 3
Exercise 2 · Permissions & Sudo
Difficulty: beginner · privilege basics
🎯 Goal: Understand who can do what, and why running as root all the time is a bad idea.
You'll use:L·06 File PermissionsL·08 Root & Sudo
What does chmod 777 file do?
777 = rwx for owner, group and others. Convenient, but almost always too permissive.
Which command runs a single command with root privileges? (4 letters)
sudo elevates just one command, which is far safer than living as root.
Running as root all the time is...
As root, any mistake or malware has full control. Use a normal account and elevate only when needed.
Which file stores Linux password hashes? Type the full path.
Show hint
It lives in /etc/ and is readable only by root — the "shadow" of the old passwd file.
/etc/shadow holds the hashed passwords, readable only by root.
🍒 What you learned: 777 is full access for everyone, sudo elevates one command, least privilege beats living as root, and password hashes live in /etc/shadow.
Next:Exercise 3 · Pipes, processes, logsAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 3
Exercise 3 · Pipes, Processes & Logs
Difficulty: beginner · chaining and investigating
🎯 Goal: Chain commands, redirect output, and know where to look when investigating.
You'll use:L·10 Pipe & RedirectL·11 ProcessesL·15 Log Files
What does the pipe | do?
A pipe connects programs — e.g. cat log | grep error sends the file into grep.
Between > and >>, which one appends to a file?
> overwrites the whole file; >> appends to the end.
Which directory holds most Linux log files? Type the path.
Logs live under /var/log — the first place to investigate, and what attackers try to clear.
Which command lists running processes? (2 letters)
Show hint
Usually run as ps aux to see everything.
ps (often ps aux) lists processes — handy for spotting malware.
🍒 What you learned: | chains commands, > overwrites while >> appends, logs live in /var/log, and ps lists processes.
Next:All exercisesBack to Exercise 1
cherry root › exercises › Block 4
Exercise 1 · Predict the Output
Difficulty: beginner · read the Python, type the result
🎯 Goal: Run small Python snippets in your head. Automation starts with reading code confidently.
You'll use:P·01 Programming LogicP·08 String ManipulationP·04 Loop
What does this print?
print(2 + 2)
Plain integer addition: 2 + 2 = 4.
What does this print?
print("ab" + "cd")
+ on strings joins them: abcd.
What does this print?
print(len([1, 2, 3]))
len counts the items in the list: 3.
How many lines does this print?
for i in range(3):
    print(i)
Show hint
range(3) produces 0, 1, 2.
range(3) yields three values, so the loop runs 3 times.
🍒 What you learned: + adds numbers but joins strings, len counts items, and range(n) gives n values.
Next:Exercise 2 · Variables & logicAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 4
Exercise 2 · Variables & Logic
Difficulty: beginner · store, compare, decide
🎯 Goal: Store values, compare them correctly, and index a list — the building blocks of any script.
You'll use:P·02 VariableP·05 ConditionP·10 Python for Hackers
How do you store 80 in a variable named port?
Assignment is variable = value. == compares; it does not store.
To compare two values in a condition, which operator do you use?
== tests equality; a single = assigns.
Given nums = [10, 20, 30], what is nums[1]?
Show hint
Index 0 is the first item, so index 1 is the second.
Lists are zero-indexed, so nums[1] is the second item: 20.
Why do hackers script in Python?
Python is quick to write and has libraries for everything — perfect for automating repetitive tasks.
🍒 What you learned: store with =, compare with ==, lists start at index 0, and Python's strength for hackers is fast automation.
Next:Exercise 3 · Lists, dicts & filesAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 4
Exercise 3 · Lists, Dicts & Files
Difficulty: beginner · handle data safely
🎯 Goal: Read values out of a dict, do it safely, and open files the right way.
You'll use:P·07 DictionaryP·09 File I/OP·10 Python for Hackers
Given d = {"user": "admin"}, what does d["user"] give?
A dict fetches the value for a key, so d["user"] is admin.
Which safely reads a key that might be missing (no crash)?
d.get("x") returns None if the key is missing; d["x"] raises a KeyError.
What's the best way to open a file so it closes automatically?
A with block closes the file for you, even if an error happens partway.
Which popular Python library is used to make HTTP requests? (one word, lowercase)
Show hint
Its name is literally the plural of what you send to a server.
The requests library is the go-to for HTTP in Python — great for testing APIs and web apps.
🍒 What you learned: dicts fetch by key, .get() avoids crashes on missing keys, with open(...) auto-closes files, and requests handles HTTP.
Next:All exercisesBack to Exercise 1
cherry root › exercises › Block 5
Exercise 1 · CIA & Core Concepts
Difficulty: beginner · the vocabulary of security
🎯 Goal: Nail the words that everything else builds on — and stop confusing threat, vulnerability and risk.
You'll use:S·01 CIA TriadS·03 VulnerabilityS·05 Risk
What does the C in the CIA triad stand for? (one word)
C is Confidentiality — keeping data secret. (The others are Integrity and Availability.)
A denial-of-service (DoS) attack mainly harms which CIA pillar?
Knocking a service offline attacks Availability — the A in CIA.
A weakness in the system is the...?
A vulnerability is the weakness. A threat is the danger that could use it; risk combines the two with impact.
Risk is best described as...?
Risk weighs how likely harm is against how bad it would be — that's how you prioritize fixes.
🍒 What you learned: CIA = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability. A vulnerability is the weakness, a threat is the danger, and risk is likelihood combined with impact.
Next:Exercise 2 · Encryption vs hashingAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 5
Exercise 2 · Encryption vs Hashing
Difficulty: beginner · don't mix these up
🎯 Goal: Tell encryption, hashing and encoding apart — a mistake here breaks real systems.
You'll use:S·09 EncryptionS·10 Hashing
Hashing is...?
A hash is one-way — you can't get the original back, only compare another hash to it.
Base64 is...?
Base64 just re-represents data — anyone can decode it. It provides no secrecy.
To store passwords safely you use...?
Use a slow, salted hash like bcrypt or argon2. MD5 is fast and crackable; the others aren't hashing at all.
Random data added before hashing a password (so identical passwords differ) is called a ____. (one word)
A salt makes each hash unique, defeating precomputed (rainbow-table) attacks.
🍒 What you learned: encryption is reversible with a key; hashing is one-way; encoding (base64) is neither. Store passwords with a slow, salted hash.
Next:Exercise 3 · AuthN, AuthZ & defenseAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 5
Exercise 3 · AuthN, AuthZ & Defense
Difficulty: beginner · who vs what
🎯 Goal: Separate "who you are" from "what you can do", and name two defensive ideas.
You'll use:S·11 AuthenticationS·12 AuthorizationS·08 Zero Trust
Proving WHO you are is...?
Authentication = proving identity (logging in).
Deciding WHAT you're allowed to access is...?
Authorization = permissions, checked after you're authenticated.
"Never trust, always verify" describes which model?
Zero Trust verifies every request, dropping the idea that the internal network is automatically safe.
Requiring a password plus a phone code is called M__ (the acronym).
Show hint
Multi-Factor Authentication — more than one proof of identity.
MFA (multi-factor auth) means a stolen password alone isn't enough.
🍒 What you learned: authentication proves who you are; authorization controls what you can do. Zero Trust verifies everything, and MFA adds a second factor.
Next:All exercisesBack to Exercise 1
cherry root › exercises › Block 6
Exercise 1 · Identify the Attack
Difficulty: beginner · read the clue, name the attack
🎯 Goal: Given a short scenario, recognise which web attack it is. This is core triage.
You'll use:A·01 SQL InjectionA·02 XSSA·07 IDOR
A login form accepts ' OR 1=1 -- and logs you in. Which flaw?
The input is breaking out of the SQL query — classic SQL injection.
A comment field stores <script> that then runs in other visitors' browsers. This is?
Injecting script that runs in other users' browsers is cross-site scripting (XSS).
Changing ?id=123 to ?id=124 shows someone else's data. This is?
Accessing another object just by changing its ID is an IDOR — a missing authorization check.
An attacker tricks a logged-in user's browser into silently submitting a request. This is?
Riding the victim's session to forge a request is CSRF (cross-site request forgery).
🍒 What you learned: SQLi breaks out of a query, XSS runs script in browsers, IDOR swaps IDs to reach others' data, and CSRF forges requests from a logged-in victim.
Next:Exercise 2 · The right fixAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 6
Exercise 2 · The Right Fix
Difficulty: beginner · defenders' choices
🎯 Goal: For each attack, pick the real fix — not the band-aid.
You'll use:A·01 SQL InjectionA·02 XSSA·05 Path Traversal
Best defense against SQL injection?
Parameterized queries separate code from data for good. Escaping misses cases; a WAF only filters.
Best defense against XSS?
Encode untrusted data when you output it, and add a Content-Security-Policy. HTTPS and obscurity don't stop XSS.
IDOR is really a failure of...?
The server must verify the user actually owns the object — unguessable IDs only slow it down.
Reading files outside a folder with ../../etc/passwd is called ____ traversal. (one word)
Path (or directory) traversal — fixed by canonicalizing the path and confining it to a base folder.
🍒 What you learned: parameterized queries beat SQLi, output encoding + CSP beat XSS, IDOR is an authorization failure, and path traversal is fixed by validating the resolved path.
Next:Exercise 3 · Spot & reasonAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 6
Exercise 3 · Spot & Reason
Difficulty: beginner · think it through
🎯 Goal: Reason about why defenses and attacks behave the way they do.
You'll use:A·05 Path TraversalA·12 Race ConditionD·05 WAF
A WAF blocks an attack. Does that fix the vulnerable code?
A WAF buys time by filtering, but the underlying vulnerability still needs fixing — and WAFs can be bypassed.
../ in user input is dangerous because it can...?
Each ../ moves up one directory, letting an attacker reach files outside the intended folder.
Firing two requests at once to redeem one coupon twice exploits a...?
Two requests hitting the check-then-act gap at the same time is a race condition (TOCTOU).
SQLi, XSS and command injection all come from trusting ____ input. (one word)
Show hint
It comes from the user and hasn't been checked or cleaned.
They all stem from trusting untrusted (user / unsanitized) input. Validate and sanitize everything from outside.
🍒 What you learned: a WAF filters but doesn't fix, ../ escapes folders, simultaneous requests cause race conditions, and the root cause of injection is trusting untrusted input.
Next:All exercisesBack to Exercise 1
cherry root › exercises › Block 7
Exercise 1 · Match the Tool
Difficulty: beginner · pick the right tool for the job
🎯 Goal: Given a task, choose the tool a pentester would reach for.
You'll use:T·01 nmapT·02 Burp SuiteT·03 Wireshark
Discover the open ports on a host. Which tool?
nmap is the standard port and service scanner.
Intercept and edit HTTP requests from the browser. Which tool?
Burp Suite is the intercepting proxy for web app testing.
Crack password hashes fast using a GPU. Which tool?
Hashcat is GPU-optimized for offline hash cracking.
Capture and analyse raw network packets. Which tool?
Wireshark captures and inspects packets — great for seeing plaintext traffic.
🍒 What you learned: nmap = ports, Burp = web proxy, Hashcat = GPU cracking, Wireshark = packet analysis. Right tool, right job.
Next:Exercise 2 · nmap & portsAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 7
Exercise 2 · nmap & Ports
Difficulty: beginner · scanning basics
🎯 Goal: Know a couple of nmap basics, a key port, and the rules of scanning.
You'll use:T·01 nmapT·05 sqlmapN·10 Ports & Protocols
Which nmap flag detects service versions? (type the flag, e.g. -sX)
Show hint
It's -s followed by a capital V (for Version).
-sV probes each open port to identify the service and its version.
Which port does HTTPS use by default? (number)
HTTPS listens on port 443 (plain HTTP is 80).
Is it legal to scan any host you want?
Unauthorized scanning can be a crime. Always get written permission for the target.
Which tool automates finding and exploiting SQL injection?
sqlmap detects and exploits SQLi automatically — use only on authorized targets.
🍒 What you learned: -sV finds service versions, HTTPS is port 443, scanning needs authorization, and sqlmap automates SQL injection.
Next:Exercise 3 · Cracking & web toolsAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 7
Exercise 3 · Cracking & Web Tools
Difficulty: beginner · passwords and HTTP
🎯 Goal: Tell the cracking tools apart and know the everyday web helpers.
You'll use:T·09 HydraT·08 HashcatT·11 curl
Brute-forcing an SSH login over the network. Which tool?
Hydra does online brute force against live services like SSH. (Hashcat/John crack captured hashes offline.)
Hashcat vs John the Ripper — which is the GPU-focused cracker?
Hashcat is built for GPUs; John is primarily CPU-based.
Which command-line tool sends HTTP requests from a script? (4 letters)
curl crafts HTTP requests from the terminal — perfect for testing APIs and reproducing requests.
Find hidden web directories and files. Which tool?
Gobuster (or dirb) brute-forces paths using a wordlist to uncover hidden endpoints.
🍒 What you learned: Hydra = online login brute force, Hashcat = GPU offline cracking, curl = scripted HTTP, Gobuster = directory busting.
Next:All exercisesBack to Exercise 1
cherry root › exercises › Block 8
Exercise 1 · Order the Phases
Difficulty: beginner · first things first
🎯 Goal: Know where each phase sits in an engagement, from first contact to final report.
You'll use:M·01 ReconnaissanceM·05 Privilege EscalationM·10 Reporting
What is the first phase of an engagement?
Recon comes first — you gather information before you touch anything.
Gathering info from public sources, sending no packets to the target, is...?
Passive recon (OSINT) uses public data, so it never touches the target — quiet and low-risk.
After landing a low-privilege shell, the usual next goal is...?
You usually land limited, then escalate to root/admin to gain full control.
What is the final deliverable the client actually pays for?
The report is the product — findings only create value when communicated clearly enough to fix.
🍒 What you learned: the flow is recon → scan → enumerate → exploit → escalate ... → report. Recon is first; the report is the deliverable.
Next:Exercise 2 · Name the phaseAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 8
Exercise 2 · Name the Phase
Difficulty: beginner · describe → phase
🎯 Goal: Match each activity to the methodology phase it belongs to.
You'll use:M·03 EnumerationM·06 PersistenceM·08 Exfiltration
Digging into a service for usernames, versions and shares is...?
Enumeration extracts detail from the services scanning found.
Keeping access so you survive a reboot is...?
Persistence is establishing a foothold that survives reboots and disconnects.
Pivoting from the first machine to others on the network is...?
Lateral movement spreads from the initial host toward higher-value systems.
Stealing the data out of the network is...?
Exfiltration is the actual data theft — often hidden inside normal traffic like HTTPS or DNS.
🍒 What you learned: enumeration = detail from services, persistence = surviving reboots, lateral movement = pivoting, exfiltration = data theft.
Next:Exercise 3 · Reason it throughAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 8
Exercise 3 · Reason It Through
Difficulty: beginner · the why behind the phases
🎯 Goal: Reason about why the methodology works the way it does.
You'll use:M·01 ReconnaissanceM·09 Covering TracksM·10 Reporting
Why do recon first?
Knowing the target up front makes every later step faster and more accurate.
Killing a malicious process always removes the threat — true?
Persistent malware restarts itself, so killing the process is not enough — you must remove its persistence.
Clearing the local logs hides everything — true?
Logs shipped off the host survive a local wipe — and a sudden gap in logs is itself an alert.
A good report includes findings, severity, evidence and ____. (one word: the fix)
Show hint
The client needs to know how to fix each finding — the recommended action.
Remediation — a finding without a recommended fix is hard for the client to act on.
🍒 What you learned: recon shapes everything, killing a process doesn't beat persistence, central logging survives a local wipe, and a report needs remediation advice.
Next:All exercisesBack to Exercise 1
cherry root › exercises › Block 9
Exercise 1 · Spot the Attack
Difficulty: beginner · clue → attack name
🎯 Goal: Recognise the social-engineering attack from a short scenario.
You'll use:E·02 Spear PhishingE·04 BaitingE·06 Vishing
A targeted, well-researched phishing email aimed at one executive is...?
Personalised, targeted phishing is spear phishing; aimed at a big fish (an exec) it's "whaling".
A fake "IT support" phone call pressuring you for your password is...?
Voice phishing = vishing. A live, urgent caller pushes you to act before thinking.
A USB drive "accidentally" left in the parking lot is...?
Tempting a curious victim with a dropped device is baiting.
Following an employee through a badge-only door is...?
Slipping in behind an authorised person is tailgating — it exploits politeness.
🍒 What you learned: spear phishing = targeted email, vishing = voice, baiting = tempting device, tailgating = following through a door.
Next:Exercise 2 · Don't get fooledAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 9
Exercise 2 · Don't Get Fooled
Difficulty: beginner · bust the myths
🎯 Goal: Drop the dangerous assumptions that make people easy targets.
You'll use:E·01 PhishingE·06 VishingE·10 Impersonation
"I can always spot a phishing email" — true?
Overconfidence is exactly what attackers exploit; copied branding and tone fool careful people too.
"Caller ID proves who's calling" — true?
Attackers can make any name or number appear. Call back on an official number to verify.
"A found USB is safe to plug in just to find the owner" — true?
Dropped USBs are a classic bait. Never plug an unknown device into your machine.
Best general defense against social engineering?
SE targets people, so the defenses are human: verify out-of-band, require MFA, and train regularly.
🍒 What you learned: anyone can be phished, caller ID lies, unknown USBs are bait, and the real defense is verification + MFA + training.
Next:Exercise 3 · Recognise the techniqueAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 9
Exercise 3 · Recognise the Technique
Difficulty: beginner · name the rest
🎯 Goal: Round out the social-engineering vocabulary.
You'll use:E·07 SmishingE·08 Watering HoleE·03 Pretexting
Phishing carried out over SMS text messages is...?
SMS phishing = smishing. Short links hide the destination and people tap fast on mobile.
Compromising a legitimate site the target group visits, to attack them there, is...?
A watering hole infects a trusted site the victims already use — stealthy because they trust it.
Offering "free tech support" in exchange for access or info is...?
Trading a "favour" for something exploits reciprocity — that's quid pro quo.
Inventing a believable backstory (e.g. "I'm from IT") to manipulate someone is called ____ing. (one word root)
Show hint
The made-up scenario is called a "pretext".
Pretexting — the believable story is the attack. Defend by verifying identity through a known channel.
🍒 What you learned: smishing = SMS, watering hole = compromise a trusted site, quid pro quo = a "favour" for access, pretexting = a fake backstory.
Next:All exercisesBack to Exercise 1
cherry root › exercises › Block 10
Exercise 1 · Identify the Malware
Difficulty: beginner · behaviour → type
🎯 Goal: Match each behaviour to the malware family it describes.
You'll use:X·02 WormX·03 TrojanX·04 Ransomware
Self-spreads across networks with no user action. Which type?
A worm self-propagates over the network. A virus needs a host file and a user to run it.
Disguised as useful software that you run yourself. Which type?
A trojan hides malice behind a legitimate-looking program.
Encrypts your files and demands payment. Which type?
Ransomware locks your data and demands payment for the key.
Secretly records everything you type. Which type?
A keylogger captures keystrokes — a way to steal passwords directly.
🍒 What you learned: worm = self-spreads, trojan = disguised, ransomware = encrypts for payment, keylogger = records keys.
Next:Exercise 2 · Bust the mythAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 10
Exercise 2 · Bust the Myth
Difficulty: beginner · true or false
🎯 Goal: Correct the malware misconceptions that get people compromised.
You'll use:X·01 VirusX·04 RansomwareX·06 Rootkit
"All malware is a virus" — true?
Worms, trojans, ransomware and more are separate categories with different behaviour.
"Antivirus catches everything" — true?
Brand-new or packed samples have no known signature, so AV alone is never enough.
"Paying the ransom guarantees you get your files back" — true?
There's no guarantee of a working key, and paying encourages further attacks. Backups are the real answer.
"Reinstalling the OS always removes a rootkit" — true?
Rootkits in firmware persist even after wiping the disk — deep, stealthy, and hard to remove.
🍒 What you learned: virus is one type among many, AV misses novel malware, paying ransom isn't a fix, and firmware rootkits survive reinstalls.
Next:Exercise 3 · Name the restAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 10
Exercise 3 · Name the Rest
Difficulty: beginner · finish the family
🎯 Goal: Cover the remaining malware types and the one defense that beats ransomware.
You'll use:X·09 BotnetX·08 BackdoorX·10 Logic Bomb
A network of infected devices controlled by an attacker is a...?
A botnet is many compromised devices (even IoT) under one attacker's command — used for DDoS, spam, mining.
Hidden access that bypasses the normal login is a...?
A backdoor skips authentication to give quiet, persistent access.
Malware that lies dormant until a date or event triggers it is a...?
A logic bomb waits for a trigger, which is why it evades early detection — insiders are a classic source.
The single best defense against ransomware is offline / immutable ____. (one word, plural)
Show hint
If your files are encrypted, you just restore from a clean, offline copy.
Backups — offline/immutable copies (the 3-2-1 rule) let you recover without paying.
🍒 What you learned: botnet = controlled device army, backdoor = hidden access, logic bomb = triggered payload, and backups beat ransomware.
Next:All exercisesBack to Exercise 1
cherry root › exercises › Block 11
Exercise 1 · Match the Control
Difficulty: beginner · job → control
🎯 Goal: Match each defensive control to what it actually does.
You'll use:D·01 FirewallD·03 SIEMD·05 WAF
Filters network traffic by allow/deny rules. Which control?
A firewall allows or blocks traffic by rules — the classic network gatekeeper.
Detects and alerts on suspicious traffic but does not block it. Which control?
An IDS detects and alerts; an IPS sits inline and can actively block.
Aggregates and correlates logs to detect threats. Which control?
A SIEM is the central nervous system for detection — it correlates logs and raises alerts.
Inspects and filters web traffic to block SQLi/XSS attempts. Which control?
A Web Application Firewall understands HTTP and blocks common web attacks — but it filters, it doesn't fix the code.
🍒 What you learned: firewall = network rules, IDS = detect/alert, SIEM = correlate logs, WAF = web-layer filtering.
Next:Exercise 2 · Defensive thinkingAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 11
Exercise 2 · Defensive Thinking
Difficulty: beginner · the blue-team mindset
🎯 Goal: Adopt the defender's mindset — layers, patching, and tested backups.
You'll use:D·06 HoneypotD·07 Patch ManagementD·08 Backup Strategy
A decoy system built to attract and study attackers is a...?
A honeypot lures attackers for early warning and intel — it must be well isolated, or it becomes a foothold.
Why patch promptly?
Attackers weaponize disclosed vulnerabilities within days — patching closes the easy doors.
"Having backups is enough" — true?
An untested backup may be corrupt or incomplete. A backup is only real if you can restore from it.
Defense in depth means...?
Layered controls cover each other's gaps — if one fails, the next still slows the attacker.
🍒 What you learned: a honeypot is a decoy, patching closes known holes, backups must be tested, and defense in depth stacks controls.
Next:Exercise 3 · Respond & preventAll exercises
cherry root › exercises › Block 11
Exercise 3 · Respond & Prevent
Difficulty: beginner · the last mile
🎯 Goal: Know how to respond to a breach and where the biggest risk really is.
You'll use:D·09 Incident ResponseD·10 Security Awareness TrainingD·08 Backup Strategy
When a breach happens, you should...?
Follow the plan: identify, contain, eradicate, recover, learn. Wiping too early destroys evidence.
The biggest attack surface in most organisations is...?
Most breaches start with a person being phished or tricked — which is why training matters.
Best org-wide protection against phishing?
Since people are targeted, ongoing training plus MFA (so a stolen password isn't enough) is the strongest combo.
The 3-2-1 rule is about your ____ strategy. (one word)
Show hint
3 copies, on 2 media, with 1 kept offsite/offline.
Backup — 3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offline/offsite. Your last line of defense against ransomware and disaster.
🍒 What you learned: follow an IR plan during a breach, people are the biggest attack surface, training + MFA beat phishing, and 3-2-1 is your backup rule. 🍒 You've finished every guided exercise — congratulations!
Next:All exercisesBack to Exercise 1
cherry root › practice mode
Practice Mode
Real-world scenarios — read the clue, answer, and check. The feedback explains the why. No Kali required.
⚠ Identify the Attack
A web server log shows this request. Which attack is it?
GET /login?user=admin' OR '1'='1' --
The ' OR '1'='1' -- is breaking out of a SQL query — classic SQL injection in the login.
And this one?
GET /search?q=<script>alert(1)</script>
Injecting a <script> tag into a parameter that gets reflected is cross-site scripting (XSS).
And this request?
GET /download?file=../../../../etc/passwd
The ../ sequences climb out of the intended folder to reach /etc/passwd — path traversal.
⚠ Spot the Phish
Is this email phishing or legitimate?
From: support@paypa1.com
Subject: Your account is LOCKED
Click http://bit.ly/verify-now within 24h
or your account will be deleted.
Phishing — a misspelled domain (paypa1.com), false urgency, and a shortened link hiding the real destination.
And this one?
From: hr@yourcompany.com
Subject: Team lunch Friday
Reminder: team lunch this Friday at noon
in the break room. No action needed.
Probably legitimate — internal sender, no link, no urgency, and nothing is being requested. Still, verify if unsure.
⚠ Read the nmap
How many ports are open in this scan? Type a number.
PORT     STATE   SERVICE
22/tcp   open    ssh
80/tcp   open    http
443/tcp  open    https
3306/tcp closed  mysql
Three show open (22, 80, 443). Port 3306 is closed, so it doesn't count.
In that scan, which service is running on port 22?
The line 22/tcp open ssh tells you SSH is listening on port 22 — a prime target for login attacks.
⚠ Decode the URL
What's suspicious about this link?
https://paypal.com.secure-login.ru/account
The real domain is the part right before the first /: secure-login.ru. paypal.com is just a subdomain label to fool you.
Where does this link actually take you? Type the real domain.
https://good.com@evil.com/login
Show hint
Anything before an @ in the host is treated as a username, not the destination.
The part before @ is just a username. The real host is evil.com.
⚠ Match the Tool
You need to capture and read raw network packets. Which tool?
Wireshark captures and analyses packets. nmap scans ports, Hydra brute-forces logins, sqlmap targets SQLi.
You found an SSH login and want to brute-force the password (with authorization). Which tool?
Hydra does online brute force against live services like SSH. (Remember: only with explicit permission.)
🍒 Nice work: identifying attacks from logs, spotting phishing, reading nmap, decoding URLs and picking the right tool are everyday skills. Want step-by-step practice? Try the Guided Exercises.
cherry root › practice
Practice Mode
Active recall over the terms. Flip each card, then rate yourself — your ratings update the Studied / Review tags.
Stop practice
cherry root › study paths
Study Paths
Curated routes through the grimoire. Pick a path and follow the terms in order.
Total Beginner
New to all of this. A gentle, ordered route through the absolute essentials — how a computer works, how the internet works, a first look at networks and Linux, and the core ideas of security.
★☆☆☆☆15 terms·~3 hours
B·01B·04B·05W·01W·02N·01L·01S·01S·03P·01
Web Security
Focused on hacking and defending web applications. Cover how the web works, the core security concepts, the major web attacks (SQLi, XSS, CSRF, IDOR), and the tools to test them.
★★★☆☆17 terms·~6 hours
Web›Sec›Attacks›Tools
Network & Linux
Build the infrastructure foundation every hacker needs. Work through networking essentials, then the Linux command line and permissions, and finish with the core network tools.
★★☆☆☆16 terms·~5 hours
Net›Linux›Tools
Full Pentest
Walk the complete engagement from start to finish: security fundamentals, then every phase of the methodology — recon, scanning, exploitation, escalation, persistence, exfiltration, and reporting.
★★★★☆19 terms·~8 hours
Recon›Exploit›PrivEsc›Report
Speedrun
One key term from each of the twelve blocks — a fast orientation tour that touches every major area. Use it to map the territory before diving in deep.
★★☆☆☆12 terms·~1.5 hours
B·01›W·01›N·01›S·01›A·01›D·01
cherry root › install app
Install App
Cherry Root works offline as an installable app. Add it to your home screen or desktop for a full-screen, no-browser experience.
iOS — Safari
1
Open this page in Safari (not Chrome — only Safari can install on iOS).
2
Tap the Share button (the square with an upward arrow) in the toolbar.
3
Scroll down and tap Add to Home Screen.
4
Confirm the name "Cherry Root" and tap Add. The icon appears on your home screen.
Android — Chrome
1
Open this page in Chrome.
2
Tap the menu (three dots) in the top-right corner.
3
Tap Install app or Add to Home screen.
4
Confirm. Cherry Root installs as a standalone app and runs offline.
Desktop — Chrome / Edge
1
Open this page in Chrome or Edge.
2
Look for the install icon (a monitor with a downward arrow) at the right end of the address bar.
3
Click it, then click Install. Cherry Root opens in its own window and is available offline.
cherry root › index
Index
Every term in the grimoire in order. Click any term to go to its page.
Progress Rings
filter: All Studied Review Hard Favorites
sort:
CodeTermOne-liner
Block 0 · PC Basics
B·01HardwarePhysical parts of a computer
B·02CPUThe brain — executes instructions
B·03RAMTemporary memory used while running programs
B·04SoftwarePrograms that run on hardware
B·05Operating SystemSoftware that manages everything else
B·06Binary & HexThe number systems computers actually use
B·07File SystemHow the OS organizes and stores files
B·08PathThe address of a file inside the system
B·09PermissionsWho can read, write or run a file
B·10User & AdminRegular user vs full-control user
B·11Terminal / CLIText interface to control the system
B·12Environment VariablesSystem-wide values programs can read
B·13ProcessA program currently running
B·14Port (local)Channel a program listens on
B·QRQuick ReferenceKey terms, commands & reminders
B·MMMind MapVisual summaries of every term
B·MNMy NotesFree space for discoveries & questions
Block 1 · How the Internet Works
W·01Internet vs WebNot the same thing
W·02IP AddressUnique address of a device on a network
W·03DNSTranslates domain names into IP addresses
W·04HTTP / HTTPSProtocol for loading web pages
W·05URLThe full address of a web resource
W·06Request & ResponseHow browser and server talk
W·07Status CodesServer reply codes (200, 404, 500…)
W·08HeadersMetadata sent with every request
W·09CookiesSmall files stored to remember the user
W·10SessionsServer-side way to keep a user logged in
W·11APIInterface for programs to talk to each other
W·12Client & ServerWho asks, who answers
W·13CDNDistributed servers that speed up delivery
W·14SSL / TLSEncryption layer that makes HTTPS work
W·QRQuick ReferenceStatus codes, headers & URL anatomy
W·MMMind MapVisual summaries of every term
W·MNMy NotesFree space for discoveries & questions
Block 2 · Networks
N·01NetworkGroup of connected devices
N·02LAN vs WANLocal vs wide area network
N·03OSI Model7-layer framework for understanding networks
N·04TCPReliable, ordered packet delivery
N·05UDPFaster, connectionless alternative to TCP
N·06MAC AddressPhysical hardware address of a device
N·07RouterConnects different networks together
N·08SwitchConnects devices within the same network
N·09FirewallFilters traffic by rules
N·10Ports & ProtocolsWhich door each service uses
N·11PacketUnit of data traveling through a network
N·12BandwidthMaximum data transfer capacity
N·13ProxyIntermediary between client and server
N·14VPNEncrypted tunnel over the internet
N·15NATTranslates private IPs to public ones
N·16SubnetDivision of a network into smaller parts
N·17DHCPAutomatically assigns IP addresses
Block 3 · Linux
L·01LinuxOpen-source OS used in most security tools
L·02DistroA version/flavor of Linux
L·03Shell / BashThe terminal language
L·04File System StructureHow Linux organizes folders
L·05Basic Commandsls, cd, cat, mkdir, rm, cp, mv
L·06File Permissionsrwx — who can do what to a file
L·07Users & GroupsHow Linux manages access by identity
L·08Root & SudoSuperuser and how to borrow its power
L·09GrepSearch for patterns inside files
L·10Pipe & RedirectConnect commands and save output
L·11ProcessesRunning programs and how to manage them
L·12SSHRemote access to another machine securely
L·13Package ManagerHow to install software (apt, brew…)
L·14CronSchedule tasks to run automatically
L·15Log FilesSystem records of what happened and when
Block 4 · Programming Basics
P·01Programming LogicThinking like a machine — step by step.
P·02VariableA named container for data.
P·03FunctionReusable block of logic with a name.
P·04LoopRepeating work until done.
P·05ConditionMaking decisions in code.
P·06Array / ListAn ordered collection of values.
P·07DictionaryKey-value mapping for fast lookup.
P·08String ManipulationWorking with text.
P·09File I/OReading from and writing to disk.
P·10Python for HackersWhy Python is the hacker's language.
Block 5 · Security Fundamentals
S·01CIA TriadThe three pillars of every security decision.
S·02ThreatA potential cause of harm.
S·03VulnerabilityA weakness that can be exploited.
S·04ExploitWeaponizing a vulnerability.
S·05RiskThe chance and impact of something going wrong.
S·06Attack SurfaceEverything exposed that could be targeted.
S·07Defense in DepthLayered defense — if one fails, the next catches it.
S·08Zero TrustTrust nothing. Verify everything.
S·09EncryptionMaking data unreadable to anyone without the key.
S·10HashingA one-way fingerprint of data.
S·11AuthenticationProving who you are.
S·12AuthorizationDeciding what you're allowed to do.
Block 6 · Web Attacks
A·01SQL InjectionInjecting SQL into a query that wasn't supposed to take any.
A·02XSSInjecting JavaScript that runs in another user's browser.
A·03CSRFTricking a logged-in user into doing actions without consent.
A·04SSRFMaking the server send requests on the attacker's behalf.
A·05Path TraversalEscaping the intended directory with ../ sequences.
A·06Command InjectionInjecting OS commands into a system call.
A·07IDORInsecure Direct Object Reference — changing an ID, seeing someone else's data.
A·08Open RedirectSending users to attacker-controlled URLs via your domain.
A·09ClickjackingTricking the user into clicking something they didn't see.
A·10Session HijackingStealing or guessing a session token to impersonate a user.
A·11Authentication BypassLogging in without valid credentials.
A·12Race ConditionTwo operations colliding in time to break a check.
Block 7 · Pentest Tools
T·01nmapNetwork mapper, port scanner, service enumerator.
T·02Burp SuiteWeb proxy for intercepting/modifying HTTP traffic.
T·03WiresharkNetwork protocol analyzer.
T·04MetasploitExploit framework with payloads and listeners.
T·05sqlmapAutomated SQL injection scanner and exploiter.
T·06Gobuster / DirbDirectory and file enumeration via wordlist brute force.
T·07John the RipperCPU-based password cracker for hashes and encrypted files.
T·08HashcatGPU-accelerated password cracker — the fastest in the world.
T·09HydraNetwork login brute-forcer for SSH, FTP, HTTP forms and more.
T·10netcatSwiss army knife of networking — listener, scanner, file transfer.
T·11curlCLI HTTP client for testing APIs and crafting requests.
T·12Browser DevToolsInspecting requests, cookies, JS and storage — built into every browser.
Block 8 · Pentest Methodology
M·01ReconnaissancePassive and active gathering of target intelligence.
M·02ScanningPort, service and version discovery on live targets.
M·03EnumerationExtracting usernames, shares, banners and structural info.
M·04ExploitationTriggering a vulnerability to gain unauthorized access.
M·05Privilege EscalationMoving from low-privilege user to root or SYSTEM.
M·06PersistenceMaintaining access through reboots, patches and detection sweeps.
M·07Lateral MovementPivoting from one compromised host to others.
M·08ExfiltrationStealing data from the target via covert channels.
M·09Covering TracksClearing logs and removing artifacts to delay detection.
M·10ReportingDocumenting findings for the client — the actual product.
Block 9 · Social Engineering
E·01PhishingMass email to trick clicks, credentials or attachment execution.
E·02Spear PhishingTargeted phishing with personalized lure for one person or small group.
E·03PretextingFabricated scenario to extract information or trigger action.
E·04BaitingPhysical or digital lure — USB drops, fake downloads, free stuff.
E·05TailgatingPhysical entry by following an authorized person through a door.
E·06VishingVoice phishing — social engineering over phone calls.
E·07SmishingSMS phishing — phishing via text messages.
E·08Watering HoleCompromising websites the target population visits.
E·09Quid Pro QuoOffering something in exchange for information or access.
E·10ImpersonationPretending to be someone of authority — IT, executive, vendor, regulator.
Block 10 · Malware
X·01VirusSelf-replicating code that infects host files.
X·02WormSelf-propagating malware; spreads across networks without a host.
X·03TrojanDisguised as legit software; payload activates on execution.
X·04RansomwareEncrypts data; demands ransom for the decryption key.
X·05SpywareCovert surveillance — keys, screen, mic, browser data.
X·06RootkitHides at OS / kernel level to maintain stealth access.
X·07KeyloggerRecords every keystroke — hardware or software.
X·08BackdoorHidden access mechanism bypassing normal authentication.
X·09BotnetNetwork of compromised devices controlled remotely.
X·10Logic BombTriggers on specific event, date, or condition.
Block 11 · Defense
D·01FirewallFilters traffic by rules — stateful vs stateless.
D·02IDS / IPSIntrusion Detection (passive) and Prevention (blocks) Systems.
D·03SIEMCentralized log aggregation, correlation, and alerting.
D·04EDREndpoint Detection & Response — visibility, hunt, respond.
D·05WAFWeb Application Firewall — Layer 7 filtering.
D·06HoneypotDecoy systems to attract and study attackers.
D·07Patch ManagementTracking and deploying vendor fixes — KEV / CVSS / EPSS.
D·08Backup Strategy3-2-1 rule, offline backups, restore testing.
D·09Incident ResponsePrep / Identify / Contain / Eradicate / Recover / Lessons.
D·10Security Awareness TrainingHuman-firewall: phishing tests + education.
Back of Book · Master Quick References
QR·01Ports CheatsheetMost important ports and what uses them
QR·02HTTP Status CodesAll codes grouped and explained
QR·03Linux CommandsCommands you will use every single day
QR·04OSI LayersEach layer, its job and an example
QR·05Attack to Cause to FixEvery web attack in one table
QR·06Pentest ChecklistPhase by phase — what to do in what order
QR·07Malware ID GuideType to behavior to detection clue
QR·08Tools at a GlanceTool to what it does to basic command
cherry root › glossary
Glossary
One-liner definitions for fast memory triggers. Alphabetical order. All blocks covered.
A
ACLAccess Control List — rules defining who can access what resource
algorithmStep-by-step set of instructions a computer follows
APIApplication Programming Interface — lets programs talk to each other
ASCIIStandard encoding that maps text characters to numbers
asymmetric encryptionUses a public key to encrypt and a private key to decrypt
attack surfaceEverything exposed that could be targeted by an attacker
authenticationProving who you are — login, password, biometrics
authorizationWhat you are allowed to do after authenticating
argon2idModern password-hashing KDF — winner of the Password Hashing Competition
arrayOrdered collection of values accessed by numeric index
atomic operationOperation that completes fully or not at all — no half states visible
authentication bypassLogging in without valid credentials via logic flaws or default creds
B
backdoorHidden access mechanism that bypasses normal authentication
botnetNetwork of compromised devices remotely controlled via a C2 server
bootkitRootkit that infects the boot loader (MBR/UEFI) and loads before the OS
baitingLeaving attractive bait (USB sticks, downloads) hoping a victim runs it
BadUSBMalicious device disguised as a USB stick that injects keystrokes on plug-in
Burp SuiteIndustry-standard web proxy for intercepting and modifying HTTP traffic
BloodHoundAD attack-path visualization tool for finding shortest path to Domain Admin
backdoorHidden access left open in a system for future entry
baitingLeaving something tempting (USB, link) to trick a victim
bandwidthMaximum data transfer capacity of a connection
bashMost common shell on Linux — interprets your commands
binaryBase-2 number system — only 0s and 1s
bitSmallest unit of data — a single 0 or 1
blue teamTeam responsible for defense, monitoring and incident response
bootloaderSoftware that loads the OS when you power on
botnetNetwork of infected machines under attacker control
broken authExploiting weak login or session logic to gain access
bufferTemporary memory area for data in transit
bug bountyProgram that pays researchers to find real vulnerabilities
byte8 bits — smallest addressable unit of memory
bcryptAdaptive password-hashing function — slow on purpose to resist GPUs
C
C2Command and Control — the server an attacker uses to control malware / bots
CISA KEVCISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog — patch with priority
Cobalt StrikeCommercial red-team C2 framework heavily abused by ransomware operators
covering tracksErasing forensic evidence of an intrusion — logs, history, artifacts
curlCommand-line HTTP client for crafting requests and testing APIs
C2 serverCommand and Control — attacker center for managing the botnet
CDNContent Delivery Network — distributed servers that speed up delivery
certificateDigital document proving identity on the web — used in HTTPS
chmodLinux command to change file permissions
CIA TriadConfidentiality, Integrity, Availability — the three pillars of security
CLICommand Line Interface — interacting with a system via text commands
clientThe device or program that sends a request to a server
closed portA port not accepting connections — not listening
command injectionExecuting system commands through unsanitized user input
cookieSmall file stored in the browser to remember user state
core (CPU)Individual processing unit inside a CPU — more cores equals more parallel work
covering tracksRemoving logs and evidence of an intrusion
CPUCentral Processing Unit — the brain that executes all instructions
CSRFCross-Site Request Forgery — tricking a user into making unwanted requests
CTFCapture The Flag — legal hacking competition to practice skills
CVECommon Vulnerabilities and Exposures — unique ID for a known vulnerability
cryptographyTurning data into unreadable form to protect it from unauthorized access
clickjackingTricking users into clicking hidden UI by overlaying invisible frames
conditionBranching point in code — if/elif/else evaluating boolean expressions
CSPContent Security Policy — HTTP header restricting which scripts/resources can load
CSRFCross-Site Request Forgery — abusing logged-in browsers to perform unwanted actions
D
DevToolsBuilt-in browser developer tools (F12) for inspecting requests, DOM, storage, JS
deepfakeAI-generated voice or video impersonating a real person convincingly
DHCPDynamic Host Configuration Protocol — automatically assigns IP addresses
distroA version and flavor of Linux (e.g. Kali, Ubuntu, Debian)
DNSDomain Name System — translates domain names into IP addresses
dropperMalware whose purpose is to install other malware
defense in depthLayered security — overlapping controls so no single failure equals a breach
dictionaryKey-value mapping for fast lookup — Python dict / hash table
E
EDREndpoint Detection & Response — telemetry-rich successor to antivirus
EmotetNotorious banking trojan / loader botnet, taken down 2021, returned later
EternalBlueLeaked NSA SMBv1 exploit (CVE-2017-0144) used by WannaCry and NotPetya
EvilginxReal-time MitM phishing framework that captures session cookies to bypass MFA
enumerationTargeted extraction of usernames, shares, banners and details from discovered services
exfiltrationMoving stolen data from target network to attacker infrastructure
exploitationTriggering a vulnerability to gain initial unauthorized access
encryptionTransforming data so only authorized parties can read it
env variableSystem-wide value stored in memory that any program can read
executableA file the OS can run as a program (binary)
exploitCode or technique that abuses a vulnerability to gain access
exploitationThe phase of using a vulnerability to actually gain access
ext4Default Linux file system format
exploit (entry)Code or technique that takes advantage of a vulnerability
F
firewallFilters network traffic by rules — stateful tracks connections, stateless per-packet
FAT32File system used on USB drives and older storage media
file systemSystem that organizes and addresses all files on disk
firewallFilters network traffic using rules — allows or blocks packets
firmwareLow-level software embedded directly into hardware
forensicsInvestigating what happened during or after a security incident
functionReusable block of code that performs a specific task
FIDO2Modern phishing-resistant auth standard using hardware security keys
file I/OReading from and writing to files on disk
functionReusable named block of code that takes inputs and returns output
G
GobusterFast Go-based brute-force tool for directories, files, vhosts and subdomains
grepLinux command to search for patterns inside files or output
H
honeypotDecoy system instrumented to detect and study attackers
honeytokenFake credential, file, or record that fires an alert when used
HashcatGPU-accelerated password cracker — fastest of its class
HydraNetwork login brute-forcer supporting 50+ protocols (SSH, FTP, RDP, HTTP)
hardeningReducing attack surface by removing weaknesses and unnecessary services
hardwarePhysical parts of a computer — keyboard, CPU, RAM, disk
hashOne-way fingerprint of data — cannot be reversed
heapPart of RAM used for dynamic memory allocation at runtime
hexHexadecimal — base-16 number system, shorthand for binary
honeypotFake system designed to attract and study attackers
HTTPHyperText Transfer Protocol — protocol for loading web pages
HTTPSHTTP with TLS encryption — the secure version of HTTP
I
IDSIntrusion Detection System — passive monitoring and alerting on attacks
IPSIntrusion Prevention System — inline, blocks attacks in real time
infostealerMalware that harvests saved passwords, cookies, and wallets (RedLine, Vidar, Lumma)
incident responseStructured handling of security incidents: PICERL — Prep, Identify, Contain, Eradicate, Recover, Lessons
impersonationAssuming another identity (IT, executive, vendor) to leverage trust and access
IDORInsecure Direct Object Reference — accessing another user data by changing an ID
IDSIntrusion Detection System — monitors and alerts on suspicious activity
impersonationPretending to be a trusted person to extract info or access
incident responseStructured process for handling and recovering from a security breach
IPSIntrusion Prevention System — detects and actively blocks suspicious activity
IP addressUnique numerical address of a device on a network
IDORInsecure Direct Object Reference — accessing others' data by changing an ID
J
John the RipperClassic CPU-based password cracker with hundreds of supported hash formats
K
keyloggerCaptures every keystroke — software (API hooks) or hardware (inline USB)
kernelCore of the OS — manages hardware resources directly
keyloggerMalware that records every keystroke the victim types
L
logic bombMalicious code that activates only when a specific condition is met
lateral movementPivoting from initial foothold to other hosts in the internal network
LANLocal Area Network — network within a limited physical area
least privilegeGiving only the minimum access needed — nothing more
log filesSystem records of events — what happened and when
loopCode structure that repeats a block of instructions (for, while)
LFILocal File Inclusion — reading server files via path traversal
listPython's ordered, mutable sequence — same as array in other languages
loopBlock of code that repeats — for or while in Python
M
MetasploitModular exploit framework standardizing exploit/payload/listener workflow
MeterpreterMetasploit's in-memory payload providing extensive post-exploitation capabilities
MAC addressPhysical hardware address permanently assigned to a network device
malwareAny software intentionally designed to cause harm
memory dumpSnapshot of RAM contents captured at a specific moment
MFAMulti-Factor Authentication — verifying identity with 2 or more factors
motherboardMain circuit board that connects all hardware components
mount pointLocation in the file system where a storage device is attached
N
netcatSwiss-army-knife TCP/UDP tool — listener, scanner, file transfer, reverse shell
nmapNetwork mapper for host, port, service and version discovery
NATNetwork Address Translation — maps private IPs to a public IP
networkGroup of connected devices that can communicate with each other
NTFSDefault Windows file system format
O
open portA port actively listening and accepting connections
OSOperating System — software that manages hardware and all other software
OSINTOpen Source Intelligence — gathering public info about a target
OSI model7-layer framework explaining how data travels through a network
OWASP Top 10List of the 10 most critical web application security risks
OAuthStandard protocol for delegated access — login with Google/GitHub
open redirectEndpoint that redirects to a user-controlled URL — abused for phishing
P
patch managementDisciplined process of testing and deploying vendor security fixes within an SLA
PegasusNSO Group commercial spyware with zero-click iOS/Android exploits
persistenceMaintaining access through reboots, patches and detection sweeps
pretextingFabricating a believable scenario to extract information or trigger action
privilege escalationMoving from low-privilege user to root or SYSTEM
packetSmall unit of data that travels independently through a network
pathFull address of a file or folder inside the file system
path traversalUsing ../ in input to escape allowed directories and read sensitive files
payloadThe malicious part of an attack — what actually executes
pentestAuthorized simulation of an attack to find vulnerabilities
peripheralExternal hardware connected to a computer (printer, mouse, USB)
permissionsRules that define who can read, write or execute a file
persistenceMaintaining access to a system even after reboot
phishingSending fake emails to trick users into giving up credentials
PIDProcess ID — unique number assigned to each running process
portNumbered channel a service listens on for incoming connections
post-exploitationActions taken after gaining access — moving laterally, escalating
pretextingCreating a fabricated scenario to extract sensitive info
priv escalationGoing from limited user access to full admin or root control
processA program currently being executed by the CPU with its own PID
process injectionEmbedding malicious code inside a legitimate running process
proxyIntermediary that sits between client and server — can intercept traffic
path traversalEscaping the intended directory using ../ sequences
PoCProof of Concept — minimal exploit demonstrating a vulnerability works
PythonHigh-level interpreted language — dominant in offensive tooling and CTFs
Q
quid pro quoSocial-engineering attack offering a benefit in exchange for information or access
R
ransomwareMalware that encrypts data and demands payment for decryption; often double-extorts via leaks
RATRemote Access Trojan — gives operator full control of the victim machine
rootkitMalware that hides at kernel / firmware level to evade detection
RPO / RTORecovery Point / Time Objective — acceptable data loss and downtime
reconnaissancePhase 1 of an attack — passive and active gathering of target intel
reportingFinal pentest phase — documenting findings, evidence and severity for the client
RAMRandom Access Memory — fast volatile memory used while programs run
ransomwareMalware that encrypts victim files and demands payment for decryption
reconReconnaissance — gathering information about a target before attacking
red teamTeam that simulates real attackers in a controlled security exercise
registersTiny ultra-fast memory inside the CPU for currently active data
rootSuperuser account with unrestricted access to the entire system
rootkitMalware that hides deep in the OS to avoid detection
routerNetwork device that connects different networks and routes traffic
rwxRead, write, execute — the three Linux permission types
RBACRole-Based Access Control — permissions granted by role membership
race conditionBug where timing/ordering of concurrent operations breaks a check
RCERemote Code Execution — running attacker code on the target system
riskLikelihood times impact — what to prioritize fixing
S
SIEMSecurity Information and Event Management — centralizes logs, correlates, alerts
spywareMalware focused on covert surveillance — keys, screen, mic, browser
SigmaVendor-neutral SIEM detection-rule language (YAML)
smishingSMS-based phishing — fraudulent text messages with malicious links
scanningProbing live hosts for open ports, services and software versions
spear phishingHighly personalized phishing targeting a specific individual based on OSINT
sqlmapAutomated SQL injection scanner and exploitation tool
scanningDiscovering open ports, services and vulnerabilities on a target
sensitive data exposureLeaving confidential data unprotected or unencrypted
serverThe device or program that listens for and responds to requests
sessionServer-side mechanism to keep track of a logged-in user
shellProgram that interprets and executes terminal commands
SIEMSecurity Information and Event Management — collects and correlates logs
smishingPhishing attacks conducted over SMS text messages
SOCSecurity Operations Center — centralized defense and monitoring hub
social engineeringManipulating people psychologically to extract info or access
softwareInstructions that tell hardware what to do — code with no physical form
spear phishingHighly targeted phishing using personal info about the victim
spywareMalware that secretly monitors and collects user data
SQL injectionInjecting SQL code into input fields to manipulate a database
SSRFServer-Side Request Forgery — making the server send requests on your behalf
SSD / HDDPersistent storage devices — data survives shutdown
SSL / TLSCryptographic protocols that secure HTTPS connections
stackPart of RAM used for function calls and local variable storage
status codeServer numeric reply code — 200 OK, 404 Not Found, 500 Error
subnetA subdivision of a larger network into smaller logical segments
sudoLinux command to run a single command with root privileges
switchNetwork device that connects multiple devices within the same network
symmetric encryptionUses the same key to both encrypt and decrypt data
SameSiteCookie attribute controlling whether cookies travel on cross-site requests
session hijackingStealing or guessing a session token to impersonate a user
SQLiSQL Injection — making the database execute attacker-controlled queries
SSRFServer-Side Request Forgery — making the server fetch URLs the attacker chooses
stringSequence of characters — text data manipulated via slicing, regex, etc.
T
trojanMalware disguised as legitimate software; payload activates when user runs it
3-2-1 backup3 copies, 2 media types, 1 off-site — classic backup-resilience rule
tailgatingPhysically following someone into a restricted area without authorization
TCPTransmission Control Protocol — ensures reliable ordered packet delivery
terminalText interface to control the OS directly by typing commands
threat huntingProactively searching for hidden attackers already inside a network
threat modelMapping what could go wrong, why, and what to protect against
threadSmallest unit of execution within a process
trojanMalware disguised as legitimate software to trick users into running it
threatPotential cause of harm — actor, event or condition
threat modelStructured analysis of what could go wrong and where
TOCTOUTime-of-Check to Time-of-Use — race condition between check and action
U
UDPUser Datagram Protocol — faster than TCP but no delivery guarantee
URLUniform Resource Locator — full address of a web resource
userAccount with limited access on a system — opposite of admin or root
V
virusSelf-replicating malicious code that infects a host file and spreads when host runs
vishingVoice phishing — social engineering over phone calls
variableA named container that stores a value in a program
virusMalware that attaches itself to files and spreads when executed
vishingPhishing attacks conducted over voice calls
volatile memoryMemory that is completely erased when power is lost (RAM)
VPNVirtual Private Network — encrypted tunnel protecting traffic over internet
vulnerabilityA weakness in software, config or hardware that can be exploited
W
WAFWeb Application Firewall — Layer 7 filtering against SQLi, XSS, etc.
wormSelf-propagating malware that spreads across networks with no user interaction
WannaCry2017 ransomware worm using EternalBlue; hit ~200k machines in 150 countries
watering holeCompromising a legitimate site the target population visits
WiresharkNetwork protocol analyzer that captures and dissects packets in real time
WANWide Area Network — network spanning large geographic distances
wormSelf-spreading malware that needs no user interaction to propagate
X
XDRExtended Detection & Response — EDR plus email, identity, cloud signals
XSSCross-Site Scripting — injecting scripts into pages other users will see
Z
zero trustSecurity model — trust nothing by default, always verify
cherry root › study method
My Study System
How to use this grimoire to learn fast and retain everything.
Daily Session (1–2h)
30 min — read one term page
20 min — watch a short video on the topic
1h — practice on TryHackMe or lab
10 min — write what you learned, update glossary
Active Recall (after every term)
Close the grimoire
Write everything you remember on a blank page
Open and check — note only what you forgot
Review the gaps, not everything again
Flashcards — Anki
After each page: create 3–5 cards with keywords
Review Anki every day for 10 minutes
Front: keyword · Back: one-line definition
Feynman Test (weekly)
Pick one topic from the week
Explain it out loud as if teaching a friend
Where you get stuck = what you do not know yet
Learning Path
PC Basics→Internet→Networks→Linux→Programming→Security→Web Attacks→Tools→Pentest
cherry root › my favorites
My Favorites
All terms you marked as favorite — your personal collection of important concepts.
No favorites yet
Click the heart on any term page to add it here. Your favorites become your personal study list — the terms you want to revisit and master.

Master Mind Map
Complete overview of all the blocks and their connections.
146 termos 12 blocos clicáveis com glossário
mapa mental
See how the terms connect. Click any term to jump straight to its page. Terms with a dotted underline have a definition in the glossary.
Cherry Root — Master Mind Map
├─ BLOCK 0 · PC Basics
│ ├─ Hardware: Computer, CPU, RAM, Storage
│ ├─ Software: OS, Software, Process
│ ├─ Files: File, Directory, Path
│ └─ Base: Binary, Bit/Byte, User, Boot
├─ BLOCK 1 · Internet
│ ├─ Identity: IP, DNS, URL
│ ├─ Protocols: HTTP, HTTPS, TLS
│ ├─ Roles: Browser, Server, API
│ ├─ State: Cookies, Sessions, Auth
│ └─ Risks: Email, Phishing
├─ BLOCK 2 · Networks
│ ├─ Basics: Network, LAN/WAN, OSI
│ ├─ Transport: TCP, UDP
│ ├─ Address: MAC, Ports, Subnet
│ ├─ Devices: Router, Switch, Firewall
│ ├─ Traffic: Packet, Bandwidth
│ └─ Services: Proxy, VPN, NAT, DHCP
├─ BLOCK 3 · Linux
│ ├─ System: Linux, Distro, Shell
│ ├─ Files: FS Tree, Permissions
│ ├─ Identity: Users, sudo
│ └─ Access: SSH, Pkg Mgr, Cron, Logs
├─ BLOCK 4 · Programming
│ ├─ Logic: Logic, Condition, Loop
│ ├─ Data: Variable, Array, Dict, String
│ └─ Code: Function, File I/O, Python
├─ BLOCK 5 · Security Fundamentals
│ ├─ Pillars: CIA, Defense-in-Depth, Zero Trust
│ ├─ Risk: Threat, Vulnerability, Exploit, Risk, Attack Surface
│ └─ Controls: Encryption, Hashing, AuthN, AuthZ
├─ BLOCK 6 · Web Attacks
│ ├─ Injection: SQLi, XSS, Cmd Inj
│ ├─ Logic: CSRF, SSRF, Path Trav, IDOR
│ └─ Session: Open Redir, Clickjacking, Hijack, Auth Bypass, Race
├─ BLOCK 7 · Pentest Tools
│ ├─ Recon: nmap, Gobuster, Wireshark
│ ├─ Web: Burp, sqlmap, curl, DevTools
│ ├Exploit: Metasploit, netcat
│ └─ Crack: John, Hashcat, Hydra
├─ BLOCK 8 · Methodology
│ ├─ Discover: Recon, Scan, Enum
│ ├─ Breach: Exploit, PrivEsc, Persist, Lateral
│ └─ Close: Exfil, Cover Tracks, Report
├─ BLOCK 9 · Social Engineering
│ ├─ Digital: Phishing, Spear, Smishing
│ ├─ Voice/Physical: Tailgating, Vishing, Baiting
│ └─ Pretexting: Pretext, Watering Hole, Quid Pro Quo, Impersonation
├─ BLOCK 10 · Malware
│ ├─ Replicate: Virus, Worm, Botnet
│ ├─ Stealth: Trojan, Rootkit, Backdoor
│ └─ Payload: Ransomware, Spyware, Keylogger, Logic Bomb
└─ BLOCK 11 · Defense
├─ Perimeter: Firewall, WAF, IDS/IPS
├─ Detect: SIEM, EDR, Honeypot
└─ Resilience: Patch Mgmt, Backup, IR, Awareness
Categoria Termo (clique pra abrir) ✦ ✦ ✦ Glossário

PC Basics Mind Map
Como os fundamentos de computador se organizam.
14 termos clicáveis com glossário
mapa mental
See how the terms connect. Click any term to jump straight to its page. Terms with a dotted underline have a definition in the glossary.
PC Basics — Block 0
├─ Hardware
│ ├─ Computer— PC, server, dispositivo
│ ├─ CPU— cérebro do computador
│ ├─ RAM— memória volátil
│ └─ Storage— HDD, SSD — persistente
├─ Software
│ ├─ Operating System— Windows, Linux, macOS
│ ├─ Software— aplicações, programas
│ └─ Process— programa em execução
├─ Filesystem
│ ├─ File— unidade de dados
│ ├─ Directory— pasta — contém arquivos
│ └─ Path— caminho — absoluto vs relativo
└─ Conceitos Base
├─ Binary— base 2 — 0 e 1
├─ Bit & Byte— unidades de dados
├─ User & Permissions— controle de acesso
└─ Boot Process— BIOS → bootloader → OS

Internet Mind Map

14 termos clicáveis com glossário
mapa mental
See how the terms connect. Click any term to jump straight to its page. Terms with a dotted underline have a definition in the glossary.
Internet — Block 1
├─ Identidade na Rede
│ ├─ IP Address— endereço numérico
│ ├─ DNS— nome → IP
│ └─ URL— endereço completo de recurso
├─ Protocolos Web
│ ├─ HTTP— sem criptografia
│ ├─ HTTPS— criptografado com TLS
│ └─ TLS/SSL— a camada de criptografia
├─ Cliente & Servidor
│ ├─ Browser— cliente que renderiza páginas
│ ├─ Server— hospeda e serve recursos
│ └─ API— interface programática
├─ Sessão & Estado
│ ├─ Cookies— estado salvo no cliente
│ ├─ Sessions— estado salvo no servidor
│ └─ Authentication— quem você é
└─ Comunicação & Riscos
├─ Email— SMTP, IMAP, POP
└─ Phishing— engenharia social via web

mapa mental

├─ Fundamentos
│ ├─ Network— dispositivos conectados
│ ├─ LAN vs WAN— local vs amplo

├─ Protocolos Transporte
│ ├─ TCP— confiável, ordenado

├─ Endereçamento
│ ├─ MAC Address— L2 — placa de rede

│ └─ Subnet— segmentação de rede
├─ Equipamentos

│ ├─ Switch— dentro da rede — L2

│ └─ Bandwidth— capacidade do link

├─ VPN— túnel criptografado
├─ NAT— tradução de IPs

Programming Mind Map
Como os fundamentos de programação se conectam.
10 termos clicáveis com glossário
mapa mental
Visualize como os termos se conectam. Clique em qualquer termo pra ir direto pra página dele.
Programming — Block 4
├─ Lógica
│ ├─ Programming Logic— pensar passo a passo
│ ├─ Condition— if/else — decisões
│ └─ Loop— for/while — repetir
├─ Dados
│ ├─ Variable— guardar valor
│ ├─ Array / List— coleção ordenada
│ ├─ Dictionary— chave → valor
│ └─ String Manipulation— texto
└─ Código
├─ Function— bloco reutilizável
├─ File I/O— ler/escrever disco
└─ Python for Hackers— linguagem do hacker

│ ├─ SQL Injection— injetar SQL
│ ├─ XSS— JS no browser da vítima
│ └─ Command Injection— comando OS
├─ Falha de Lógica
│ ├─ CSRF— forçar ação logada
│ ├─ SSRF— servidor faz requests
│ ├─ Path Traversal— ../ fora do dir

├─ Open Redirect— redirect malicioso
├─ Clickjacking— iframe invisível
├─ Session Hijacking— roubar token

cherry root › block 0 › B·01
Hardware
Everything you can physically touch on a computer.Tudo o que você pode tocar fisicamente em um computador.
B·01pc basicsphysical
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:Start here — no prerequisites.
① Simple Definition
"Everything you can physically touch on a computer."
Hardware is all the physical components — input devices (keyboard, mouse), output devices (monitor, speakers), storage (HDD, SSD), processing (CPU) and memory (RAM). Software cannot exist without hardware to run on.
② Explanation
